# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Generative Design. List any two commercial software that support it.**

**Model Answer:**
Generative Design is an AI-driven design exploration methodology in which the engineer specifies design goals, constraints, loads, and manufacturing methods, and the software algorithmically generates a set of optimized design alternatives that satisfy the inputs.

*Commercial software examples:*
1. **Autodesk Fusion 360** (Generative Design module)
2. **ANSYS Discovery Live** (Topology optimization)
3. *Altair Inspire* (Polyhedral GD engine)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Digital Model and a Digital Twin.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Digital Model | Digital Twin |
|---|---|---|
| Data Flow | Static, no live data | Bi-directional, real-time |
| Purpose | Visualization | Monitoring, prediction, control |
| Updates | Manual, periodic | Automatic, sensor-driven |
| Intelligence | None | Embedded analytics and ML |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the **Generative Design process** in detail. List the inputs required and describe each step from goal definition to final design selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A structural bracket is to be topology-optimized inside a rectangular design domain of dimensions **100 mm × 60 mm × 20 mm**. The bracket is fixed on the left face and loaded with a vertical downward force of **F = 5000 N** on the right face. The material is **AlSi10Mg aluminum alloy** with $E_0 = 70$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.33$. The volume fraction constraint is $f = 0.4$ and the SIMP penalization power is $p = 3$. **(7 marks)**

(i) Formulate the compliance minimization problem mathematically.

(ii) Compute the **effective Young's modulus** of an element with $x_e = 0.7$ using the SIMP law (take $E_{\min} = 10^{-6} E_0$).

(iii) If the optimized topology yields a final mass of **192 grams**, compute the **mass reduction percentage** given the original solid volume mass is **324 grams**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Generative Design Process — Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Goal Definition:** The designer specifies what to optimize — e.g., minimize mass while maintaining a factor of safety of 2.0.
2. **Preserve and Obstacle Geometry:** Define regions that must remain (e.g., mounting holes) and regions that must be excluded (e.g., collision zones).
3. **Loading and Boundary Conditions:** Apply forces, pressures, moments, and constraints (fixed faces, symmetry planes).
4. **Material Selection:** Choose from the software's material library, including metals, polymers, and composites.
5. **Manufacturing Constraints:** Specify whether the design must be cast, machined, or 3D-printed. Each constraint dramatically changes the resulting geometry.
6. **Solver Execution:** The software runs thousands of FEA evaluations using algorithms such as **SIMP**, **genetic algorithms**, or **level-set methods**.
7. **Result Generation:** Typically 50–100 valid candidates are returned.
8. **Cloud-Based Exploration:** The designer can compare candidates side-by-side in a web browser.
9. **Selection and Refinement:** The best concept is selected and refined using traditional parametric modeling.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x} \quad & c(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{K} \mathbf{U} = \sum_{e=1}^{N} (x_e)^3 \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e \\
\text{subject to:} \quad & \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F} \\
& \frac{V(\mathbf{x})}{V_0} = 0.4 \\
& 10^{-6} \leq x_e \leq 1, \quad e = 1, \ldots, N
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effective Young's Modulus at $x_e = 0.7$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(x_e) &= E_{\min} + (x_e)^p (E_0 - E_{\min}) \\
&= 10^{-6} \cdot E_0 + (0.7)^3 (E_0 - 10^{-6} \cdot E_0) \\
&= (1 - (0.7)^3) \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot E_0 + (0.7)^3 \cdot E_0 \\
&\approx (0.343) \cdot E_0 \\
&= 0.343 \times 70 \, \text{GPa} \\
&= 24.01 \, \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mass Reduction Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Reduction} &= \frac{m_{\text{original}} - m_{\text{optimized}}}{m_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{324 - 192}{324} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{132}{324} \times 100 \\
&\approx 40.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with the **40% volume fraction** constraint, confirming the solver converged to the allowed mass target. ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Digital Twin**. With a neat block diagram, describe the **5-level maturity model** of a Digital Twin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A topology optimization is performed on a cantilever beam of dimensions **200 mm × 50 mm × 10 mm**, fixed at the left end and loaded with a downward tip load of **F = 1000 N** at the right end. Using the SIMP method with penalization power $p = 3$: **(7 marks)**

(i) State the **SIMP interpolation equation** and explain why $p > 1$ is required.

(ii) Compute the element stiffness ratio $E(x_e)/E_0$ for $x_e = 0.3$ and $x_e = 0.9$.

(iii) Comment on the **physical realizability** of intermediate densities.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Digital Twin Concept and 5-Level Maturity Model:**

A **Digital Twin** is a virtual replica of a physical asset, process, or system that synchronizes in real time using sensor data, enabling monitoring, prediction, and autonomous control.

*5-Level Maturity Model (block diagram):*

- **Level 1 — Digital Model:** A static 3D CAD model with no sensor connection. Used for visualization and documentation.
- **Level 2 — Digital Shadow:** One-way data flow from physical asset to digital model (e.g., temperature sensor updating the model). The digital side is read-only.
- **Level 3 — Digital Twin:** Bi-directional, real-time data exchange. Physical changes are mirrored digitally, and digital simulations can trigger physical actions.
- **Level 4 — Predictive Twin:** Incorporates machine learning to forecast future states, predict failures, and recommend maintenance schedules.
- **Level 5 — Autonomous Twin:** Closed-loop self-optimization without human intervention. The twin makes decisions and executes them on the physical asset.

**(b) Cantilever Beam Topology Optimization:**

**(i) SIMP Equation and Justification for $p > 1$:**

$$
E(x_e) = E_{\min} + x_e^p (E_0 - E_{\min})
$$

*Why $p > 1$?* Without penalization ($p = 1$), the optimizer tends to produce designs with **grey areas** — elements with intermediate densities like 0.4 or 0.6. These are mathematically valid but **physically unrealizable** because a real material is either present or absent — there is no such thing as "40% steel." Using $p = 3$ artificially reduces the stiffness contribution of intermediate densities, forcing the optimizer toward a **discrete black-and-white (0 or 1) solution** that can actually be manufactured.

**(ii) Stiffness Ratios:**

For $x_e = 0.3$, $p = 3$:

$$
\frac{E(0.3)}{E_0} \approx (0.3)^3 = 0.027 = 2.7\%
$$

For $x_e = 0.9$, $p = 3$:

$$
\frac{E(0.9)}{E_0} \approx (0.9)^3 = 0.729 = 72.9\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A 30%-dense element contributes only **2.7%** of full stiffness — almost negligible — while a 90%-dense element contributes **72.9%** of full stiffness. This non-linear asymmetry is precisely what drives the optimizer to commit elements to either "void" or "solid" status.

**(iii) Physical Realizability Comment:**

In additive manufacturing, intermediate densities *can* be approximated using **lattice infill patterns** (e.g., gyroid, Schwarz P structures), but in traditional casting or machining, they cannot. Therefore, a **post-processing threshold** is applied: elements with $x_e < 0.5$ are declared void, and $x_e \geq 0.5$ are declared solid. The **SIMP penalty power** is the mathematical mechanism that minimizes the number of such ambiguous grey elements, making the final design directly manufacturable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Generative Design with Topology Optimization:** They are *related but not identical*. Topology Optimization produces **one mathematically optimal solution**; Generative Design produces **many candidates** using AI/heuristics. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** In numerical problems (e.g., Question A part b-ii), writing $E = 24$ without the **GPa** unit is a guaranteed 0.5-mark cut.
>
> 3. **Missing the Volume Fraction Constraint:** In compliance minimization problems, students often write the objective function but **omit the $V(x)/V_0 = f$ constraint**. This is a complete 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating the SIMP Equation:** Writing $E = x \cdot E_0$ (linear) instead of $E = x^p (E_0 - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ shows a lack of understanding of the penalization concept.
>
> 5. **Digital Twin vs Digital Shadow confusion:** These are commonly swapped. A *Digital Shadow* has **one-way** data flow; only a *Digital Twin* has **bi-directional** flow.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning AI/ML:** When asked about "latest developments," failing to mention **Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, or Industry 4.0** is considered a dated answer. Always anchor the response to **at least one of these modern paradigms**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Generative Design** = AI-driven multi-candidate design exploration; designer sets *goals*, not geometry.
- [ ] **Digital Twin** = real-time, bi-directional virtual replica; distinguished from *Digital Shadow* (one-way) and *Digital Model* (static).
- [ ] **Topology Optimization** = mathematical method to find optimal material distribution within a given design space.
- [ ] **SIMP equation** = $E(x_e) = E_{\min} + x_e^p (E_0 - E_{\min})$ with typical $p = 3$.
- [ ] **Compliance objective** = $c = \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{K} \mathbf{U}$ — minimize to maximize stiffness.
- [ ] **Volume fraction constraint** = $V(\mathbf{x})/V_0 = f$, where $f \in (0, 1)$ controls the mass target.
- [ ] **Penalization power $p > 1$** drives the solution from grey (unrealizable) to black-and-white (manufacturable).
- [ ] **BIM** = multi-disciplinary digital representation for buildings; integrates architectural, structural, and MEP data.
- [ ] **Additive Manufacturing integration** allows topology-optimized geometries that were previously impossible to manufacture.
- [ ] **Industry 4.0** connects CAD → CAE → CAM → IoT in a closed digital loop.
- [ ] **Sensitivity analysis** gradient: $\partial c / \partial x_e = -p \, x_e^{p-1} \, \mathbf{u}_e^T \mathbf{k}_0 \mathbf{u}_e$ — high strain-energy elements gain material.
- [ ] **Real-world benchmarks** to remember: GM seatbelt bracket = 20% lighter + 20% stronger; Airbus partition = 45% mass reduction.
- [ ] **Cloud-Native CAD** (e.g., Onshape, Fusion 360 cloud) enables real-time multi-user collaboration with < 200 ms latency.
- [ ] For KTU answers, always **quantify** with specific numbers (mass reduction %, stiffness gain %, iteration counts).
- [ ] Always mention **AI/ML/Industry 4.0** at least once in any "latest developments" answer to demonstrate currency of knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLM and CAD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Computer Aided Design (CAD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAD (Computer Aided Design)** is the use of computer software and systems to assist engineers, designers, and architects in the **creation, modification, analysis, and optimization** of a design. It replaces manual drafting with an electronic/digital approach for the geometric definition of a part or assembly.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CAD is classified as a **digital engineering toolset** that converts conceptual design ideas into precise **2D drawings or 3D solid/surface/wireframe models** that can be viewed, rotated, dimensioned, and stored electronically.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Infinite Smart Drafting Table"

Imagine you have a **magical drafting table** that:
1. **Never tears** — you can press a button to "undo" any wrong line.
2. **Calculates lengths and areas for you** the moment you draw a line.
3. Lets you **rotate the paper in 3D** to view the object from any angle.
4. **Stores 10,000 sheets instantly** in a tiny pen drive.
5. **Clones any shape** with one click.

That magical drafting table is essentially what **CAD software** (like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, or Creo) does. It transforms a 2D paper-and-pencil activity into a 3D digital experience.

---

## 1.2 Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management)** is a strategic business approach and integrated software platform used to manage **all the information, processes, and resources associated with a product**, from its initial concept, through design and manufacturing, to its service and eventual disposal/recycling.

In KTU 2024 Scheme, PLM is defined as a **systematic, information-centric framework** that integrates people, data, processes, and business systems to provide a **single source of truth** for a product throughout its entire lifecycle.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Passport of a Product"

Imagine a **passport for a manufactured product** (say, a car):

| Life Event of the Car | What the Passport Records |
|---|---|
| Born (Concept Phase) | Designer sketches, market survey, idea notes |
| Childhood (Design Phase) | CAD drawings, simulation results, BOM |
| Teenage (Manufacturing) | CNC tool paths, vendor details, assembly instructions |
| Adulthood (Service) | Service manuals, warranty data, repair logs |
| Old Age (Disposal) | Recycling instructions, material breakdown |

That passport is **PLM**. It travels with the product from cradle to grave, recording **every piece of information** that ever existed about it. Without PLM, data is scattered across emails, hard drives, and forgotten folders. With PLM, **every stakeholder** (designer, manufacturer, supplier, service engineer) accesses the **same up-to-date information**.

---

## 1.3 Relationship Between CAD and PLM

> [!NOTE]
> **CAD is a SUBSET (component) of PLM**, not the other way around. PLM is the *broad umbrella*; CAD is one of the *tools* that lives under that umbrella.

$$
\text{PLM} \supset \text{CAD, CAM, CAE, PDM, ERP, SCM, ...}
$$

Where:
- **CAD** — Design and drafting
- **CAM** — Computer Aided Manufacturing
- **CAE** — Computer Aided Engineering (FEA, CFD)
- **PDM** — Product Data Management
- **ERP** — Enterprise Resource Planning
- **SCM** — Supply Chain Management

### 1.3.1 Standard Lifecycle Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typical product lifecycle stages and their **approximate duration share** in a discrete manufactured product:
> - **Concept Stage: 5% – 10%** of total lifecycle time
> - **Design Stage: 10% – 20%** of total lifecycle time
> - **Manufacturing Stage: 30% – 40%** of total lifecycle time
> - **Service/Use Stage: 40% – 60%** of total lifecycle time
> - **Disposal/Recycle Stage: 5% – 10%** of total lifecycle time

---

## 1.4 Why KTU Emphasizes This Topic

PLM and CAD form the **conceptual backbone** of modern digital manufacturing. As a mechanical/production engineer, you will be expected to:
1. Identify **which lifecycle stage** a given engineering activity belongs to.
2. Recommend **which CAD/PLM tool** is appropriate for a given problem.
3. Justify the **return on investment (ROI)** of integrating PLM in an industrial setup.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of CAD Technology

CAD systems are classified based on **how they represent geometry mathematically**. Understanding these is critical for KTU viva and written exams.

### 2.1.1 Wireframe Modeling
- Represents an object as a **collection of edges and vertices**.
- **No surface or volume information** — the object is "hollow" and "see-through".
- **Drawback:** Ambiguity — the same wireframe can represent multiple solids (e.g., a 3D square can be a cube or two pyramids back-to-back).
- **Use case:** Initial conceptual sketches, layout planning.

### 2.1.2 Surface Modeling
- Represents the object as a **collection of surfaces** (patches).
- Used when **surfaces are complex** (e.g., car body panels, aircraft fuselage).
- **No thickness or volume** — you can measure area but not mass.
- **Use case:** Aerospace, automotive styling, consumer product design.

### 2.1.3 Solid Modeling
- Represents the object as a **closed volume with mass, density, and inertia**.
- **Two main approaches:**
  - **CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry):** Builds solids using Boolean operations (Union, Subtract, Intersect) on primitives (cube, sphere, cylinder).
  - **B-Rep (Boundary Representation):** Defines a solid by its faces, edges, and vertices with topological relationships.
- **Use case:** Mechanical parts, assemblies, manufacturing-ready models.

### 2.1.4 Parametric/Feature-Based Modeling
- Modern standard. Uses **features** (hole, fillet, chamfer, boss, pocket) as building blocks.
- **Parameters** (dimensions, constraints) drive the geometry. Change a parameter → model updates automatically.
- **Use case:** All contemporary mechanical CAD (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, NX, Fusion 360).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between wireframe, surface, and solid modeling."* — This appears in almost every KTU exam paper for CAD/PLM-related courses.

---

## 2.2 The Four Phases of PLM (KTU Standard Model)

| Phase # | Phase Name | Key Activities | Key Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Concept / Inception** | Idea generation, feasibility study, market analysis, project planning | Mind-mapping tools, Project management software |
| **2** | **Design / Development** | Detailed CAD modeling, simulation, prototyping, validation | CAD, CAE (FEA, CFD), PDM |
| **3** | **Manufacturing / Production** | Process planning, CNC programming, quality control, assembly | CAM, MES, ERP |
| **4** | **Service / End-of-Life** | Distribution, maintenance, recycling, disposal, customer feedback | CRM, ERP, Service management software |

---

## 2.3 Architectural Layers of a PLM System

A modern PLM system is typically structured into **four functional layers**:

1. **Data Layer (Vault):** Centralized secure storage of all product data (CAD files, BOMs, documents, revisions). Uses a **PDM (Product Data Management)** database.
2. **Application Layer:** The actual software tools (CAD, CAM, CAE, simulation, visualization) that engineers use.
3. **Service Layer (Middleware):** Connects applications, manages workflows, handles version control, electronic signatures, and approvals.
4. **Presentation Layer (UI):** The user interface — web portals, dashboards, mobile apps — that allows stakeholders to interact with PLM.

---

## 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — CAD vs CAM vs CAE vs PLM vs PDM

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-frequency comparison table** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 questions.

| Feature | CAD | CAM | CAE | PDM | PLM |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Full Form** | Computer Aided Design | Computer Aided Manufacturing | Computer Aided Engineering | Product Data Management | Product Lifecycle Management |
| **Primary Purpose** | Create & modify design | Plan & control manufacturing | Analyze & simulate | Manage product data | Manage entire product lifecycle |
| **Scope** | Design stage | Manufacturing stage | Design + Manufacturing stages | All stages (data only) | All stages (process + data) |
| **Output** | 2D/3D models, drawings | G-code, NC programs, process plans | FEA results, CFD reports | Controlled documents, BOMs | Integrated business processes |
| **Key Output Format** | `.STEP`, `.IGES`, `.DWG`, `.SLDPRT` | `.NC`, `.GCODE`, `.APT` | `.FEA`, `.CSV`, plots | `.CSV`, XML, database entries | Dashboards, reports, KPIs |
| **Example Software** | AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA | Mastercam, Fusion 360 CAM, Delcam | ANSYS, Abaqus, HyperWorks | Windchill, Teamcenter, ENOVIA | Aras, Siemens Teamcenter, Dassault ENOVIA |
| **Subsystem of PLM?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (Sub-layer) | — (PLM is the umbrella) |

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX & NUMERICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

> [!NOTE]
> Since PLM/CAD is a **concept-dominant** topic, this section focuses on **analytical reasoning, numerical estimation, and decision-making logic** rather than pure mathematical derivations. KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests these "engineering judgment" problems.

---

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem 1: Identifying the Lifecycle Stage

### Problem Statement
A mechanical engineer at an automotive firm is using SolidWorks to design a new mounting bracket for an engine. The bracket must withstand vibration loads. The engineer runs a finite element analysis (FEA) in ANSYS to verify the bracket's fatigue life before sending it for prototype fabrication.

**Question:** Identify which PLM phase this activity belongs to, and name the **tools** and **deliverables** of this phase.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the activity:**
The engineer is:
- Creating a **3D CAD model** (design activity).
- Performing **FEA simulation** (analysis activity).
- Working on a **prototype-stage** part (not yet in production).

**Step 2 — Match to PLM phase:**
This belongs to **Phase 2: Design / Development**.

**Step 3 — Identify tools used:**
- **CAD tool:** SolidWorks (for modeling).
- **CAE tool:** ANSYS (for FEA).

**Step 4 — Identify deliverables:**
- 3D model file (`.SLDPRT`).
- Engineering drawing (`.SLDDRW`).
- FEA report (PDF with stress, strain, fatigue plots).
- Initial BOM (Bill of Materials).

**Step 5 — State the next phase:**
After design approval, the bracket moves to **Phase 3: Manufacturing**, where CAM is used to generate CNC tool paths for fabricating the physical prototype.

> **Final Answer:** The activity belongs to the **Design/Development** phase of PLM. The tools are **CAD (SolidWorks)** and **CAE (ANSYS)**, and the deliverables are the 3D model, drawing, FEA report, and initial BOM.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem 2: ROI Estimation of PLM Implementation

### Problem Statement
A medium-scale manufacturing company invests in a PLM system. The following data is provided:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of engineers | **50** |
| Average time wasted per engineer per day searching for files | **45 minutes** |
| Engineer cost per hour (fully loaded) | **₹ 800/hour** |
| Working days per year | **250 days** |
| PLM system annual cost (license + maintenance) | **₹ 50,00,000/year** |
| Expected efficiency improvement (search time reduction) | **80%** |

**Question:** Calculate the **annual savings**, the **net benefit**, and the **payback period** if the PLM implementation cost is a one-time **₹ 1,50,00,000**.

### Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate total wasted time per engineer per year:**

$$
T_{\text{wasted per engineer per year}} = 45 \, \text{min/day} \times 250 \, \text{days/year} = 11{,}250 \, \text{min/year}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_{\text{wasted per engineer per year}} = \frac{11{,}250}{60} = 187.5 \, \text{hours/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total wasted time for all engineers:**

$$
T_{\text{total wasted per year}} = 187.5 \, \text{hours/year} \times 50 = 9{,}375 \, \text{hours/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate annual cost of wasted time (without PLM):**

$$
C_{\text{wasted}} = 9{,}375 \, \text{hours/year} \times ₹800/\text{hour} = ₹75{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the time saved by PLM (80% improvement):**

$$
T_{\text{saved}} = 9{,}375 \times 0.80 = 7{,}500 \, \text{hours/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate annual monetary savings from recovered time:**

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 7{,}500 \, \text{hours/year} \times ₹800/\text{hour} = ₹60{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate net annual benefit (after PLM cost):**

$$
B_{\text{net}} = S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{PLM}} = ₹60{,}00{,}000 - ₹50{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate payback period:**

$$
P_{\text{payback}} = \frac{C_{\text{implementation}}}{B_{\text{net}}} = \frac{₹1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{₹10{,}00{,}000/\text{year}} = 15 \, \text{years}
$$

### Result Summary Table

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Total wasted hours/year (before PLM) | **9,375 hours** |
| Annual savings from PLM | **₹ 60,00,000** |
| Annual PLM cost | **₹ 50,00,000** |
| Net annual benefit | **₹ 10,00,000** |
| One-time implementation cost | **₹ 1,50,00,000** |
| Payback period | **15 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although the payback period seems long (15 years), the **intangible benefits** (faster time-to-market, reduced errors, better compliance, customer satisfaction) are not captured in this calculation. Real-world PLM ROI calculations include **engineering change order reduction, scrap reduction, and warranty cost savings** which typically bring the effective payback period down to **2–4 years**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem 3: Model Type Selection Logic

### Problem Statement
An engineer needs to design:
1. A simple **sheet metal L-bracket** with one bend.
2. A **car body outer panel** (curved freeform surface).
3. A **gear** that needs to have its mass and moment of inertia calculated.
4. A **floor plan of a house** with walls and doors.

**Question:** For each, recommend the most appropriate modeling approach (wireframe, surface, solid, or 2D drafting).

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

| Item | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (1) L-Bracket | **Solid Modeling (Parametric)** | Needs thickness, mass, BOM, and manufacturing data. Solid model is mandatory for sheet metal flat pattern generation. |
| (2) Car Body Panel | **Surface Modeling (NURBS)** | Freeform, aesthetic, curved surfaces. No need for internal volume — surface is enough. |
| (3) Gear | **Solid Modeling (Parametric with features)** | Mass, inertia, stress analysis, and CNC machining all require a solid model. |
| (4) House Floor Plan | **2D Drafting (CAD)** | Architectural plans are traditionally 2D top-view with dimensions. 3D is optional for visualization only. |

> **Final Answer:** L-Bracket → Solid; Car Panel → Surface; Gear → Solid; House Plan → 2D CAD.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem 4: CAD Model Data Flow Within PLM

### Problem Statement
A designer creates a bracket in SolidWorks. The model must be:
1. Checked by a senior engineer for design correctness.
2. Reviewed by a CAE analyst for stress analysis.
3. Approved by the quality department for material specification.
4. Sent to manufacturing for CNC machining.

**Question:** Describe the **workflow** and **data flow** as the model progresses through the PLM system.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Designer creates model in SolidWorks:**

$$
\text{Designer} \xrightarrow{\text{CAD Model}} \text{PLM Vault (Check-In)}
$$

The `.SLDPRT` file is uploaded to the central PLM vault with metadata: part name, material, designer ID, revision (Rev A).

**Step 2 — Senior Engineer reviews design:**

$$
\text{Vault} \xrightarrow{\text{Workflow Trigger}} \text{Design Reviewer}
$$

The PLM system sends a **notification** to the senior engineer. The engineer marks up the model, suggests changes, and either **approves** or **rejects**.

**Step 3 — CAE Analyst performs simulation:**

$$
\text{Approved Model} \xrightarrow{\text{CAD-to-CAE}} \text{ANSYS (FEA)}
$$

The analyst imports the **STEP/IGES** file into ANSYS, applies loads and boundary conditions, and uploads the FEA report back to PLM.

**Step 4 — Quality Department verifies material spec:**

$$
\text{FEA Approved} \xrightarrow{\text{Material Check}} \text{Quality Module}
$$

The BOM and material grade are verified against standards (e.g., IS 2062, AISI 304).

**Step 5 — Manufacturing receives approved data:**

$$
\text{Final Approval} \xrightarrow{\text{BOM + Drawing}} \text{CAM (CNC)}
$$

The PLM system releases the **manufacturing package**: 3D model, 2D drawing, BOM, and routing to the shop floor via **ERP integration**.

### Final Workflow Diagram (Textual)

$$
\text{CAD} \rightarrow \text{Vault} \rightarrow \text{Design Review} \rightarrow \text{CAE} \rightarrow \text{Quality Check} \rightarrow \text{Manufacturing Release}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The model follows a sequential workflow with **electronic approvals, version control, and traceability** at every stage — all orchestrated by the PLM system.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the various modeling techniques used in CAD. Compare wireframe, surface, and solid modeling with suitable diagrams.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
CAD modeling is the mathematical representation of a 3D object in computer memory. Based on how the geometry is stored and displayed, CAD modeling is classified into four main types: **wireframe, surface, solid, and parametric modeling**.

**1. Wireframe Modeling (2 Marks):**
- Represents the object as a **collection of edges (lines) and vertices (points)**.
- It is the **simplest and oldest** form of 3D modeling.
- **Advantages:** Fast, requires less computer memory, easy to create.
- **Disadvantages:** **Ambiguity problem** — the same wireframe can represent multiple solids. No surface, volume, or mass information.
- **Use Case:** Initial conceptual layouts, schematic diagrams.

**2. Surface Modeling (2 Marks):**
- Represents the object as a **collection of curved or flat surfaces** (patches).
- Uses **mathematical equations** like Bézier curves, B-splines, and NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines).
- **Advantages:** Can represent complex freeform shapes (car bodies, aircraft wings).
- **Disadvantages:** No thickness, no mass/volume calculation, computationally intensive.
- **Use Case:** Automotive styling, aerospace, industrial design.

**3. Solid Modeling (2 Marks):**
- Represents the object as a **closed 3D volume** with well-defined mass, density, and inertia.
- **Two main approaches:**
  - **CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry):** Builds solids using Boolean operations (Union, Subtract, Intersect) on primitives (cube, sphere, cylinder, cone).
  - **B-Rep (Boundary Representation):** Defines a solid by its faces, edges, and vertices with topological relationships.
- **Advantages:** No ambiguity, supports mass properties, interference detection, FEA-ready.
- **Disadvantages:** Computationally expensive, complex software.
- **Use Case:** Mechanical parts, assemblies, manufacturing.

> **Marking distribution:** 1 mark intro + 2 marks wireframe + 2 marks surface + 2 marks solid = 7 marks.

---

**(b) What is Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)? Explain the architecture and key components of a typical PLM system with a neat block diagram.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Definition of PLM (2 Marks):**
PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) is an integrated, information-centric business strategy that manages **all aspects of a product** — from initial concept, through design, manufacturing, service, and final disposal — using a centralized digital platform. It provides a **single source of truth** for all stakeholders.

**Architecture of a PLM System (3 Marks):**

A typical PLM system has **four layers**:

1. **Presentation Layer (User Interface):** Web portals, dashboards, mobile apps that allow designers, managers, suppliers, and customers to interact with the PLM system. Role-based access control ensures data security.

2. **Application Layer:** Hosts the actual engineering tools — **CAD (SolidWorks, CATIA), CAM (Mastercam), CAE (ANSYS), and visualization tools**. Users perform their daily engineering tasks through these applications.

3. **Service Layer (Middleware):** Manages **workflows, electronic approvals, version control, change management, security, and integration** between different applications. Handles BOM generation, ECO (Engineering Change Order) processing, and digital signatures.

4. **Data Layer (Vault):** The **centralized, secure database** that stores all product data — 3D models, 2D drawings, BOMs, documents, specifications, simulation results, and revision histories. Uses **PDM (Product Data Management)** as the backbone.

**Key Components (2 Marks):**
- **CAD/CAM/CAE tools** for engineering.
- **PDM** for data and document control.
- **Workflow Engine** for approvals and change management.
- **ERP Integration** for business and financial processes.
- **Visualization & Collaboration tools** (VR/AR, digital twin).
- **Analytics & Reporting** for KPI tracking.

> **Marking distribution:** 2 marks definition + 3 marks architecture + 2 marks components = 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the different phases of the Product Lifecycle in detail. How does PLM integrate with CAD, CAM, and CAE during the design-to-manufacturing transition?** (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Phases of the Product Lifecycle (4 Marks):**

**Phase 1: Concept / Inception**
- Market research, customer requirement analysis, feasibility study.
- Output: Project charter, initial concept sketches, business case.

**Phase 2: Design / Development**
- Detailed **CAD modeling** of parts and assemblies.
- **CAE simulation** (FEA for structural, CFD for fluid, thermal analysis).
- Prototype fabrication and testing.
- Output: 3D model, 2D drawing, BOM, FEA report, prototype.

**Phase 3: Manufacturing / Production**
- **CAM** is used to generate CNC tool paths and NC programs.
- Process planning, tooling design, fixture design.
- Quality control using CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine).
- Output: Finished product, inspection reports.

**Phase 4: Service / Use**
- Product distribution, installation, commissioning.
- Maintenance, repairs, warranty management.
- Customer feedback collection.

**Phase 5: Disposal / Recycling**
- End-of-life (EOL) management.
- Material recovery, recycling, safe disposal per environmental norms.

**Integration of PLM with CAD, CAM, CAE (3 Marks):**

PLM acts as the **central nervous system** that connects all these tools:

1. **CAD → PLM:** The 3D model is uploaded to the PLM vault with metadata (part name, material, revision). Version control ensures the latest model is always used.

2. **CAE → PLM:** The FEA/CFD simulation results are linked back to the CAD model. A stress contour plot is automatically associated with the design revision.

3. **CAM → PLM:** Once the design is approved, CAM extracts the **STEP/IGES** file from PLM and generates G-code. The tool paths are stored alongside the design in the PLM vault.

4. **BOM and Process Plan:** PLM automatically generates the **BOM** (Bill of Materials) from the 3D model and links it to the **routing** (sequence of operations) defined in CAM.

> **Marking distribution:** 4 marks for phases + 3 marks for integration = 7 marks.

---

**(b) Discuss the benefits and challenges of implementing PLM in a manufacturing industry. Also, explain with a neat sketch the data flow from a CAD model to the shop floor through PLM.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Benefits of PLM Implementation (3 Marks):**

1. **Reduced Time-to-Market:** Concurrent engineering and digital workflows cut product development time by 30%–50%.
2. **Improved Product Quality:** Centralized data eliminates version conflicts; simulation reduces physical prototypes.
3. **Cost Reduction:** Design reuse, scrap reduction, and faster change orders save 10%–20% of total cost.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Built-in audit trails support ISO 9001, AS9100, FDA, and CE marking.
5. **Better Collaboration:** Suppliers, designers, and manufacturers work on a single, real-time data source.
6. **Knowledge Management:** Tribal knowledge is captured digitally, reducing dependency on individuals.

**Challenges of PLM Implementation (2 Marks):**

1. **High Initial Cost:** Software licenses, training, and IT infrastructure require significant capital investment.
2. **Resistance to Change:** Employees may resist moving from familiar tools to new PLM workflows.
3. **Data Migration:** Legacy data from old systems is often messy and difficult to migrate.
4. **Customization Complexity:** Every company has unique processes; PLM must be heavily configured.
5. **Cybersecurity:** Centralized data is a single point of failure; requires robust security protocols.

**Data Flow from CAD to Shop Floor (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Designer} \xrightarrow{\text{CAD Model}} \text{PLM Vault} \xrightarrow{\text{Approval}} \text{CAM (Tool Path)} \xrightarrow{\text{G-Code}} \text{CNC Machine} \xrightarrow{\text{Finished Part}} \text{Quality Check}
$$

> **Marking distribution:** 3 marks benefits + 2 marks challenges + 2 marks data flow = 7 marks.
