## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define 2-D rotation. Write the homogeneous transformation matrix for rotating a point counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$ about the origin.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A 2-D rotation is a rigid-body transformation that repositions a point $(x, y)$ to a new location $(x', y')$ by revolving it about a pivot point (default: origin) through an angle $\theta$, preserving distance, area, and shape.

**Matrix:**
$$
R(\theta) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta & -\sin \theta & 0 \\
\sin \theta & \phantom{-}\cos \theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$
**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Equations (1M) + Matrix (1M).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is the difference between clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation? How is the sign convention represented in the rotation matrix?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Counter-clockwise (CCW):** Positive angle ($\theta > 0$). Standard mathematical convention. Matrix uses $-\sin \theta$ in the $(1,2)$ position and $+\sin \theta$ in the $(2,1)$ position.
- **Clockwise (CW):** Negative angle ($\theta < 0$). Achieved by replacing $\theta$ with $-\theta$, which flips the signs of the sine terms.

**Mark Distribution:** Sign convention explanation (2M) + Matrix difference (1M).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice]**
>
> *(a)* Derive the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix for rotating a point counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$ about an arbitrary pivot point $(x_r, y_r)$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A square has vertices $P_1(1, 1)$, $P_2(4, 1)$, $P_3(4, 4)$, $P_4(1, 4)$. Rotate the square by $60^\circ$ counter-clockwise about the pivot point $(2, 2)$. Compute the new coordinates of all four vertices using the composite matrix. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** State the three required sub-transformations and their matrices (2 Marks).

Translation to origin:
$$
T(-x_r, -y_r) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -x_r \\
0 & 1 & -y_r \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Rotation about origin:
$$
R(\theta) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta & -\sin \theta & 0 \\
\sin \theta & \cos \theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Translation back:
$$
T(x_r, y_r) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & x_r \\
0 & 1 & y_r \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the product $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta)$ (2 Marks):
$$
= 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta & -\sin \theta & x_r \\
\sin \theta & \cos \theta & y_r \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $T(-x_r, -y_r)$ on the right to obtain the final composite matrix (3 Marks):
$$
M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta & -\sin \theta & x_r(1-\cos\theta) + y_r \sin\theta \\
\sin \theta & \cos \theta & x_r \sin\theta + y_r(1-\cos\theta) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\theta = 60^\circ$, $(x_r, y_r) = (2, 2)$.
**Values:** $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, $\sin 60^\circ = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$.

**Step 1:** Compute the translation column (1 Mark):
$$T_x = 2(1 - 0.5) + 2(0.866) = 1 + 1.732 = 2.732$$
$$T_y = 2(0.866) + 2(1 - 0.5) = 1.732 + 1 = 2.732$$

**Step 2:** Build the composite matrix $M$ (1 Mark):
$$
M = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0.5 & -0.866 & 2.732 \\
0.866 & 0.5 & 2.732 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply $M$ to each vertex (5 Marks — 1.25 per vertex).

For $P_1(1, 1)$:
$$x'_1 = 0.5(1) - 0.866(1) + 2.732 = 0.5 - 0.866 + 2.732 = 2.366$$
$$y'_1 = 0.866(1) + 0.5(1) + 2.732 = 0.866 + 0.5 + 2.732 = 4.098$$
$$P_1' = (2.366,\ 4.098)$$

For $P_2(4, 1)$:
$$x'_2 = 0.5(4) - 0.866(1) + 2.732 = 2 - 0.866 + 2.732 = 3.866$$
$$y'_2 = 0.866(4) + 0.5(1) + 2.732 = 3.464 + 0.5 + 2.732 = 6.696$$
$$P_2' = (3.866,\ 6.696)$$

For $P_3(4, 4)$:
$$x'_3 = 0.5(4) - 0.866(4) + 2.732 = 2 - 3.464 + 2.732 = 1.268$$
$$y'_3 = 0.866(4) + 0.5(4) + 2.732 = 3.464 + 2 + 2.732 = 8.196$$
$$P_3' = (1.268,\ 8.196)$$

For $P_4(1, 4)$:
$$x'_4 = 0.5(1) - 0.866(4) + 2.732 = 0.5 - 3.464 + 2.732 = -0.232$$
$$y'_4 = 0.866(1) + 0.5(4) + 2.732 = 0.866 + 2 + 2.732 = 5.598$$
$$P_4' = (-0.232,\ 5.598)$$

**Final Answer:** $P_1'(2.366, 4.098)$, $P_2'(3.866, 6.696)$, $P_3'(1.268, 8.196)$, $P_4'(-0.232, 5.598)$.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice]**
>
> *(a)* Explain the concept of composite transformations. Derive the composite matrix for rotation about an arbitrary point using the right-multiplication order convention. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A line segment has endpoints $A(3, 2)$ and $B(7, 5)$. Rotate the segment by $45^\circ$ counter-clockwise about the point $(4, 3)$. Find the new endpoints using the 3-step transformation method (translate-rotate-translate). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Composite Transformations (2 Marks):**
A composite transformation is a sequence of two or more geometric transformations combined into a single matrix. The order of multiplication is critical: when applying a sequence of transformations to a column vector, matrices multiply **right-to-left** (i.e., the rightmost matrix is applied first to the point).

**Derivation (5 Marks):**
Same derivation as Question A(a) — reference the 3-step composite $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ and the final expanded matrix.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\theta = 45^\circ$, $(x_r, y_r) = (4, 3)$.
**Values:** $\cos 45^\circ = \sin 45^\circ = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$.

**Step 1: Translate by $(-4, -3)$ (1 Mark):**
- $A_1 = (3 - 4, 2 - 3) = (-1, -1)$
- $B_1 = (7 - 4, 5 - 3) = (3, 2)$

**Step 2: Rotate $A_1$ and $B_1$ by $45^\circ$ CCW about origin (3 Marks):**

For $A_1(-1, -1)$:
$$x'_{A_1} = (-1)(0.7071) - (-1)(0.7071) = -0.7071 + 0.7071 = 0$$
$$y'_{A_1} = (-1)(0.7071) + (-1)(0.7071) = -0.7071 - 0.7071 = -1.4142$$
$$A_2 = (0, -1.4142)$$

For $B_1(3, 2)$:
$$x'_{B_1} = 3(0.7071) - 2(0.7071) = 2.1213 - 1.4142 = 0.7071$$
$$y'_{B_1} = 3(0.7071) + 2(0.7071) = 2.1213 + 1.4142 = 3.5355$$
$$B_2 = (0.7071, 3.5355)$$

**Step 3: Translate back by $(+4, +3)$ (1 Mark):**

For $A'$:
$$A' = (0 + 4, -1.4142 + 3) = (4, 1.5858)$$

For $B'$:
$$B' = (0.7071 + 4, 3.5355 + 3) = (4.7071, 6.5355)$$

**Step 4: Verification using the composite matrix (2 Marks):**
Build the composite matrix $M$ with $T_x, T_y$ and verify by direct multiplication to confirm the same results. This ensures full marks.

**Final Answer:** $A'(4, 1.5858)$, $B'(4.7071, 6.5355)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in the Matrix:** The most common mistake is placing $+\sin\theta$ in the $(1,2)$ position and $-\sin\theta$ in the $(2,1)$ position. This reverses the direction. **Always remember: CCW rotation has $-\sin\theta$ in the top-right and $+\sin\theta$ in the bottom-left.** This costs students 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Multiplication Order:** In $M = T \cdot R \cdot T$, students sometimes write $R \cdot T \cdot T$, which is mathematically incorrect. The rightmost matrix is applied first to the point.
> 3. **Forgetting the Homogeneous Row:** The bottom row $(0, 0, 1)$ must be included. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Radian vs Degree Errors:** KTU accepts degrees, but ensure your calculator is in the correct mode. A single $\sin 60^\circ$ vs $\sin(\pi/3)$ mix-up causes cascading errors.
> 5. **Pivot Mistranslation:** When computing $T_x$ and $T_y$, students often use the sign of the pivot incorrectly. **Always use the formula $x_r(1-\cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta$ without modification.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 2-D Rotation (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A rigid-body isometry that revolves points about a pivot by an angle $\theta$.
- **Sign Convention:** CCW is positive (standard math); CW is negative.
- **Basic Rotation Matrix (CCW, about origin):**
$$\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Rotation Equations:**
$$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$
$$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$
- **Rotation About Arbitrary Pivot $(x_r, y_r)$:** Composite matrix $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$.
- **Inverse Property:** $R(\theta)^{-1} = R(-\theta) = R(\theta)^T$ (orthogonal matrix).
- **Determinant:** $\det(R(\theta)) = +1$ (proper rotation, no reflection).
- **Properties Preserved:** Length (distance), angles, area, orientation.
- **Properties NOT Preserved:** Coordinates of individual points (unless at the pivot).
- **Composite Order Rule:** Matrices multiply right-to-left when applied to column vectors.
- **Memory Aid:** "**T R T**" — Translate to origin, Rotate, Translate back. Always in this order.
- **Frequent Mistake:** Using the CW matrix when CCW is asked, or vice versa.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Gear assembly, robotic arm kinematics, CNC tool paths, finite element mesh alignment, architectural plan rotation.
- **Exam Tip:** Always show all three sub-matrices explicitly before multiplying, even if the question only asks for the final composite matrix. This earns partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Computer Aided Design and Analysis**, *Reflection* is defined as a **rigid-body geometric transformation** that maps every point $P(x, y)$ of a 2D object to its corresponding **mirror image** $P'(x', y')$ across a specified line of reflection (also called the *axis of reflection* or *mirror line*).

Mathematically, reflection is a special case of a **negative scaling** operation in which the **shape, size, and angles** of the object are preserved, but the **orientation is reversed** (handedness flips — a *right-handed* coordinate becomes *left-handed*).

In the **homogeneous coordinate system**, a 2D reflection is represented by a $3 \times 3$ transformation matrix of the form:

$$
T_{ref} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{11} & r_{12} & t_x \\
r_{21} & r_{22} & t_y \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $r_{ij}$ represent the *reflection* coefficients that encode the mirror geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Reflection belongs to the family of **2D geometric transformations** along with Translation, Scaling, Rotation, and Shearing. Unlike rotation or scaling, reflection produces a **determinant of $-1$** (improper transformation), which is a board-favourite conceptual question.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lake Analogy"

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a perfectly calm lake, holding a cardboard triangle. When you hold it flat over the water:

- **You** = the original object
- **The water surface** = the mirror line (axis of reflection)
- **The reflection of the triangle in the water** = the transformed object

> If the lake surface is *horizontal* (X-axis), the triangle gets flipped **upside-down** (Y-coordinates invert).
> If the lake surface is *vertical* (Y-axis), the triangle becomes a **mirror image across a wall** (X-coordinates invert).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Reflection does *not* deform the object. The triangle in the water is the **exact same size and shape** as the one in your hand — only its **chirality (handedness)** is reversed. This is why reflection is often called *"mirror transformation"* in CAD textbooks.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Constants

For the standard mirror lines used in KTU numerical problems, the following constants are implicit:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Axis of reflection | $X$-axis ($y = 0$) | Horizontal mirror |
| Axis of reflection | $Y$-axis ($x = 0$) | Vertical mirror |
| Axis of reflection | Origin | Both axes combined |
| Axis of reflection | $y = x$ | Diagonal (45°) mirror |
| Axis of reflection | $y = -x$ | Anti-diagonal (135°) mirror |
| Determinant $\lvert T_{ref} \rvert$ | $-1$ | Signifies improper (orientation-reversing) transformation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Directive:** KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recognize that reflection is one of the fundamental **2D transformations** in the *graphics pipeline* of any CAD software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, NX), where it is used for symmetric part creation and pattern duplication.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Reflection (KTU Taxonomy)

Reflections in 2D CAD are classified based on the **mirror line** chosen:

1. **Reflection about the X-axis**
2. **Reflection about the Y-axis**
3. **Reflection about the origin** (composite of X- and Y-axis reflections)
4. **Reflection about the line $y = x$**
5. **Reflection about the line $y = -x$**
6. **Reflection about an arbitrary line $y = mx + c$** (general case — most frequently asked in KTU derivations)

The first five are *special cases* (with simple integer matrices), while the sixth is the *general case* that requires a **multi-step composite transformation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Reflection Works the Way It Does

Reflection in 2D is fundamentally a **change-of-basis** operation. The mirror line acts as a *fixed locus* of points (the **eigenvector with eigenvalue $+1$**), and the perpendicular direction to the mirror line gets **negated** (the **eigenvector with eigenvalue $-1$**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A point lying **on the mirror line remains invariant** under reflection. A point **perpendicularly far** from the mirror line has its signed distance reversed. This is the *Why* behind every reflection matrix.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Standard Reflection

### (A) Reflection about the X-axis
- The X-axis is the mirror line.
- A point $(x, y)$ reflects to $(x, -y)$.
- The $x$-coordinate is unchanged; the $y$-coordinate is negated.
- **Why?** The X-axis is a *horizontal line of symmetry*; the mirror is below the original for points above and vice-versa.

$$
P' = 
\begin{bmatrix}
x' \\
y' \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### (B) Reflection about the Y-axis
- The Y-axis is the mirror line.
- A point $(x, y)$ reflects to $(-x, y)$.
- The $y$-coordinate is unchanged; the $x$-coordinate is negated.

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x' \\
y' \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### (C) Reflection about the Origin
- A point $(x, y)$ reflects to $(-x, -y)$.
- Equivalent to *reflection about X-axis* followed by *reflection about Y-axis* (or vice versa — matrix multiplication commutes here).

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x' \\
y' \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### (D) Reflection about the line $y = x$
- The line $y = x$ (45° diagonal through origin) is the mirror.
- A point $(x, y)$ reflects to $(y, x)$ — **the coordinates swap**.

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x' \\
y' \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### (E) Reflection about the line $y = -x$
- The line $y = -x$ (135° diagonal through origin) is the mirror.
- A point $(x, y)$ reflects to $(-y, -x)$.

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
x' \\
y' \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & -1 & 0 \\
-1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
x \\
y \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### (F) Reflection about an Arbitrary Line $y = mx + c$ (General Case)

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 2. The strategy is to **reduce the arbitrary line to one of the standard axes** using a sequence of transformations, perform the reflection, and then **undo** the sequence in reverse order.

The composite transformation matrix is:

$$
M_{ref} = T(c_x, c_y) \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-c_x, -c_y)
$$

where:
- $(c_x, c_y)$ is a point on the line (typically the y-intercept $(0, c)$),
- $\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)$ is the angle the line makes with the X-axis,
- $Ref_X$ is the reflection about X-axis matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Multiplication is RIGHT to LEFT (Post-multiplication):** The rightmost matrix operates on the point *first*, the leftmost operates *last*. This is a notorious KTU error — students frequently multiply in the wrong order.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule:** Vertical bars `|` are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table formatting.

| **S.No.** | **Reflection Type** | **Mirror Line** | **Transformation Matrix $M$** | **Determinant $\lvert M \vert$** | **Point Mapping** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | About X-axis | $y = 0$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$ |
| 2 | About Y-axis | $x = 0$ | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$ |
| 3 | About Origin | Both axes | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $1$ | $(x, y) \to (-x, -y)$ |
| 4 | About $y = x$ | $y = x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $(x, y) \to (y, x)$ |
| 5 | About $y = -x$ | $y = -x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $(x, y) \to (-y, -x)$ |
| 6 | About $y = mx + c$ | Arbitrary | Composite (see derivation) | $1$ | Complicated — derived step-by-step |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **CAD Domain** | **Application of Reflection** |
|:---|:---|
| **Sheet Metal Design** | Creating mirror-image bent components (e.g., L-brackets, channels) |
| **Symmetric Assembly** | Generating the right-hand side of a symmetric assembly from the left |
| **Tool Path Generation (CAM)** | Mirror tool paths for symmetric machining operations |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Reconstructing the missing half of a damaged symmetric part |
| **FEA Pre-Processing** | Applying symmetric boundary conditions by reflecting mesh |
| **Architecture & BIM** | Floor plan symmetry, facade mirroring |
| **Automotive Styling** | Generating left/right body panels from a master surface |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern CAD systems (CATIA, NX, SolidWorks), reflection is implemented as a *pattern feature* — the user defines a *mirror plane* (in 3D) or *mirror line* (in 2D sketch), and the software automatically generates the reflected geometry with full parametric associativity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Reflection about an Arbitrary Line $y = mx + c$

This is the **classic KTU Module 2 derivation** (worth 7 marks in most board papers).

### Step 0 — Goal Statement

We need a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix $M$ such that any point $P(x, y, 1)$ is reflected to $P'(x', y', 1)$ about the line $L: y = mx + c$.

### Step 1 — Identify a Point on the Line

A convenient point on the line is the **Y-intercept**: $P_0 = (0, c)$.

### Step 2 — Strategy: Five-Stage Composite Transformation

The reflection about an arbitrary line is composed of **five operations in sequence**:

1. **Translate** the line so that $P_0$ moves to the origin.
2. **Rotate** the line so that it aligns with the X-axis.
3. **Reflect** about the X-axis.
4. **Rotate back** (inverse of Step 2).
5. **Translate back** (inverse of Step 1).

The angle the line makes with the X-axis is $\theta$, where:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)
$$

> **Why this works:** Once the mirror line coincides with the X-axis, we already know the reflection matrix from Section 2.2(A). The surrounding translate–rotate–rotate–translate operations merely *carry* the original coordinate system along so that the simple reflection works in the *transformed* frame.

### Step 3 — Write the Five Matrices

**Step 3.1 — Translation to origin (move $P_0 = (0, c)$ to $(0, 0)$):**

$$
T(-0, -c) =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & -c \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.2 — Rotation by $-\theta$ (to align the line with X-axis):**

$$
R(-\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
-\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.3 — Reflection about X-axis:**

$$
Ref_X =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.4 — Rotation by $+\theta$ (rotate back to original orientation):**

$$
R(\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.5 — Translation back (move origin to $(0, c)$):**

$$
T(0, c) =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & c \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compose the Final Matrix

The combined transformation matrix is:

$$
M_{ref} = T(0, c) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot T(0, -c)
$$

> **Note on order:** The point $P$ is multiplied **right-to-left**. So the *first* operation to act on $P$ is the *rightmost* matrix ($T(0, -c)$), and the *last* operation to act is the *leftmost* ($T(0, c)$).

### Step 5 — Multiply $Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta)$

$$
Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
-\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> *Row-by-row justification:*
> - Row 1: $(1)(\cos\theta) + (0)(-\sin\theta) + (0)(0) = \cos\theta$; $(1)(\sin\theta) + (0)(\cos\theta) + (0)(0) = \sin\theta$.
> - Row 2: $(0)(\cos\theta) + (-1)(-\sin\theta) + (0)(0) = \sin\theta$; $(0)(\sin\theta) + (-1)(\cos\theta) + (0)(0) = -\cos\theta$.
> - Row 3 remains $(0, 0, 1)$ unchanged.

### Step 6 — Multiply the Result with $R(\theta)$ (in the middle)

$$
\left[ Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) \right] \cdot R(\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Performing element-by-element multiplication:

- $M_{11} = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos(2\theta)$
- $M_{12} = -\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta = 0$
- $M_{21} = \sin\theta\cos\theta - \cos\theta\sin\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$ — wait, re-check:

Let us recompute carefully:
- $M_{11} = (\cos\theta)(\cos\theta) + (\sin\theta)(\sin\theta) + (0)(0) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$
- $M_{12} = (\cos\theta)(-\sin\theta) + (\sin\theta)(\cos\theta) + (0)(0) = -\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta = 0$
- $M_{13} = 0$
- $M_{21} = (\sin\theta)(\cos\theta) + (-\cos\theta)(\sin\theta) + (0)(0) = \sin\theta\cos\theta - \cos\theta\sin\theta = 0$
- $M_{22} = (\sin\theta)(-\sin\theta) + (-\cos\theta)(\cos\theta) + (0)(0) = -\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -1$
- $M_{23} = 0$
- $M_{31} = 0$, $M_{32} = 0$, $M_{33} = 1$

So the **central composite** simplifies to:

$$
Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot R(\theta) = Ref_X =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> This is *expected* — the two rotations cancel out. The lesson: for the pure reflection about an arbitrary line *through the origin*, only translation, one rotation, reflection, and the inverse rotation survive.

### Step 7 — General Final Matrix (For Line Through Origin, $c = 0$)

When $c = 0$, the line passes through the origin and $T(0, -c) = T(0, c) = I$ (identity). The composite reduces to:

$$
M_{ref, \, c=0} = R(\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta)
$$

Substituting:

$$
M_{ref,\, c=0} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
-\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Performing the rightmost multiplication first (we already computed $Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta)$ in Step 5):

$$
Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Now post-multiply by $R(\theta)$:

$$
M_{ref,\, c=0} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Element-wise:
- $M_{11} = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos(2\theta)$
- $M_{12} = \cos\theta\sin\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta = 0$

Wait, let us recompute. The element $M_{12}$:

$M_{12} = (\cos\theta)(\sin\theta) + (-\sin\theta)(-\cos\theta) + 0 = \cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$

$M_{21} = (\sin\theta)(\cos\theta) + (\cos\theta)(\sin\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$

$M_{22} = (\sin\theta)(\sin\theta) + (\cos\theta)(-\cos\theta) = \sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -\cos(2\theta)$

So:

$$
M_{ref,\, c=0} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(2\theta) & \sin(2\theta) & 0 \\
\sin(2\theta) & -\cos(2\theta) & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Special-case verification:** For the line $y = x$, we have $\theta = 45°$, so $2\theta = 90°$, giving $\cos(2\theta) = 0$ and $\sin(2\theta) = 1$. The matrix becomes $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, which **exactly matches** the formula in Section 2.2(D). ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern

> **Problem (KTU Pattern, 7 marks):** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 3)$, $B(5, 4)$, $C(4, 6)$. Reflect the triangle about the line $y = x$. Obtain the new coordinates of the reflected triangle and show the orientation change.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Choose the reflection matrix.**

The line $y = x$ corresponds to the matrix:

$$
M = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert each vertex to homogeneous coordinates.**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the transformation to vertex A:**

$$
A' = M \cdot A = 
\begin{bmatrix}
(0)(2) + (1)(3) + (0)(1) \\
(1)(2) + (0)(3) + (0)(1) \\
(0)(2) + (0)(3) + (1)(1)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
3 \\
2 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

So $A' = (3, 2)$.

**Step 4 — Apply the transformation to vertex B:**

$$
B' = M \cdot B = 
\begin{bmatrix}
(0)(5) + (1)(4) + (0)(1) \\
(1)(5) + (0)(4) + (0)(1) \\
(0)(5) + (0)(4) + (1)(1)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
4 \\
5 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

So $B' = (4, 5)$.

**Step 5 — Apply the transformation to vertex C:**

$$
C' = M \cdot C = 
\begin{bmatrix}
(0)(4) + (1)(6) + (0)(1) \\
(1)(4) + (0)(6) + (0)(1) \\
(0)(4) + (0)(6) + (1)(1)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
6 \\
4 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

So $C' = (6, 4)$.

**Step 6 — Verify the handedness flip.**

Original triangle $ABC$ is in the *first quadrant* spanning points with $x \le 5$, $y \le 6$.
Reflected triangle $A'B'C'$ has $A'(3, 2)$, $B'(4, 5)$, $C'(6, 4)$ — also in the first quadrant, but the orientation (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise) has reversed. This confirms the determinant $-1$ property.

**Final Answer:** $A'(3, 2)$, $B'(4, 5)$, $C'(6, 4)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reflection Geometry Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following schematic describes the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a reflection operation within a CAD graphics pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Original 2D Object P with vertices V1 V2 V3 ...] --> node2[Identify Mirror Line L]
    node2 --> node3{Standard Line?}
    node3 -- Yes --> node4[Lookup Predefined Matrix from Formula Table]
    node3 -- No --> node5[Compute Angle theta = atan m]
    node5 --> node6[Form Composite Matrix M = T R theta Ref_X R neg theta T inv]
    node4 --> node7[Apply Homogeneous Transform: P prime = M times P]
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[Render Reflected Object P prime]
    node8 --> node9[Update CAD Model Tree with Parametric Link]
    node9 --> node10[Display in Viewport with Mirror Symmetry Constraint]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A 2D object enters the pipeline → the mirror line is identified → a *decision branch* selects either a predefined matrix (for standard axes) or computes a composite matrix (for arbitrary lines) → the transformation is applied via homogeneous coordinates → the reflected object is rendered and the parametric model tree is updated.

---

## 4.2 Mapped Reflections Across Mirror Lines (Block Matrix View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Object P] --> nodeB[Reflection Module]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Choose Mirror Line}
    nodeC -- About X axis --> nodeD[Matrix MX]
    nodeC -- About Y axis --> nodeE[Matrix MY]
    nodeC -- About Origin --> nodeF[Matrix MO]
    nodeC -- About y equals x --> nodeG[Matrix MD1]
    nodeC -- About y equals neg x --> nodeH[Matrix MD2]
    nodeC -- About y equals mx plus c --> nodeI[Composite Matrix MC]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Output P prime]
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

---

## 4.3 Eigen-Decomposition View of Reflection

The reflection matrix can be understood through its **eigenvectors** (directions preserved or flipped):

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeK[Reflection Operator R] --> nodeL{Is point on mirror line?}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[Eigenvalue plus 1, Fixed Point]
    nodeL -- No --> nodeN[Eigenvalue minus 1, Direction Flipped]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Output is same as input]
    nodeN --> nodeP[Output is mirrored across the line]
```

> **Key takeaway for KTU viva:** A mirror line is the **+1 eigenspace** of the reflection matrix, and the perpendicular direction is the **-1 eigenspace**. This explains *why* the determinant is always $\pm 1$ (product of eigenvalues) and equals $-1$ when the mirror line is a single axis (improper transformation).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q:** Define reflection in the context of 2D computer graphics transformations. Write the transformation matrix for reflection of a 2D point about the X-axis and Y-axis.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Reflection is a geometric transformation that produces the mirror image of an object across a specified line (called the *mirror line* or *axis of reflection*). It preserves the shape, size, and angles of the object but reverses its orientation (handedness).
>
> **Reflection about X-axis** (mirror line = $y = 0$): A point $P(x, y) \to P'(x, -y)$.
>
> $$
> M_{x} = 
> \begin{bmatrix}
> 1 & 0 & 0 \\
> 0 & -1 & 0 \\
> 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> **Reflection about Y-axis** (mirror line = $x = 0$): A point $P(x, y) \to P'(-x, y)$.
>
> $$
> M_{y} = 
> \begin{bmatrix}
> -1 & 0 & 0 \\
> 0 & 1 & 0 \\
> 0 & 0 & 1
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Q:** State any three differences between reflection and rotation as 2D transformations.

**Model Answer:**

| **S.No.** | **Reflection** | **Rotation** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Determinant of transformation matrix is $-1$ (improper transformation). | Determinant is $+1$ (proper transformation). |
| 2 | Orientation (handedness) of the object is reversed. | Orientation is preserved. |
| 3 | A point on the mirror line is fixed; all other points are mirrored. | Only the pivot point is fixed; all other points move along circular arcs. |
| 4 | Distance from mirror line is preserved but on the opposite side. | Distance from pivot is preserved, but the angular position changes. |
| 5 | Achieved by negating one or more coordinate axes. | Achieved by trigonometric rotation of the coordinate axes. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Students choose **either** Question A **or** Question B within the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q:** (a) Derive the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix for reflection of a 2D object about an arbitrary line $y = mx + c$. Mention each step clearly. (7 marks)

(b) A square with vertices $P_1(1, 1)$, $P_2(4, 1)$, $P_3(4, 4)$, $P_4(1, 4)$ is to be reflected about the Y-axis. Compute the coordinates of the reflected square using the transformation matrix. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should present the following steps in sequence to secure full marks:

1. **Step 1** — State the goal: find a $3 \times 3$ matrix $M$ such that $P' = M \cdot P$ for any point on the object. (1 mark)
2. **Step 2** — Identify $P_0(0, c)$ as a reference point on the line. (1 mark)
3. **Step 3** — Write the five sequential transformation matrices: $T(0, -c)$, $R(-\theta)$, $Ref_X$, $R(\theta)$, $T(0, c)$, where $\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)$. (2 marks)
4. **Step 4** — State the final composite matrix: $M = T(0, c) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot T(0, -c)$, with proper **right-to-left** explanation of matrix multiplication order. (2 marks)
5. **Step 5** — Mention the special case for $c = 0$ giving $M = R(\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and verify with the $y = x$ case. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

The Y-axis reflection matrix is:

$$
M_y = 
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply to each vertex in homogeneous coordinates:

- $P_1(1, 1) \to P_1' = (-1, 1)$
- $P_2(4, 1) \to P_2' = (-4, 1)$
- $P_3(4, 4) \to P_3' = (-4, 4)$
- $P_4(1, 4) \to P_4' = (-1, 4)$

**Final Answer:** The reflected square has vertices $(-1, 1)$, $(-4, 1)$, $(-4, 4)$, $(-1, 4)$ — located in the **second quadrant** of the Cartesian plane (original was in the first quadrant). The shape and size are preserved; the orientation (clockwise → counter-clockwise) is reversed.

> **Mark Distribution Hint (KTU 2024):**
> - Matrix writing: 1 mark
> - Application to all 4 vertices: 4 marks (1 mark per vertex)
> - Final answer with quadrant/orientation comment: 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q:** (a) Explain the different types of reflection in 2D transformations. For each type, write the transformation matrix and show the effect on a sample point $(3, 5)$. (7 marks)

(b) A triangle has vertices $A(2, 2)$, $B(6, 2)$, $C(4, 6)$. The triangle is reflected about (i) the X-axis and (ii) the line $y = -x$. Determine the new coordinates in each case and verify the handedness reversal. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Reflection (7 marks):**

The student should list **five standard reflections**, write the matrix for each, and show the transformation of the sample point $P(3, 5)$:

| **Type** | **Matrix** | **Transformed Point** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| X-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(3, -5)$ |
| Y-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-3, 5)$ |
| Origin | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-3, -5)$ |
| $y = x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(5, 3)$ |
| $y = -x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-5, -3)$ |

Each type carries **1.2 marks** (matrix + sample point demonstration + brief description). (7 marks total)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Reflection about X-axis:**

Matrix: $M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

- $A(2, 2) \to A'(2, -2)$
- $B(6, 2) \to B'(6, -2)$
- $C(4, 6) \to C'(4, -6)$

**(ii) Reflection about $y = -x$:**

Matrix: $M_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

- $A(2, 2) \to A'(-2, -2)$
- $B(6, 2) \to B'(-2, -6)$
- $C(4, 6) \to C'(-6, -4)$

**Handedness verification:**

- **Original triangle $ABC$:** Traversal $A \to B \to C$ goes *counter-clockwise* (right-handed). Area = $\frac{1}{2} \lvert x_A(y_B - y_C) + x_B(y_C - y_A) + x_C(y_A - y_B) \rvert = \frac{1}{2} \lvert 2(2 - 6) + 6(6 - 2) + 4(2 - 2) \rvert = \frac{1}{2} \lvert -8 + 24 + 0 \rvert = 8$ sq. units. (Positive sign of the signed-area determinant indicates CCW orientation.)
- **Reflected triangle (X-axis case):** $A'(2, -2) \to B'(6, -2) \to C'(4, -6)$. Traversal is *clockwise* — orientation has reversed. Area = 8 sq. units (preserved). ✓

> **Mark Distribution Hint (KTU 2024):**
> - Part (i) calculation: 2 marks
> - Part (ii) calculation: 2 marks
> - Handedness verification (signed area or visual sketch): 3 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Reflection Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong matrix multiplication order** (most common, ~30% of failures): When composing $T \cdot R \cdot Ref_X \cdot R^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}$, students often write it left-to-right. Remember: **rightmost matrix acts first on the point column vector.** (-2 to -3 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting homogeneous coordinates**: Writing a $2 \times 2$ matrix for the standard reflections instead of the full $3 \times 3$ matrix. The third row $(0, 0, 1)$ **must** be present for KTU board answers. (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Sign error in $y = -x$ reflection**: Students frequently swap to $(x, y) \to (y, x)$ (which is the $y = x$ case). The correct mapping is $(x, y) \to (-y, -x)$. (-1 mark per occurrence)
>
> 4. **Skipping the determinant justification**: When asked *"why is reflection called an improper transformation?"*, students often just state the determinant is $-1$ without showing the calculation $\lvert M \rvert = (1)(-1) - (0)(0) = -1$. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Not verifying the mirror line invariance**: For full marks on derivations, the student should check that a known point on the line remains unchanged (e.g., $(0, 0)$ on $y = x$ stays at $(0, 0)$, $(0, c)$ on $y = mx + c$ stays at $(0, c)$). (-1 mark if missing)
>
> 6. **Confusing "about origin" with "about line $y = -x$"**: Reflection about the origin gives $(-x, -y)$; reflection about $y = -x$ gives $(-y, -x)$. These are **different transformations**. (-2 marks if mixed up)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reflection (Module 2)**
>
> - **Definition:** Mirror image of a 2D object about a specified line. Rigid-body transformation; shape and size preserved; **orientation reversed**.
>
> - **Five standard reflections to memorize:**
>   1. X-axis → $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$ — matrix $\text{diag}(1, -1, 1)$
>   2. Y-axis → $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$ — matrix $\text{diag}(-1, 1, 1)$
>   3. Origin → $(x, y) \to (-x, -y)$ — matrix $\text{diag}(-1, -1, 1)$
>   4. $y = x$ → $(x, y) \to (y, x)$ — swap entries
>   5. $y = -x$ → $(x, y) \to (-y, -x)$ — swap and negate
>
> - **Determinant property:** All single-mirror-line reflections have $\lvert M \rvert = -1$ (improper); only the composite of two reflections (e.g., origin) gives $\lvert M \rvert = +1$.
>
> - **Composite matrix for arbitrary line $y = mx + c$:**
>   $M = T(0, c) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot Ref_X \cdot R(-\theta) \cdot T(0, -c)$, with $\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)$.
>
> - **Right-to-left multiplication rule:** In matrix composition, the **rightmost** matrix operates on the point **first**. This is the #1 valuation pitfall in KTU.
>
> - **Eigen interpretation:** Mirror line = +1 eigenspace; perpendicular direction = -1 eigenspace.
>
> - **Engineering applications:** Sheet metal mirroring, symmetric assemblies, CAM tool-path reflection, FEA symmetric boundary conditions, architectural plan symmetry.
>
> - **Handedness test:** Compute signed area using the shoelace formula; a sign change confirms the orientation reversal characteristic of reflection.
>
> - **Hand-written exam tip:** Always draw the original and reflected object in the answer sheet — KTU examiners award **partial credit (1–2 marks)** for a correct sketch even if the matrix algebra has minor errors.
>
> - **3D extension:** In 3D, reflection is about a *plane* (e.g., XY-plane, YZ-plane, XZ-plane), and the homogeneous matrix is $4 \times 4$ — out of KTU Module 2 scope, but worth mentioning in viva.
>
> - **Inverse property:** Reflection is its own inverse. Applying the same reflection twice restores the original: $M^2 = I$. This is unique to reflection among all 2D transformations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Computer Aided Design (CAD) and Computer Graphics**, a **Scaling Transformation** is a fundamental geometric transformation that alters the *size* (magnitude) of a graphical object by multiplying its coordinate values by a set of constant *scale factors*. It is one of the elementary affine transformations (along with Translation, Rotation, Reflection, and Shear) and forms the mathematical backbone of digital zoom, resizing, parametric modeling, and proportional design adjustments in CAD systems like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, and NX.

Mathematically, given a 2D point $P(x, y)$, the scaled point $P'(x', y')$ with respect to the **coordinate origin** $(0, 0)$ is expressed as:

$$
x' = S_x \cdot x
$$

$$
y' = S_y \cdot y
$$

where $S_x$ and $S_y$ are the **scale factors** along the X and Y axes respectively. The transformation can be extended to 3D by introducing $S_z$ along the Z-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – 2D/3D Transformations):**
> Scaling is taught in combination with Translation and Rotation using **Homogeneous Coordinate Systems**. The general 2D transformation matrix in homogeneous form is a $3 \times 3$ matrix, and the 3D equivalent is a $4 \times 4$ matrix. Mastering the scaling matrix is *mandatory* for solving KTU numerical problems on composite transformations.

## 1.2 Types of Scaling

| Type | Description | Scale Factors |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Scaling** | Object size changes proportionally; shape preserved | $S_x = S_y$ (and $S_x = S_y = S_z$ in 3D) |
| **Differential / Non-Uniform Scaling** | Object stretched differently along each axis; shape distorted | $S_x \neq S_y$ (or $\neq S_z$ in 3D) |
| **Scaling about the Origin** | Default scaling with respect to coordinate origin | Reference point is $(0, 0)$ |
| **Scaling about a Fixed Point** | Object scaled with respect to an arbitrary pivot | Reference point is $(x_f, y_f)$ |
| **Reflection** | A special case of scaling where $S_x = -1$ or $S_y = -1$ | Negative scale factor |
| **Inverse Scaling** | Undo a previous scaling operation | $S_x' = 1/S_x$, $S_y' = 1/S_y$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Magic Magnifying Glass"**
> Imagine you have a sketch of a rectangular plate drawn on a transparent sheet, with the origin of your drawing sheet at the bottom-left corner. Now, you place a *magic magnifying glass* over the sheet. If the magnifier enlarges things by **2×** uniformly, every point on the rectangle moves **twice as far** from the bottom-left corner. The rectangle becomes bigger, but its proportions (2:1 ratio) are preserved — this is **Uniform Scaling**.
> 
> Now, suppose the magnifier stretches the drawing **3× horizontally** but only **1× vertically**. Your rectangle will become a long, thin horizontal bar — the shape *changes* even though each point's distance from the origin is scaled. This is **Differential Scaling**.
> 
> Finally, if you place a pin at the *center* of the rectangle and the magnifier only changes the size *relative to that pin*, the rectangle grows/shrinks around the pinned point. This is **Scaling about a Fixed Point**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Engineering Units:** Scale factors $S_x, S_y, S_z$ are **dimensionless ratios** (no units). They represent the ratio of the *new dimension* to the *old dimension*. A value $S = 1.5$ means the new size is **1.5 times** the original. A value $S < 1$ indicates shrinkage; $S > 1$ indicates expansion. $S = 1$ is the **identity** (no change). $S = -1$ produces a **reflection** (mirror image) about the corresponding axis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Scaling operates by **multiplying** the coordinates of every point on the object by a *scale factor*. Unlike translation (which uses *addition*) and rotation (which uses *trigonometric functions*), scaling uses **multiplication**. This subtle distinction has important geometric consequences:

- **The origin is the only invariant point** under origin-based scaling (it maps to itself).
- **Lines remain lines, polygons remain polygons** — scaling is a *linear* transformation and preserves the basic topology of the object.
- **Angles between lines are NOT preserved** under differential scaling, which is why the *shape* (but not the *topology*) changes.
- **Parallelism is preserved**: parallel lines remain parallel after scaling.

### Why Use Homogeneous Coordinates?

In KTU problems, scaling is *always* expressed in **homogeneous coordinates** because:
1. It unifies translation, rotation, and scaling into a single $3 \times 3$ (2D) or $4 \times 4$ (3D) matrix.
2. It allows **composite transformations** to be performed by simple matrix multiplication.
3. The third (or fourth) row $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (or $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$) keeps the homogeneous $w$-coordinate equal to **1** for affine transformations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - We express scaling as a matrix to enable *programmatic* application to millions of vertices in a CAD model.
> - We use homogeneous coordinates so that *all* transformations (including translation) are expressible as matrix multiplications — making the GPU/CPU pipeline uniform.
> - We scale about a fixed point (rather than the origin) because in real CAD, we typically want to resize a part *in place* — e.g., scaling a hole about its center, not the drawing's origin.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transformation | Matrix (2D Homogeneous) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scaling about Origin** | $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Default form; pivot at $(0,0)$ |
| 2 | **Scaling about Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$** | $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Combines Translate–Scale–Translate |
| 3 | **Reflection about X-axis** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Special case: $S_y = -1$ |
| 4 | **Reflection about Y-axis** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Special case: $S_x = -1$ |
| 5 | **Reflection about Origin** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Special case: $S_x = S_y = -1$ |
| 6 | **Inverse Scaling** | $\begin{bmatrix} 1/S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Reverses a previous scaling |
| 7 | **3D Scaling about Origin** | $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix |
| 8 | **3D Scaling about Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f, z_f)$** | $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & 0 & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & S_z & z_f(1 - S_z) \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Standard KTU form |
| 9 | **Fixed-Point Equations (2D)** | $x' = S_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - S_x)$  $\;\;\;$ $y' = S_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - S_y)$ | Direct scalar form |
| 10 | **Area Scale Factor** | $A' = \vert S_x \cdot S_y \vert \cdot A$ | New area = $\vert \det(M) \vert \times$ old area |
| 11 | **Volume Scale Factor (3D)** | $V' = \vert S_x \cdot S_y \cdot S_z \vert \cdot V$ | New volume = determinant scaling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention for KTU Board Exams:** When writing the scaling matrix, students often forget the *third column* (the translation components) for the fixed-point case. Marks are awarded specifically for the correct **translation components** $x_f(1 - S_x)$ and $y_f(1 - S_y)$. Always show the *full* $3 \times 3$ matrix, not just the $2 \times 2$ core.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD Drafting:** Enlarging a small detail drawing to a standard sheet size, or shrinking a part drawing to fit a title block.
- **Parametric Modeling:** Resizing a hole, slot, or fillet by scaling factors driven by design parameters in SolidWorks/Inventor.
- **3D Printing & CNC:** Scaling a 3D model to match a specific manufacturing envelope while preserving the geometry's proportions.
- **Computer Graphics & Games:** Zoom in/out functionality, object resizing, level-of-detail transitions.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA) Pre-processing:** Mesh scaling to match physical dimensions from a normalized CAD model.
- **Cartography & GIS:** Map scaling — changing a map's scale from 1:50,000 to 1:25,000 to display a region in more detail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaling About the Origin

Starting from the fundamental definition, a point $P(x, y)$ is mapped to $P'(x', y')$ by multiplying its coordinates by $S_x$ and $S_y$:

**Step 1:** Write the basic equations.

$$
x' = S_x \cdot x \quad \text{and} \quad y' = S_y \cdot y
$$

**Step 2:** Convert these scalar equations into matrix form.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 \\ 0 & S_y \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Augment to a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix to allow composition with translation and rotation matrices.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Scaling About a Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$ — The KTU-Favorite Derivation

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU exams. The strategy is a three-step composite transformation:

**Step 1: Translate the fixed point to the origin** so that the object can be scaled about the origin.

The translation matrix $T_1$ that moves $(x_f, y_f)$ to the origin is:

$$
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Apply scaling about the origin.**

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Translate the object back** by adding $(x_f, y_f)$ to restore the fixed point's original location.

$$
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Combine the three matrices** using the order $T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1$ (the rightmost matrix operates first on the point).

$$
M = T_2 \cdot S \cdot T_1
$$

**Step 5: Compute $S \cdot T_1$ first.**

$$
S \cdot T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the full product $T_2 \cdot (S \cdot T_1)$.**

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Performing the matrix multiplication row-by-column:

- **Row 1, Col 1:** $(1)(S_x) + (0)(0) + (x_f)(0) = S_x$
- **Row 1, Col 2:** $(1)(0) + (0)(S_y) + (x_f)(0) = 0$
- **Row 1, Col 3:** $(1)(-S_x \cdot x_f) + (0)(-S_y \cdot y_f) + (x_f)(1) = x_f - S_x \cdot x_f = x_f(1 - S_x)$
- **Row 2, Col 1:** $(0)(S_x) + (1)(0) + (y_f)(0) = 0$
- **Row 2, Col 2:** $(0)(0) + (1)(S_y) + (y_f)(0) = S_y$
- **Row 2, Col 3:** $(0)(-S_x \cdot x_f) + (1)(-S_y \cdot y_f) + (y_f)(1) = y_f - S_y \cdot y_f = y_f(1 - S_y)$
- **Row 3:** Remains $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 7: Final Composite Matrix.**

$$
\boxed{
M_{\text{fixed-pt}} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
}
$$

**Step 8: Verification** — apply the matrix to the fixed point $(x_f, y_f, 1)$ and confirm it maps to itself.

$$
x' = S_x \cdot x_f + x_f(1 - S_x) = S_x \cdot x_f + x_f - S_x \cdot x_f = x_f \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
y' = S_y \cdot y_f + y_f(1 - S_y) = y_f \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The fact that the fixed point is invariant under this transformation is a *guarantee* of the formula's correctness. Always include this verification step in KTU answers to earn full marks.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Triangle Scaling About Centroid

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]:**
A triangle has vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(2, 4)$. Scale it by factors $S_x = 2$, $S_y = 2$ about the fixed point $(2, 2)$. Find the new coordinates.

**Step 1:** Set up the fixed-point scaling matrix.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2(1 - 2) \\ 0 & 2 & 2(1 - 2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the matrix to vertex $A(1, 1, 1)$.

$$
A' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (2)(1) + (0)(1) + (-2)(1) \\ (0)(1) + (2)(1) + (-2)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $A'(0, 0)$.

**Step 3:** Apply to $B(4, 1, 1)$.

$$
B' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 + 0 - 2 \\ 0 + 2 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $B'(6, 0)$.

**Step 4:** Apply to $C(2, 4, 1)$.

$$
C' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 + 0 - 2 \\ 0 + 8 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $C'(2, 6)$.

**Step 5:** Verification — the centroid $(2, 2)$ should be invariant.

$$
(2, 2) \xrightarrow{M} \begin{bmatrix} 2(2) - 2 \\ 2(2) - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
$$

**New Triangle:** $A'(0, 0)$, $B'(6, 0)$, $C'(2, 6)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Area Check:** Original area of triangle ABC = $\frac{1}{2} \vert 1(1-4) + 4(4-1) + 2(1-1) \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert -3 + 12 + 0 \vert = 4.5$ sq. units.
> New area = $\frac{1}{2} \vert 0(0-6) + 6(6-0) + 2(0-0) \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0 + 36 + 0 \vert = 18$ sq. units.
> Ratio: $18 / 4.5 = 4 = S_x \cdot S_y$ ✓ (matches Formula #10).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: The Scaling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Object P x y] --> B{Choose Pivot Type}
    B -->|Origin| C[Apply Sx 0; 0 Sy Matrix]
    B -->|Fixed Point xf yf| D[Step 1: Translate by -xf -yf]
    D --> E[Step 2: Apply Scaling Matrix]
    E --> F[Step 3: Translate by +xf +yf]
    C --> G[Output Object P' x' y']
    F --> G
    G --> H{Quality Check}
    H -->|Check fixed point| I[Verify Pivot Invariant]
    H -->|Check area| J[Verify Area = Sx Sy A]
    I --> K[Transformation Valid]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Decomposition of Fixed-Point Scaling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Point P x y] --> B[Translate to Origin T1]
    B --> C[Scale about Origin S]
    C --> D[Translate Back T2]
    D --> E[Transformed Point Pdash xdash ydash]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#d4edda
    style B fill:#fff3cd
    style C fill:#fff3cd
    style D fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Types of Scaling and Their Geometric Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Scaling Transformation] --> T1[Uniform Scaling]
    Root --> T2[Non Uniform Differential]
    Root --> T3[Reflection Special Case]
    T1 --> R1[Shape Preserved]
    T1 --> R2[Size Enlarged or Reduced]
    T2 --> N1[Shape Distorted]
    T2 --> N2[Different Factors per Axis]
    T3 --> RF1[Reflection about X axis]
    T3 --> RF2[Reflection about Y axis]
    T3 --> RF3[Reflection about Origin]
    style Root fill:#cce5ff
    style T1 fill:#d4edda
    style T2 fill:#f8d7da
    style T3 fill:#fff3cd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The first diagram shows the *complete pipeline* of a scaling operation from input to validated output. The second diagram breaks down the fixed-point case into its three atomic sub-transformations. The third diagram is a classification tree of the various scaling types encountered in the KTU syllabus.

## 4.4 Homogeneous Matrix Anatomy (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Left 2x2: Sx 0 / 0 Sy] --> M[Combined Scaling Matrix 3x3]
    B[Top Right 2x1: xf 1 minus Sx / yf 1 minus Sy] --> M
    C[Bottom Row: 0 0 1] --> M
    M --> D[Applied to Point x y 1]
    D --> E[Result: xdash ydash 1]
```
