# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between implicit, explicit, and parametric forms of representing a curve. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Form | Definition | Example (Circle) |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Implicit** | All coordinates appear in a single equation $F(x, y, z) = 0$ | $x^2 + y^2 - r^2 = 0$ |
| **Explicit** | One coordinate is expressed as a function of others | $y = \pm\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$ (loses sign info) |
| **Parametric** | Coordinates are functions of a single parameter | $P(\theta) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ |

**Why this matters:** Parametric form is preferred in CAD because it handles multi-valued and closed curves (e.g., circles) without ambiguity and directly gives tangent/normal vectors via differentiation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the Bernstein basis functions of degree 3 and state two important properties of a cubic Bezier curve.**

**Model Answer:**

The Bernstein basis functions of degree 3 are:
$$B_{0,3}(u) = (1-u)^3$$
$$B_{1,3}(u) = 3u(1-u)^2$$
$$B_{2,3}(u) = 3u^2(1-u)$$
$$B_{3,3}(u) = u^3$$

**Two key properties:**
1. **Partition of unity:** $B_{0,3} + B_{1,3} + B_{2,3} + B_{3,3} = 1$ for all $u \in [0, 1]$.
2. **Convex hull property:** The curve lies entirely within the convex hull of its control polygon $\lbrace P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3 \rbrace$.

(Additional property accepted: Endpoints interpolation: $P(0) = P_0$, $P(1) = P_3$.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Derive the parametric form of a circle of radius $r$ centered at origin from its implicit equation. Show all steps clearly. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the implicit form.**
The implicit equation of a circle of radius $r$ centered at the origin in the $xy$-plane is:
$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the Pythagorean trigonometric identity.**
$$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply equation (2) by $r^2$.**
$$r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta = r^2$$
$$(r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2 = r^2 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Compare (1) and (3).**
Both have the structure of a sum of squares equaling $r^2$. Therefore, identify:
$$x = r\cos\theta \quad \text{...(4)}$$
$$y = r\sin\theta \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 5 — Form the parametric vector.**
$$P(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} x(\theta) \\ y(\theta) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\theta \\ r\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Specify the parameter range.**
$$\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$$

This range ensures the parameter sweeps out the **entire** circle exactly once.

**Step 7 — Verification by substitution.**
Substituting (4) and (5) into (1):
$$(r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2 = r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = r^2 \cdot 1 = r^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Mention an engineering application.**
This parametric form is the basis for circular interpolation in CNC machining, where the controller moves the tool along circular arcs using the G-code commands **G02** (clockwise) and **G03** (counter-clockwise), with the center and radius (or $I, J, K$ offsets) specified by the programmer.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the construction of a cubic Bezier curve. Derive the curve equation using Bernstein polynomials and list any four properties. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define control points and parameter.**
A cubic Bezier curve is defined by **4 control points** $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$ and a parameter $u \in [0, 1]$.

- $P_0$ — start point (curve passes through it)
- $P_3$ — end point (curve passes through it)
- $P_1, P_2$ — handle/control points (pull the curve, not on the curve)

**Step 2 — Write the Bernstein basis functions of degree 3.**
$$B_{0,3}(u) = (1-u)^3$$
$$B_{1,3}(u) = 3u(1-u)^2$$
$$B_{2,3}(u) = 3u^2(1-u)$$
$$B_{3,3}(u) = u^3$$

**Step 3 — Derive the curve equation.**
The cubic Bezier curve is defined as a weighted sum of the control points, with the Bernstein basis as weights:
$$P(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} B_{i,3}(u) P_i$$

Expanding:
$$P(u) = (1-u)^3 P_0 + 3u(1-u)^2 P_1 + 3u^2(1-u) P_2 + u^3 P_3$$

**Step 4 — Expand into polynomial form.**
$$P(u) = (-P_0 + 3P_1 - 3P_2 + P_3)u^3 + (3P_0 - 6P_1 + 3P_2)u^2 + (-3P_0 + 3P_1)u + P_0$$

This confirms that a cubic Bezier curve is a **cubic polynomial** in $u$.

**Step 5 — List four properties.**

1. **Endpoint interpolation:** $P(0) = P_0$ and $P(1) = P_3$.
2. **Convex hull property:** The curve lies entirely within the convex hull of $\lbrace P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3 \rbrace$.
3. **Partition of unity:** $B_{0,3} + B_{1,3} + B_{2,3} + B_{3,3} = 1$ for all $u \in [0, 1]$.
4. **Affine invariance:** Applying an affine transformation to the control points is equivalent to applying it to the curve itself.
5. **Symmetry:** Reversing the order of control points ($P_0 \leftrightarrow P_3, P_1 \leftrightarrow P_2$) gives the same curve traced in reverse.
6. **Variation diminishing:** The curve does not oscillate more than its control polygon.

(Any four accepted; the first four are the most commonly tested.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** What are the different types of algebraic forms used to represent curves and surfaces in CAD? Derive the parametric form of an ellipse with semi-major axis $a$ and semi-minor axis $b$. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the algebraic forms.**

1. **Implicit form:** $F(x, y, z) = 0$ — all variables in one equation.
2. **Explicit form:** $y = f(x)$ or $z = f(x, y)$ — one variable solved.
3. **Parametric form:** $P(u) = [x(u), y(u), z(u)]$ — coordinates as functions of a parameter.

**Step 2 — Implicit form of an ellipse centered at origin.**
$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3 — Use the parametric substitution based on $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.**
Let:
$$x = a\cos\theta \quad \text{...(2)}$$
$$y = b\sin\theta \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} &= \frac{(a\cos\theta)^2}{a^2} + \frac{(b\sin\theta)^2}{b^2} \\
&= \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final parametric form.**
$$P(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} a\cos\theta \\ b\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad \theta \in [0, 2\pi]$$

**Step 6 — Tangent vector (for engineering use).**
$$P'(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -a\sin\theta \\ b\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Curvature at the major-axis endpoint** ($\theta = 0$):
$$\kappa(0) = \frac{a}{b^2}$$

This is critical in cam design and gear tooth profiling.

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the tangent vector and curvature formula for a general 2D parametric curve $P(u) = (x(u), y(u))$. Apply the result to a circle of radius $r$ and verify the curvature is uniform. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tangent vector.**
Differentiating $P(u)$ with respect to $u$:
$$P'(u) = \left( \frac{dx}{du}, \frac{dy}{du} \right) = (x'(u), y'(u))$$

**Step 2 — Unit tangent vector.**
$$T(u) = \frac{P'(u)}{|P'(u)|} = \frac{(x'(u), y'(u))}{\sqrt{[x'(u)]^2 + [y'(u)]^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Unit normal vector** (perpendicular to $T$, obtained by 90° rotation):
$$N(u) = \frac{(-y'(u), x'(u))}{\sqrt{[x'(u)]^2 + [y'(u)]^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Curvature formula.**
$$\kappa(u) = \frac{|x'(u) y''(u) - y'(u) x''(u)|}{\left([x'(u)]^2 + [y'(u)]^2\right)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply to a circle** $P(\theta) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$:

First derivatives:
$$x'(\theta) = -r\sin\theta, \quad y'(\theta) = r\cos\theta$$

Second derivatives:
$$x''(\theta) = -r\cos\theta, \quad y''(\theta) = -r\sin\theta$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator.**
$$\begin{aligned}
|x'(\theta) y''(\theta) - y'(\theta) x''(\theta)| &= |(-r\sin\theta)(-r\sin\theta) - (r\cos\theta)(-r\cos\theta)| \\
&= |r^2\sin^2\theta + r^2\cos^2\theta| \\
&= r^2(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) = r^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the denominator.**
$$\begin{aligned}
([x'(\theta)]^2 + [y'(\theta)]^2)^{3/2} &= (r^2\sin^2\theta + r^2\cos^2\theta)^{3/2} \\
&= (r^2)^{3/2} = r^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Curvature.**
$$\kappa(\theta) = \frac{r^2}{r^3} = \frac{1}{r}$$

**Conclusion:** The curvature is **independent of $\theta$** and equals $1/r$. This is the geometric definition of a circle: the locus of points at a constant distance $r$ from a center has uniform curvature $1/r$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in curvature formula:** Many students forget the **absolute value** in the numerator. Curvature is always non-negative.
> 2. **Forgetting parameter range:** When writing parametric forms, always specify the domain (e.g., $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ for a full circle). Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Convex hull property confusion:** Students often incorrectly state that the curve passes through $P_1$ and $P_2$. It does **not** — only $P_0$ and $P_3$ are interpolated.
> 4. **Implicit vs Parametric misidentification:** A common error is calling $y = mx + c$ a "parametric" form. It is **explicit** form; parametric requires a separate parameter variable.
> 5. **Missing the "why":** Examiners award marks for explaining the engineering relevance (CNC, FEA, CAM). A purely mathematical answer without application context loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Partition of unity proof skipped:** When asked to "derive" or "show" Bernstein properties, students must **show the expansion**, not just state the result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Algebraic and Geometric Forms**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Three algebraic forms** of a curve: Implicit $F(x,y,z)=0$, Explicit $y=f(x)$, Parametric $P(u)=[x(u),y(u),z(u)]$.
- ☐ Parametric form is **universal in CAD** because it handles closed/multi-valued curves and gives derivatives directly.
- ☐ **Conic sections** (circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) are the building blocks of classical geometric modeling.
- ☐ Modern CAD uses **Bezier, B-Spline, and NURBS** — all parametric and geometric (control-point-based).

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ Circle: $x^2+y^2=r^2$ ↔ $P(\theta)=(r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$
- ☐ Ellipse: $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ ↔ $P(\theta)=(a\cos\theta, b\sin\theta)$
- ☐ Parabola: $y^2=4ax$ ↔ $P(t)=(at^2, 2at)$
- ☐ Plane: $ax+by+cz+d=0$ ↔ $\vec{r}(u,v)=\vec{r_0}+u\vec{a}+v\vec{b}$
- ☐ Sphere: $x^2+y^2+z^2=r^2$ ↔ parametric in $\theta, \phi$
- ☐ Bernstein basis: $B_{i,n}(u)=\binom{n}{i}u^i(1-u)^{n-i}$
- ☐ Cubic Bezier: $P(u)=(1-u)^3P_0+3u(1-u)^2P_1+3u^2(1-u)P_2+u^3P_3$
- ☐ Curvature: $\kappa(u)=\frac{|x'y''-y'x''|}{[(x')^2+(y')^2]^{3/2}}$
- ☐ Curvature of a circle: $\kappa = 1/r$ (uniform)

### Properties to State When Asked
- ☐ **Partition of unity:** Sum of Bernstein basis functions = 1.
- ☐ **Convex hull:** Bezier curve lies within the control polygon.
- ☐ **Endpoint interpolation:** $P(0)=P_0$, $P(1)=P_n$.
- ☐ **Affine invariance:** Affine transforms of control points = affine transform of curve.
- ☐ **Variation diminishing:** Bezier curve is "smoother" than its control polygon.

### Conversion Procedures
- ☐ Implicit → Parametric: Use trigonometric substitution (e.g., $x^2+y^2=r^2 \Rightarrow x=r\cos\theta$).
- ☐ Parametric → Implicit: Eliminate the parameter algebraically.
- ☐ Hermite → Bezier: Use the conversion matrix $B_0=P_0$, $B_1=P_0+P_0'/3$, $B_2=P_1-P_1'/3$, $B_3=P_1$.

### Engineering Applications to Mention in Answers
- ☐ **CNC machining:** G02/G03 circular interpolation, APT toolpaths.
- ☐ **Automotive/Aerospace:** Class-A NURBS surfacing.
- ☐ **FEA:** Geometry → Mesh → Solver pipeline.
- ☐ **Reverse Engineering:** Point cloud → NURBS fitting.
- ☐ **Data Exchange:** IGES, STEP, STL formats for interoperability.

### Common Examiner Traps
- ☐ Forgetting parameter domain $\Rightarrow$ -1 mark.
- ☐ Confusing explicit with parametric $\Rightarrow$ -2 marks.
- ☐ Missing absolute value in curvature $\Rightarrow$ -1 mark.
- ☐ Skipping the verification step in derivations $\Rightarrow$ -1 mark.
- ☐ No engineering context mentioned $\Rightarrow$ -1 to -2 marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(1)** State the form/equation, **(2)** Show step-by-step derivation, **(3)** Verify the result, **(4)** State 2–3 properties, **(5)** Mention one engineering application. This 5-part structure consistently scores **12+/14 marks** in KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangents and Normals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tangents and Normals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the differential calculus framework underpinning CAD and geometric modeling, a **tangent** to a curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the limiting position of a secant line $PQ$ as the second point $Q$ approaches $P$ along the curve. Mathematically, the slope of the tangent equals the **first derivative** of the function evaluated at that point.

$$m_{tangent} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{(x_1, y_1)} = f'(x_1)$$

A **normal** to the curve at $P$ is the line passing through $P$ and perpendicular to the tangent at that point. Since the product of slopes of two perpendicular lines is $-1$, the slope of the normal is the **negative reciprocal** of the tangent's slope.

$$m_{normal} = -\frac{1}{m_{tangent}} = -\frac{1}{f'(x_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** *A tangent to a curve is a straight line that touches the curve at a given point and has the same instantaneous direction as the curve at that point. The normal is the straight line perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car along a curved road.

- **The Tangent** is the direction your steering wheel points at any instant — it is the *instantaneous direction of travel*. If you release the wheel and drive "straight" for a brief moment, you follow the tangent.
- **The Normal** is the direction perpendicular to your motion — it points *sideways off the road*, into the shoulder or the median. In banking, the normal direction determines the centripetal force.

In **CAD/CAM software** (AutoCAD, CATIA, SolidWorks, ANSYS), when you click a point on a spline or Bezier curve, the software computes the tangent vector to:
1. Place the **tool path orientation** correctly during CNC machining.
2. Generate **surface normals** for finite element meshing and stress analysis.
3. Drive the **light direction** in ray-traced rendering (normals are used to compute shading).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CAD:** Every spline, fillet, and chamfer in a 3D model is mathematically defined by a parametric curve, and its *normal vector* is what determines how light reflects, how forces act, and how the next surface joins smoothly.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Constants & Conventions)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Angle made by tangent with positive x-axis | $m = \tan \theta$ |
| $m$ | Slope of tangent | $m = \frac{dy}{dx}$ |
| $P(x_1, y_1)$ | Point of tangency | Must lie **on** the curve |
| $\psi$ | Angle between two curves | $\tan \psi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The angle $\theta$ a tangent makes with the **positive x-axis** is the **inclination** of the tangent. Always report it modulo $\pi$ (since parallel tangents differ by $\pi$, not by $2\pi$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Tangents and Normals Are Computed

The end-to-end procedure is best understood as a five-stage pipeline:

1. **Verify the point lies on the curve.** Substitute $(x_1, y_1)$ into $y = f(x)$. If equality fails, the point is not on the curve.
2. **Differentiate implicitly or explicitly** to obtain $\frac{dy}{dx} = f'(x)$.
3. **Evaluate the slope** at the point of tangency: $m = f'(x_1)$.
4. **Write the line equations** using the point-slope form.
5. **Compute derived geometric quantities** (sub-tangent, sub-normal, length of tangent, length of normal) using the slope and the y-coordinate of the point.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 is non-negotiable.** A tangent is defined *at a point on the curve*. If the point is external, the correct tool is the equation of a tangent *from an external point*, which is a different problem entirely.
- **Step 2 requires care.** For implicit curves like $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$, explicit differentiation is impossible — use **implicit differentiation** to extract $\frac{dy}{dx}$.
- **Step 3 is the heart of the method.** The derivative is literally the instantaneous rate of change — it encodes the slope.
- **Step 4 — point-slope form** is the simplest robust form because it requires only one slope and one point.
- **Step 5 — geometric projections** matter in kinematics (velocity vector decomposition) and CAD (foot of perpendicular calculations).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every Part A and Part B question on this module is solvable using one or more of these formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Geometric Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slope of tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ | $m = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{(x_1, y_1)}$ | Direction of curve at that instant |
| 2 | Equation of tangent | $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ | Line touching the curve at $(x_1, y_1)$ |
| 3 | Slope of normal | $m_n = -\dfrac{1}{m}$ | Direction perpendicular to tangent |
| 4 | Equation of normal | $y - y_1 = -\dfrac{1}{m}(x - x_1)$ | Line perpendicular to tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ |
| 5 | Inclination of tangent | $m = \tan \theta$ | $\theta$ is angle with +ve x-axis |
| 6 | Sub-tangent (ST) | $ST = \dfrac{y_1}{dy/dx} = y_1 \dfrac{dx}{dy}$ | Projection of tangent segment on x-axis |
| 7 | Sub-normal (SN) | $SN = y_1 \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Projection of normal segment on x-axis |
| 8 | Length of tangent (LT) | $LT = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dx}{dy}\right)^2}$ | Distance from $(x_1, y_1)$ to x-intercept of tangent |
| 9 | Length of normal (LN) | $LN = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$ | Distance from $(x_1, y_1)$ to x-intercept of normal |
| 10 | Angle between two curves | $\tan \psi = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$ | Acute angle at common point |
| 11 | Orthogonal (perpendicular) intersection | $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ | Curves cross at $90^\circ$ |
| 12 | Condition for tangency | Curve meets auxiliary condition $D = 0$ | Line touches curve at exactly one point |

**Boundary / Special Conditions (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**

- If $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at a point → tangent is **horizontal** (parallel to x-axis): $y = y_1$.
- If $\frac{dy}{dx} \to \infty$ at a point → tangent is **vertical** (parallel to y-axis): $x = x_1$.
- If the curve has a **cusp or vertical tangent** at $x = a$, then $\frac{dx}{dy} = 0$ at that point.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Tangent/Normal Concept |
|---|---|
| **CAD (NURBS, Bezier)** | Continuity of curvature requires smooth normal vectors across patches |
| **CNC Machining** | Tool orientation vector = surface normal at contact point |
| **Finite Element Analysis** | Pressure loads applied along surface normals; contact pairs use normal direction |
| **Robotics** | End-effector trajectory planning uses tangent vectors for path smoothing |
| **Computer Graphics** | Phong/Blinn shading uses surface normals to compute light reflection |
| **Civil Engineering** | Slope of a road embankment = tangent direction; cross-section normals = load directions |

> [!NOTE]
> In **ANSYS / Abaqus**, the term "surface normal" you see in element contact definitions is mathematically identical to the normal derived in this module. The fundamentals you learn here directly power industrial simulation software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of the Tangent

**Goal:** Prove that the equation of the tangent to $y = f(x)$ at $P(x_1, y_1)$ is $y - y_1 = f'(x_1)(x - x_1)$.

Let $P(x_1, y_1)$ and $Q(x_1 + \Delta x, y_1 + \Delta y)$ be two neighboring points on the curve $y = f(x)$, so both satisfy the curve equation:

$$y_1 = f(x_1) \quad \text{and} \quad y_1 + \Delta y = f(x_1 + \Delta x)$$

The slope of the secant line $PQ$ is:

$$m_{PQ} = \frac{(y_1 + \Delta y) - y_1}{(x_1 + \Delta x) - x_1} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

As $Q \to P$, we have $\Delta x \to 0$, and the secant line rotates into the tangent line. Taking the limit:

$$m_{tangent} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x = x_1} = f'(x_1)$$

Using the point-slope form of a straight line passing through $(x_1, y_1)$ with slope $f'(x_1)$:

$$\boxed{y - y_1 = f'(x_1)\,(x - x_1)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Equation of the Normal

Since the normal is **perpendicular** to the tangent, the product of their slopes equals $-1$:

$$m_{tangent} \cdot m_{normal} = -1$$

Therefore:

$$m_{normal} = -\frac{1}{m_{tangent}} = -\frac{1}{f'(x_1)}$$

Substituting into the point-slope form:

$$\boxed{y - y_1 = -\frac{1}{f'(x_1)}\,(x - x_1)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Sub-Tangent and Sub-Normal

**Setup:** Let the tangent at $P(x_1, y_1)$ meet the x-axis at $T(x_2, 0)$, and the normal meet the x-axis at $N(x_3, 0)$. The segments $PT$ and $PN$ are the **length of tangent** and **length of normal** respectively. Their projections onto the x-axis are the **sub-tangent** and **sub-normal**.

Let $\theta$ be the angle the tangent makes with the positive x-axis, so $m = \tan \theta$.

From the right triangle $PTN$ (and the y-coordinate of $P$ being the height):

- $PT$ makes angle $\theta$ with x-axis → vertical drop = $PT \sin \theta$ = $y_1$ → $PT = y_1 \csc \theta$.
- Horizontal projection of $PT$ = $PT \cos \theta$ = $y_1 \cot \theta$.

Therefore:

$$ST = y_1 \cot \theta = \frac{y_1}{\tan \theta} = \frac{y_1}{dy/dx} = y_1 \frac{dx}{dy}$$

Similarly for the normal, which makes angle $(90^\circ - \theta)$ with the x-axis, but more directly:

$$SN = y_1 \tan \theta = y_1 \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$$

For the lengths themselves:

$$LT = \sqrt{ST^2 + y_1^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{y_1}{dy/dx}\right)^2 + y_1^2} = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)^2}$$

$$LN = \sqrt{SN^2 + y_1^2} = \sqrt{\left(y_1 \frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 + y_1^2} = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick:** ST has $y_1$ in numerator; SN has $y_1$ in the **product** with $\frac{dy}{dx}$. So **ST = y₁ ÷ (dy/dx)** and **SN = y₁ × (dy/dx)**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Angle Between Two Curves

Let two curves $C_1: y = f(x)$ and $C_2: y = g(x)$ intersect at a point $P$. Let $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ be the angles their respective tangents make with the x-axis, with slopes $m_1 = f'(x_1)$ and $m_2 = g'(x_1)$.

The acute angle $\psi$ between the two tangent lines equals the absolute difference of their inclinations:

$$\psi = \vert \theta_1 - \theta_2 \vert$$

Using the tangent subtraction identity:

$$\tan \psi = \tan(\theta_1 - \theta_2) = \frac{\tan \theta_1 - \tan \theta_2}{1 + \tan \theta_1 \tan \theta_2} = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}$$

Taking the absolute value to ensure the acute angle:

$$\boxed{\tan \psi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert}$$

**Special Case — Orthogonal Intersection:** If the curves cut at right angles, then $\psi = 90^\circ$, so $\tan \psi \to \infty$. This requires the denominator to vanish:

$$1 + m_1 m_2 = 0 \implies m_1 m_2 = -1$$

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** *Find the equation of the tangent and normal to the curve $y = x^2$ at the point $(1, 1)$. Hence compute the length of the tangent, length of the normal, sub-tangent, and sub-normal at this point.*

**Step 1 — Verify the point lies on the curve.**

At $x_1 = 1$, $f(1) = 1^2 = 1 = y_1$. ✓ The point is on the curve.

**Step 2 — Differentiate the curve.**

$$y = x^2 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = 2x$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the slope at $(1, 1)$.**

$$m = \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=1} = 2(1) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Write the equation of the tangent.**

Using point-slope form $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$:

$$y - 1 = 2(x - 1)$$

$$y - 1 = 2x - 2$$

$$\boxed{y = 2x - 1} \quad \text{...(Tangent)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the slope of the normal.**

$$m_n = -\frac{1}{m} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Write the equation of the normal.**

$$y - 1 = -\frac{1}{2}(x - 1)$$

$$2(y - 1) = -(x - 1)$$

$$2y - 2 = -x + 1$$

$$\boxed{x + 2y = 3} \quad \text{...(Normal)}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the sub-tangent (ST).**

$$ST = \frac{y_1}{dy/dx} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the sub-normal (SN).**

$$SN = y_1 \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 1 \cdot 2 = 2 \text{ units}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the length of the tangent (LT).**

$$LT = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)^2} = 1 \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{5}{4}}$$

$$\boxed{LT = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.118 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 10 — Compute the length of the normal (LN).**

$$LN = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2} = 1 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5}$$

$$\boxed{LN = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 11 — Inclination of the tangent.**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(m) = \tan^{-1}(2) \approx 63.43^\circ$$

---

### 3.6 Second Worked Example — Angle Between Two Curves

> **Problem:** *Find the angle of intersection between the parabola $y^2 = 4x$ and the ellipse $4x^2 + y^2 = 8$ at their common point in the first quadrant.*

**Step 1 — Find the common point.**

From the parabola: $y^2 = 4x$, so $x = \dfrac{y^2}{4}$. Substitute into the ellipse:

$$4\left(\frac{y^2}{4}\right)^2 + y^2 = 8$$

$$4 \cdot \frac{y^4}{16} + y^2 = 8$$

$$\frac{y^4}{4} + y^2 - 8 = 0$$

Let $u = y^2$:

$$\frac{u^2}{4} + u - 8 = 0 \implies u^2 + 4u - 32 = 0$$

$$(u + 8)(u - 4) = 0 \implies u = 4 \quad (\text{rejecting } u = -8)$$

So $y^2 = 4 \Rightarrow y = 2$ (first quadrant). Then $x = y^2/4 = 1$.

**Common point:** $P(1, 2)$ ✓

**Step 2 — Slope of tangent to $y^2 = 4x$ (implicit differentiation).**

$$2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 4 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4}{2y} = \frac{2}{y}$$

At $(1, 2)$:

$$m_1 = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Slope of tangent to $4x^2 + y^2 = 8$.**

$$8x + 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{8x}{2y} = -\frac{4x}{y}$$

At $(1, 2)$:

$$m_2 = -\frac{4(1)}{2} = -2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angle.**

$$\tan \psi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{1 - (-2)}{1 + (1)(-2)} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{3}{1 - 2} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{3}{-1} \right\vert = 3$$

$$\boxed{\psi = \tan^{-1}(3) \approx 71.57^\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of orthogonality check:** $m_1 \cdot m_2 = (1)(-2) = -2 \neq -1$. Therefore, the curves are **not** perpendicular at this point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Tangent & Normal Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Given curve y = fx and point P x1 y1]) --> verify{Does P lie on curve?}
    verify -- No --> invalid[Reject: Point is not on the curve]
    verify -- Yes --> diff[Differentiate to get dydx = f'x]
    diff --> eval[Evaluate slope m = f'x1 at x1]
    eval --> tangent[Equation of tangent: y minus y1 = m x minus x1]
    eval --> normalslope[Slope of normal: mn = negative 1 over m]
    normalslope --> normalline[Equation of normal: y minus y1 = negative 1 over m times x minus x1]
    tangent --> geom[Compute geometric quantities]
    normalline --> geom
    geom --> st[Sub-tangent ST = y1 divided by dydx]
    geom --> sn[Sub-normal SN = y1 times dydx]
    geom --> lt[Length of tangent LT = y1 times sqrt 1 plus dx by dy squared]
    geom --> ln[Length of normal LN = y1 times sqrt 1 plus dy by dx squared]
    geom --> incl[Inclination theta = arctan m]
    st --> final([Output all quantities in boxed form])
    sn --> final
    lt --> final
    ln --> final
    incl --> final
```

### 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Sub-tangent and Sub-normal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    origin([Origin O]):::axis
    tx[T: Tangent meets x-axis]:::point
    nx[N: Normal meets x-axis]:::point
    p[P: Point of tangency x1, y1]:::point
    pt[Length of tangent LT]:::segment
    pn[Length of normal LN]:::segment
    stn[Sub-tangent ST: from T to foot of perp]:::projection
    snn[Sub-normal SN: from N to foot of perp]:::projection
    yline[y1: vertical height of P above x-axis]:::height
    origin --- tx
    tx --- nx
    nx --- origin
    p --- pt
    pt --- tx
    p --- pn
    pn --- nx
    p --- stn
    p --- snn
    p --- yline
    classDef axis fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef point fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef segment fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    classDef projection fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef height fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Relationship Between All Geometric Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: y1 and dydx at P] --> B{Sign and magnitude of dydx}
    B -- dydx positive --> C[Slope of tangent m positive]
    B -- dydx negative --> D[Slope of tangent m negative]
    B -- dydx zero --> E[Horizontal tangent: y = y1]
    B -- dydx undefined --> F[Vertical tangent: x = x1]
    C --> G[ST = y1 / dydx positive]
    C --> H[SN = y1 x dydx positive]
    C --> I[LT = y1 sqrt 1 + 1/m^2]
    C --> J[LN = y1 sqrt 1 + m^2]
    D --> G2[ST = y1 / dydx negative: measured leftward]
    D --> H2[SN = y1 x dydx negative: measured leftward]
    D --> I2[LT always positive: distance]
    D --> J2[LN always positive: distance]
    E --> K[ST undefined or infinite; SN = 0]
    F --> L[ST = 0; SN undefined or infinite]
    I --> M[Final Output: Vector of 5 quantities]
    J --> M
    G --> M
    H --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation of these diagrams:** The first flowchart is the **algorithmic procedure** a student must follow in the exam hall. The second is the **geometric mental model** to visualize the projections. The third is the **decision logic** for handling special-case slopes ($\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ or undefined). In CAD, the same decision logic governs how splines are rendered near vertical tangents or cusps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define the term "tangent to a curve". Hence find the slope of the tangent to the curve $y = x^3 - 2x + 5$ at the point $x = 1$.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A tangent to a curve at a point $P$ is the limiting position of a secant line $PQ$ as the point $Q$ approaches $P$ along the curve. Its slope equals the derivative $\frac{dy}{dx}$ evaluated at $P$.

> **Solution:**

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2 - 2$$

At $x = 1$:

$$m = 3(1)^2 - 2 = 3 - 2 = 1$$

$$\boxed{m = 1}$$

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition 1 mark + differentiation 1 mark + evaluation 1 mark = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is the condition for two curves to intersect orthogonally? Check whether the curves $y = x^2$ and $xy = 1$ intersect at right angles at their common point.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Condition:** Two curves intersect orthogonally if the product of the slopes of their tangents at the common point equals $-1$, i.e., $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$.

> **Solution:**

For $y = x^2$: $\frac{dy}{dx} = 2x$, so $m_1 = 2x$.

For $xy = 1 \Rightarrow y = \frac{1}{x}$: $\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{1}{x^2}$, so $m_2 = -\frac{1}{x^2}$.

Common points: $x^2 = \frac{1}{x} \Rightarrow x^3 = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1$, $y = 1$.

At $(1, 1)$: $m_1 = 2$, $m_2 = -1$.

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = (2)(-1) = -2 \neq -1$$

$$\boxed{\text{The curves do NOT intersect orthogonally.}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**(a)** Find the equation of the tangent and normal to the curve $y = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$ at the point $(1, 0)$. Also find the point where the tangent is parallel to the x-axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the length of the tangent, length of the normal, sub-tangent, and sub-normal for the curve $y = 2x^2$ at the point where $x = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Find the slope of the tangent at $(1, 0)$.**

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 3x^2 - 6x$$

At $x = 1$:

$$m = 3(1)^2 - 6(1) = 3 - 6 = -3$$

**Step 2 — Equation of the tangent.**

$$y - 0 = -3(x - 1) \implies \boxed{y = -3x + 3}$$

**Step 3 — Equation of the normal.**

$$m_n = -\frac{1}{-3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$y - 0 = \frac{1}{3}(x - 1) \implies \boxed{3y = x - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Point where tangent is parallel to the x-axis.**

For the tangent to be parallel to the x-axis, $m = 0$:

$$3x^2 - 6x = 0 \implies 3x(x - 2) = 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ or } x = 2$$

At $x = 0$: $y = 0 - 0 + 2 = 2 \Rightarrow (0, 2)$.

At $x = 2$: $y = 8 - 12 + 2 = -2 \Rightarrow (2, -2)$.

$$\boxed{\text{Tangent is parallel to x-axis at } (0, 2) \text{ and } (2, -2)}$$

**Step 5 — Tangent equations at these points** (for completeness).

At $(0, 2)$: $y = 2$.

At $(2, -2)$: $y = -2$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Find the point.**

At $x = 1$: $y = 2(1)^2 = 2 \Rightarrow P(1, 2)$. ✓

**Step 2 — Compute the slope.**

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 4x \implies m = 4(1) = 4$$

**Step 3 — Sub-tangent.**

$$ST = \frac{y_1}{dy/dx} = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ unit}$$

**Step 4 — Sub-normal.**

$$SN = y_1 \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Length of tangent.**

$$LT = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right)^2} = y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{1}{m}\right)^2} = 2\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{16}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{17}{16}}$$

$$\boxed{LT = \frac{\sqrt{17}}{2} \approx 2.06 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 6 — Length of normal.**

$$LN = y_1 \sqrt{1 + m^2} = 2\sqrt{1 + 16} = 2\sqrt{17}$$

$$\boxed{LN = 2\sqrt{17} \approx 8.25 \text{ units}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Find the angle of intersection of the curves $y = x^2$ and $x^2 + y^2 = 5$ at their common point(s) in the first quadrant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the curves $x^2 - y^2 = a^2$ and $xy = c^2$ intersect orthogonally for any value of $a$ and $c$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Find the common point(s) in the first quadrant.**

Substitute $y = x^2$ into $x^2 + y^2 = 5$:

$$x^2 + (x^2)^2 = 5 \implies x^2 + x^4 = 5 \implies x^4 + x^2 - 5 = 0$$

Let $u = x^2$:

$$u^2 + u - 5 = 0 \implies u = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 20}}{2} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{21}}{2}$$

$$x = \sqrt{\frac{-1 + \sqrt{21}}{2}}, \quad y = x^2 = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{21}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute slopes at the common point.**

For $y = x^2$:

$$m_1 = 2x$$

For $x^2 + y^2 = 5$:

$$2x + 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies m_2 = -\frac{x}{y} = -\frac{x}{x^2} = -\frac{1}{x}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product of slopes.**

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = 2x \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x}\right) = -2$$

**Step 4 — Apply orthogonal condition check.**

Since $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -2 \neq -1$, the curves are **not orthogonal** at this point.

**Step 5 — Compute the angle.**

$$\tan \psi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{2x + \frac{1}{x}}{1 - 2} \right\vert = \left\vert 2x + \frac{1}{x} \right\vert$$

For the numerical value, let $x = \sqrt{\frac{-1 + \sqrt{21}}{2}}$. With $\sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$:

$$x \approx \sqrt{\frac{3.583}{2}} \approx \sqrt{1.791} \approx 1.339$$

$$\tan \psi = \left\vert 2(1.339) + \frac{1}{1.339} \right\vert = \vert 2.678 + 0.747 \vert \approx 3.425$$

$$\boxed{\psi = \tan^{-1}(3.425) \approx 73.74^\circ}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Find the common point.**

From $xy = c^2$, we have $y = \frac{c^2}{x}$. Substitute into $x^2 - y^2 = a^2$:

$$x^2 - \frac{c^4}{x^2} = a^2 \implies x^4 - a^2 x^2 - c^4 = 0$$

This is a quadratic in $x^2$:

$$x^2 = \frac{a^2 \pm \sqrt{a^4 + 4c^4}}{2}$$

(For orthogonal intersection to hold, we only need to compute slopes at the common point.)

**Step 2 — Slope of tangent to $x^2 - y^2 = a^2$.**

$$2x - 2y \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies m_1 = \frac{x}{y}$$

**Step 3 — Slope of tangent to $xy = c^2$.**

$$x \frac{dy}{dx} + y = 0 \implies m_2 = -\frac{y}{x}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the product of slopes.**

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = \frac{x}{y} \cdot \left(-\frac{y}{x}\right) = -1$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

Since $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ for **all** values of $a$ and $c$ (the parameters cancel out), the curves intersect orthogonally at every common point.

$$\boxed{\text{Hence, the curves are orthogonal for all } a, c.}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU favorite problem** — the kind that appears almost every year in some form. Memorize the slope-computation technique for hyperbolas and rectangular hyperbolas.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks per Question:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the point lies on the curve** (loses 1 mark). Always substitute $(x_1, y_1)$ into $y = f(x)$ before doing anything else.
> 2. **Mixing up $ST$ and $SN$ formulas.** Remember: **ST has $y_1$ divided by $\frac{dy}{dx}$**; SN has **$y_1$ multiplied by $\frac{dy}{dx}$**. A useful check: $ST \cdot SN = y_1^2$ (always).
> 3. **Using $\tan^{-1}(m)$ instead of $\tan^{-1}(m)$ in degrees** — the answer is expected in degrees, not radians.
> 4. **Dropping the absolute value** in the angle formula $\tan \psi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$. The angle of intersection is always the **acute** angle.
> 5. **Sign error when $\frac{dy}{dx} < 0$** — the sub-tangent becomes negative, meaning it lies on the negative x-direction. State this explicitly or the examiner deducts marks for "incomplete interpretation".
> 6. **Failing to handle special cases** $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ or $\frac{dy}{dx} \to \infty$ — always mention these separately.
> 7. **In implicit differentiation**, forgetting to apply the product/chain rule to **both** occurrences of $x$ and $y$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Tangent slope = derivative at the point**: $m = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{(x_1, y_1)}$
- [ ] **Normal slope = negative reciprocal**: $m_n = -\dfrac{1}{m}$
- [ ] **Tangent line equation**: $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$
- [ ] **Normal line equation**: $y - y_1 = -\dfrac{1}{m}(x - x_1)$
- [ ] **Sub-tangent (ST)** = $y_1 \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dy} = \dfrac{y_1}{dy/dx}$
- [ ] **Sub-normal (SN)** = $y_1 \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$
- [ ] **Length of tangent (LT)** = $y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dx}{dy}\right)^2}$
- [ ] **Length of normal (LN)** = $y_1 \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$
- [ ] **Identity check**: $ST \cdot SN = y_1^2$ (use this to verify your answer)
- [ ] **Angle between curves**: $\tan \psi = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$
- [ ] **Orthogonal intersection condition**: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$
- [ ] **Inclination of tangent**: $\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)$
- [ ] **Special cases**:
  - $m = 0$ → horizontal tangent: $y = y_1$
  - $m \to \infty$ → vertical tangent: $x = x_1$
- [ ] **Always verify the point lies on the curve** before computing the slope
- [ ] **Always take absolute value** in the angle formula for the acute angle
- [ ] **For implicit curves**, use implicit differentiation carefully
- [ ] **Engineering link**: Surface normals in CAD = normals of parametric curves; tool path direction in CNC = tangent direction
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blending Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blending Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BLENDING FUNCTIONS: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Computer-Aided Design and Analysis (CAGD)**, a **Blending Function** (also called a **basis function**, **mixing function**, or **weighting function**) is a scalar-valued function $B_i(u)$ that mathematically *blends* or *mixes* the influence of individual **control points** $P_i$ (or control parameters) to generate a continuous geometric entity such as a **curve** or **surface** in a parametric form.

The general matrix-vector form of a parametric curve expressed via blending functions is:

$$C(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i(u) \cdot P_i, \quad u \in [0, 1]$$

where:
- $C(u)$ = Point on the curve at parameter value $u$
- $B_i(u)$ = The $i^{th}$ blending function (a polynomial/trigonometric/rational function of $u$)
- $P_i$ = The $i^{th}$ control point (a coordinate vector)
- $n+1$ = Total number of control points (order of the curve is $n$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Blending functions are the *coefficient functions* of a linear combination of control points. They determine **how much** each control point contributes to the shape of the final curve at any given parameter value $u$. The curve does **NOT** generally pass through every control point — it is *attracted* toward them with weights given by $B_i(u)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMET523 Module 3):** The blending function concept is the **mathematical backbone** of Bezier curves, B-splines, Hermite interpolation, and B-spline surfaces. Mastery of blending function properties is mandatory for Module 3.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "DJ Mixing Console" Intuition

Imagine a **DJ mixing console** with 4 volume sliders (faders), each controlling the loudness of a different musical track (Track A, Track B, Track C, Track D):

| DJ Slider Analogy | Mathematical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The 4 individual tracks (instruments) | The 4 **control points** $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$ |
| The DJ's fader positions at time $t$ | The **blending functions** $B_0(t), B_1(t), B_2(t), B_3(t)$ |
| The final mixed sound reaching the audience | The resulting **curve point** $C(t)$ |
| Time progression of the song | The **parameter** $u \in [0, 1]$ |

At any moment $t$, the DJ pushes some faders up and others down. The **sum of all fader positions equals 1.0** (full volume total = unity gain). What the audience hears is a *blend* of all four tracks, weighted by the fader positions. **Blending functions behave identically**: they are scalar "faders" attached to each control point, and the constraint $\sum B_i(u) = 1$ (the **partition of unity** property) ensures geometric consistency.

A second analogy: **mixing paint colors**. Each control point is a pure pigment; blending functions dictate how much of each pigment goes into the final mixed color at every point along the curve.

---

## 1.3 Why Blending Functions Matter in CAD — The Engineering Motivation

In modern CAD/CAM/CAE systems (CATIA, NX, SolidWorks, ANSYS, AutoCAD), every curved edge, surface patch, and smooth fillet you see is mathematically constructed using blending functions. Without them:

- We could not represent **free-form surfaces** (car body panels, aircraft wings, ship hulls, turbine blades).
- We could not perform **smooth blending** between adjacent surfaces.
- We could not ensure **local control** (editing one control point affecting only a region of the curve).

The **three engineering properties** that every useful blending function family must satisfy are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Partition of Unity:** $\sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i(u) = 1$ for all $u$ — guarantees affine invariance (curve transforms predictably with control points).
> 2. **Non-negativity (Convex Hull Property):** $B_i(u) \geq 0$ for all valid $u$ — guarantees the curve stays inside the convex hull of control points (numerical stability).
> 3. **Local Support / Variation Diminishing:** Only a few $B_i(u)$ are non-zero at any given $u$ — enables local editing and computational efficiency.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Blending Functions (KTU Module 3 Roadmap)

| Class | Example | Polynomial Degree | Locality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polynomial** | Bernstein (Bezier) Basis $B_{i,n}(u)$ | $n$ | Global |
| **Piecewise Polynomial** | B-Spline Basis $N_{i,p}(u)$ | $p$ (piecewise) | Local |
| **Trigonometric** | Fourier basis (rare in CAD) | — | Global |
| **Rational** | NURBS basis $R_{i,p}(u)$ | $p$ (piecewise) | Local |
| **Hermite / Lagrange** | Cardinal spline basis | 3 / $n$ | Global |

The remainder of these notes derives and analyzes these functions in detail.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BLENDING FUNCTIONS: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — How Blending Functions Generate a Curve

The complete operational sequence to construct a curve from blending functions is:

1. **Specify Control Polygon:** Designer supplies an ordered set of $n+1$ control points $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n$ (in 2D or 3D).
2. **Choose Basis Family:** Select blending function family (Bernstein, B-spline, Lagrange, etc.) and degree/order.
3. **Define Parametric Range:** Assign parameter domain — typically normalized to $u \in [0, 1]$ (Bezier/Lagrange) or knot vector $u \in [u_0, u_{n+p+1}]$ (B-spline).
4. **Evaluate Blending Functions:** For each $u$, compute all $B_i(u)$ values — these are scalar weights.
5. **Form Linear Combination:** Compute $C(u) = \sum B_i(u) \cdot P_i$ — geometrically "weights" each control point.
6. **Sweep $u$ Across Domain:** Vary $u$ from start to end; the locus of $C(u)$ traces the curve.
7. **Render / Output:** Sample many $u$ values; connect with straight segments or render smoothly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core "Why":** Blending functions decouple **shape** (controlled by $P_i$) from **parameterization** (controlled by $B_i(u)$). This decoupling is what makes CAD editable — you can change shape without rewriting the math.

---

## 2.2 The Bernstein (Bezier) Blending Functions — Primary KTU Topic

The **$i^{th}$ Bernstein polynomial of degree $n$** is defined as:

$$B_{i,n}(u) = \binom{n}{i} u^{i} (1-u)^{n-i}, \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$$

where $\binom{n}{i} = \dfrac{n!}{i!(n-i)!}$ is the **binomial coefficient**.

**Expanding the three lowest-degree cases** (most frequently tested):

For $n = 1$ (linear Bezier — 2 control points):
$$B_{0,1}(u) = 1 - u, \quad B_{1,1}(u) = u$$

For $n = 2$ (quadratic Bezier — 3 control points):
$$B_{0,2}(u) = (1-u)^2, \quad B_{1,2}(u) = 2u(1-u), \quad B_{2,2}(u) = u^2$$

For $n = 3$ (cubic Bezier — 4 control points):
$$B_{0,3}(u) = (1-u)^3, \quad B_{1,3}(u) = 3u(1-u)^2, \quad B_{2,3}(u) = 3u^2(1-u), \quad B_{3,3}(u) = u^3$$

### 2.2.1 Recurrence Relation (Cox–de Boor style for Bernstein)

Bernstein polynomials satisfy the elegant recurrence:
$$B_{i,n}(u) = (1-u) \cdot B_{i,n-1}(u) + u \cdot B_{i-1,n-1}(u)$$
with the base case $B_{0,0}(u) = 1$ and the convention that $B_{i,n}(u) = 0$ if $i < 0$ or $i > n$.

### 2.2.2 Key Properties (Universally Tested)

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Partition of Unity** | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} B_{i,n}(u) = 1$ | Affine invariance — curve moves with polygon |
| **Non-negativity** | $B_{i,n}(u) \geq 0$ for $u \in [0,1]$ | Convex hull containment — numerical stability |
| **Endpoints** | $B_{0,n}(0) = 1$, $B_{n,n}(1) = 1$ | Curve starts at $P_0$, ends at $P_n$ |
| **Symmetry** | $B_{i,n}(u) = B_{n-i,n}(1-u)$ | Reversing control points reverses the curve |
| **Degree Elevation** | $B_{i,n} = \frac{n+1-i}{n+1} B_{i,n+1} + \frac{i+1}{n+1} B_{i+1,n+1}$ | Convert between degrees without changing shape |
| **Maximum** | $B_{i,n}(u)$ peaks at $u = i/n$ | Each function has a "center of influence" |

---

## 2.3 The Hermite Cubic Blending Functions (Curve Interpolation)

Hermite blending functions allow specifying **both endpoint positions and endpoint tangent vectors**. The curve is:
$$C(u) = h_0(u) P_0 + h_1(u) P_1 + h_2(u) P_0' + h_3(u) P_1'$$

The four Hermite basis functions are:

$$h_0(u) = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1, \quad h_1(u) = -2u^3 + 3u^2$$
$$h_2(u) = u^3 - 2u^2 + u, \quad h_3(u) = u^3 - u^2$$

**Boundary conditions** (the defining property):
- $h_0(0) = 1$, $h_0(1) = 0$, $h_0'(0) = 0$, $h_0'(1) = 0$
- $h_1(0) = 0$, $h_1(1) = 1$, $h_1'(0) = 0$, $h_1'(1) = 0$
- $h_2(0) = 0$, $h_2(1) = 0$, $h_2'(0) = 1$, $h_2'(1) = 0$
- $h_3(0) = 0$, $h_3(1) = 0$, $h_3'(0) = 0$, $h_3'(1) = 1$

---

## 2.4 The B-Spline Blending Functions (Local Support — Industry Standard)

B-spline basis functions $N_{i,p}(u)$ of degree $p$ are defined recursively on a **non-decreasing knot vector** $U = \{u_0, u_1, \ldots, u_{m}\}$ where $m = n + p + 1$.

**Zero-degree (piecewise constant):**
$$N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u_i \leq u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Higher-degree (Cox–de Boor recursion):**
$$N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining feature: at any $u$, **only $p+1$ basis functions are non-zero**, giving B-splines their celebrated **local support** property — moving one control point affects only a small arc of the curve.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | General curve blending | $C(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i(u) P_i$ |
| 2 | Bernstein polynomial | $B_{i,n}(u) = \binom{n}{i} u^{i} (1-u)^{n-i}$ |
| 3 | Bernstein recurrence | $B_{i,n}(u) = (1-u)B_{i,n-1}(u) + u\,B_{i-1,n-1}(u)$ |
| 4 | Partition of Unity | $\sum B_i(u) = 1$ |
| 5 | Convex Hull | $0 \leq B_i(u) \leq 1$ for $u \in [0,1]$ |
| 6 | Hermite $h_0$ | $2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1$ |
| 7 | Hermite $h_1$ | $-2u^3 + 3u^2$ |
| 8 | Hermite $h_2$ | $u^3 - 2u^2 + u$ |
| 9 | Hermite $h_3$ | $u^3 - u^2$ |
| 10 | Cox–de Boor recursion | $N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u-u_i}{u_{i+p}-u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1}-u}{u_{i+p+1}-u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u)$ |
| 11 | Local support span | $N_{i,p}(u)$ non-zero only on $[u_i, u_{i+p+1}]$ |
| 12 | Binomial coefficient | $\binom{n}{i} = \frac{n!}{i!(n-i)!}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exams, students frequently **omit the binomial coefficient** when writing the Bernstein polynomial. This is the single most common mark-losing error. Always include $\binom{n}{i}$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use of Blending Functions |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Design** | Bezier/NURBS surfaces for car body panels (BMW, Tesla use NURBS) |
| **Aircraft Aerodynamics** | Wing surface lofting, fuselage skin design |
| **Ship Hull Design** | Fairing curves for hull lines |
| **Animation / VFX** | Pixar's RenderMan uses subdivision + blending functions |
| **Font Design** | TrueType / PostScript fonts stored as Bezier curves |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Point-cloud fitting with B-spline blending |
| **Robotics Path Planning** | Smooth trajectory blending with cubic splines |
| **Biomedical CAD** | Custom prosthetics and implants (NURBS surfaces) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BLENDING FUNCTIONS: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cubic Bernstein Blending Functions (from scratch)

We seek polynomials $B_{i,3}(u)$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, 3$ such that:
- $C(0) = P_0$ and $C(1) = P_3$ (interpolate endpoints)
- $C(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} B_{i,3}(u) P_i$ is a polynomial in $u$

**Step 1 — Endpoint Constraint (Curve must pass through $P_0$ at $u=0$):**
$$C(0) = B_{0,3}(0) P_0 + B_{1,3}(0) P_1 + B_{2,3}(0) P_2 + B_{3,3}(0) P_3 = P_0$$
This forces $B_{0,3}(0) = 1$ and $B_{1,3}(0) = B_{2,3}(0) = B_{3,3}(0) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Endpoint Constraint (Curve must pass through $P_3$ at $u=1$):**
$$C(1) = B_{0,3}(1) P_0 + B_{1,3}(1) P_1 + B_{2,3}(1) P_2 + B_{3,3}(1) P_3 = P_3$$
This forces $B_{3,3}(1) = 1$ and $B_{0,3}(1) = B_{1,3}(1) = B_{2,3}(1) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Symmetry + Lowest-Degree Choice:**
We choose cubic (degree 3) — the lowest degree that gives an S-shaped curve with enough flexibility. The polynomial has 4 unknown coefficients per $B_{i,3}$, so 4 conditions per function are sufficient.

**Step 4 — Use the Binomial-Theorem Identity:**
Recall: $(u + (1-u))^3 = 1^3 = 1$. Expand:
$$(u + (1-u))^3 = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{3}{i} u^{i}(1-u)^{3-i} = 1$$

By the binomial theorem, define:
$$B_{i,3}(u) = \binom{3}{i} u^{i}(1-u)^{3-i}$$

**Step 5 — Verify All Four Functions:**

$$B_{0,3}(u) = \binom{3}{0} u^{0}(1-u)^{3} = (1-u)^3 = 1 - 3u + 3u^2 - u^3$$

$$B_{1,3}(u) = \binom{3}{1} u^{1}(1-u)^{2} = 3u(1-u)^2 = 3u - 6u^2 + 3u^3$$

$$B_{2,3}(u) = \binom{3}{2} u^{2}(1-u)^{1} = 3u^2(1-u) = 3u^2 - 3u^3$$

$$B_{3,3}(u) = \binom{3}{3} u^{3}(1-u)^{0} = u^3$$

**Step 6 — Verify Partition of Unity (add all four):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=0}^{3} B_{i,3}(u) &= (1 - 3u + 3u^2 - u^3) + (3u - 6u^2 + 3u^3) \\
&\quad + (3u^2 - 3u^3) + u^3 \\
&= 1 + (-3u + 3u) + (3u^2 - 6u^2 + 3u^2) + (-u^3 + 3u^3 - 3u^3 + u^3) \\
&= 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verify Non-negativity:**
For $u \in [0,1]$, all four functions are products of non-negative quantities, hence $\geq 0$. Maximum of $B_{0,3}$ is at $u=0$ (value 1); maximum of $B_{3,3}$ is at $u=1$ (value 1); $B_{1,3}$ peaks at $u = 1/3$ (value $4/9$); $B_{2,3}$ peaks at $u = 2/3$ (value $4/9$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hermite Cubic Blending Functions (from boundary conditions)

We seek four cubic polynomials $h_0, h_1, h_2, h_3$ satisfying eight boundary conditions:

| Function | $h(0)$ | $h(1)$ | $h'(0)$ | $h'(1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h_0$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $h_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $h_2$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $h_3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

**Step 1 — General cubic form:** $h(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$, $h'(u) = a_1 + 2a_2 u + 3a_3 u^2$.

**Step 2 — Derive $h_0(u)$:** Apply the four constraints $h_0(0)=1, h_0(1)=0, h_0'(0)=0, h_0'(1)=0$:

$$h_0(0) = a_0 = 1$$
$$h_0'(0) = a_1 = 0$$
$$h_0(1) = a_0 + a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + 0 + a_2 + a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 + a_3 = -1$$
$$h_0'(1) = a_1 + 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow 0 + 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0$$

Solving: $a_3 = 2$, $a_2 = -3$. Thus:
$$\boxed{h_0(u) = 1 - 3u^2 + 2u^3 = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Derive $h_1(u)$:** Apply $h_1(0)=0, h_1(1)=1, h_1'(0)=0, h_1'(1)=0$:

$$a_0 = 0, \quad a_1 = 0$$
$$a_2 + a_3 = 1, \quad 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -3, a_3 = 2$$
$$\boxed{h_1(u) = -3u^2 + 2u^3 = -2u^3 + 3u^2}$$

**Step 4 — Derive $h_2(u)$:** Apply $h_2(0)=0, h_2(1)=0, h_2'(0)=1, h_2'(1)=0$:

$$a_0 = 0, \quad a_1 = 1$$
$$a_2 + a_3 = -1, \quad 2 + 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow 2a_2 + 3a_3 = -2$$

Solving: $a_3 = 0, a_2 = -1$. Thus:
$$\boxed{h_2(u) = u - u^2 = u^3 - 2u^2 + u}$$

**Step 5 — Derive $h_3(u)$:** Apply $h_3(0)=0, h_3(1)=0, h_3'(0)=0, h_3'(1)=1$:

$$a_0 = 0, \quad a_1 = 0$$
$$a_2 + a_3 = 0, \quad 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 1 \Rightarrow a_2 = -1, a_3 = 1$$
$$\boxed{h_3(u) = -u^2 + u^3 = u^3 - u^2}$$

**Step 6 — Verify Partition of Unity:**
$$\begin{aligned}
h_0 + h_1 &= (2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1) + (-2u^3 + 3u^2) = 1 \\
h_2 + h_3 &= (u^3 - 2u^2 + u) + (u^3 - u^2) = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u
\end{aligned}$$

Total: $1 + 2u^3 - 3u^2 + u \neq 1$ in general. **Hermite functions do NOT satisfy partition of unity as a set** — they are not blending functions in the strict convex-combination sense; they are *interpolation* functions. This is a subtle but frequently tested distinction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Evaluating a Cubic Bezier Curve

**Given:** Control points $P_0 = (0, 0)$, $P_1 = (1, 2)$, $P_2 = (3, 2)$, $P_3 = (4, 0)$. Find $C(0.5)$.

**Step 1 — Evaluate Bernstein polynomials at $u = 0.5$:**

$$B_{0,3}(0.5) = (1-0.5)^3 = 0.125$$

$$B_{1,3}(0.5) = 3(0.5)(0.5)^2 = 0.375$$

$$B_{2,3}(0.5) = 3(0.5)^2(0.5) = 0.375$$

$$B_{3,3}(0.5) = (0.5)^3 = 0.125$$

**Step 2 — Check sum:** $0.125 + 0.375 + 0.375 + 0.125 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute x-coordinate of curve point:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_x(0.5) &= 0.125(0) + 0.375(1) + 0.375(3) + 0.125(4) \\
&= 0 + 0.375 + 1.125 + 0.500 \\
&= 2.000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute y-coordinate of curve point:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_y(0.5) &= 0.125(0) + 0.375(2) + 0.375(2) + 0.125(0) \\
&= 0 + 0.750 + 0.750 + 0 \\
&= 1.500
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $C(0.5) = (2.0, 1.5)$.

**Step 5 — Tangent at $u = 0.5$ (for additional practice):**
$$C'(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} B'_{i,3}(u) P_i$$
where $B'_{i,n}(u) = n[B_{i-1,n-1}(u) - B_{i,n-1}(u)]$. The slope at $u = 0.5$ is $C'_y(0.5)/C'_x(0.5) = 0/3 = 0$ — the curve is horizontal at the midpoint (by symmetry of this control polygon).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Quadratic B-Spline Blending Functions (Local Support)

**Given:** Uniform knot vector $U = \{0, 0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 3, 3\}$, $p = 2$ (quadratic), so $m = 7$, number of basis functions $n = m - p - 1 = 4$.

**Step 1 — Zero-degree (p=0) basis:**
$$N_{0,0}(u) = 1 \text{ for } u \in [0,1), \quad 0 \text{ otherwise}$$
$$N_{1,0}(u) = 1 \text{ for } u \in [1,2), \quad 0 \text{ otherwise}$$
$$N_{2,0}(u) = 1 \text{ for } u \in [2,3), \quad 0 \text{ otherwise}$$

**Step 2 — Degree-1 basis using recursion with $p=0 \to p=1$:**

$$N_{0,1}(u) = \frac{u - 0}{0 - 0} N_{0,0}(u) + \frac{1 - u}{1 - 0} N_{1,0}(u)$$

The first term is **0/0 — undefined** (treat as 0 when denominator is 0). So $N_{0,1}(u) = 1 - u$ for $u \in [0, 1]$, else 0.

$$N_{1,1}(u) = \frac{u - 0}{1 - 0} N_{0,0}(u) + \frac{2 - u}{2 - 1} N_{1,0}(u)$$
$$= u \cdot 1 + (2-u) \cdot 1 \text{ on } [0,2) \text{ (combined)}$$

For $u \in [0,1)$: $N_{1,1} = u$.
For $u \in [1,2)$: $N_{1,1} = (2-u)$.

$$N_{2,1}(u) = \frac{u-1}{2-1} N_{1,0}(u) + \frac{3-u}{3-2} N_{2,0}(u)$$
$$= (u-1) \text{ on } [1,2), \quad 1 \text{ on } [2,3)$$

**Step 3 — Degree-2 basis using recursion with $p=1 \to p=2$:**

$$N_{0,2}(u) = \frac{u-0}{0-0} N_{0,1}(u) + \frac{1-u}{1-0} N_{1,1}(u) = (1-u)(1-u) = (1-u)^2$$
for $u \in [0, 1]$, else 0.

$$N_{1,2}(u) = \frac{u-0}{1-0} N_{0,1}(u) + \frac{2-u}{2-1} N_{1,1}(u)$$

For $u \in [0,1)$: $N_{1,2} = u(1-u) + (2-u)(u) = u(1-u) + u(2-u) = u(3 - 2u)$.
For $u \in [1,2)$: $N_{1,2} = (2-u)(2-u) = (2-u)^2$.

$$N_{2,2}(u) = \frac{u-1}{2-1} N_{1,1}(u) + \frac{3-u}{3-2} N_{2,1}(u)$$

For $u \in [1,2)$: $N_{2,2} = (u-1)(2-u) + (3-u)(u-1) = (u-1)(2-u+3-u) = (u-1)(5-2u)$.
For $u \in [2,3)$: $N_{2,2} = (3-u)^2$.

**Final Quadratic B-Spline Basis (summary):**
$$N_{0,2}(u) = \begin{cases} (1-u)^2 & 0 \leq u \leq 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
$$N_{1,2}(u) = \begin{cases} u(3-2u) & 0 \leq u < 1 \\ (2-u)^2 & 1 \leq u < 2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
$$N_{2,2}(u) = \begin{cases} (u-1)(5-2u) & 1 \leq u < 2 \\ (3-u)^2 & 2 \leq u < 3 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

These functions are **non-zero over only 3 consecutive knot spans** — demonstrating local support.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# MODULE 3 — BLENDING FUNCTIONS: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — The Role of Blending Functions in CAD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Designer Specifies Control Points P0 P1 P2 P3] --> B[Choose Basis Family Bernstein Bspline Hermite]
    B --> C[Define Parametric Domain u in 0 1]
    C --> D[Evaluate Blending Functions B0 u B1 u B2 u B3 u]
    D --> E[Form Linear Combination Cu equals Sum Bi u Pi]
    E --> F[Sweep u Across Domain]
    F --> G[Output Parametric Curve]
    G --> H[Render Display in CAD Software]
    H --> I[Apply to Surface Lofting CAM Toolpath]
    D --> D1[Verify Partition of Unity equals 1]
    D --> D2[Verify Non Negativity greater or equal 0]
    D --> D3[Check Local Support]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Three Major Blending Function Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blending Function Families] --> B[Bernstein Bezier]
    A --> C[Hermite Cubic]
    A --> D[BSpline NURBS]
    B --> B1[Degree n]
    B --> B2[Global Support]
    B --> B3[End Point Interpolation]
    B --> B4[Convex Hull Property]
    C --> C1[Degree 3 only]
    C --> C2[Global Support]
    C --> C3[Tangent Control at Ends]
    C --> C4[No Convex Hull Guarantee]
    D --> D1[Piecewise Degree p]
    D --> D2[Local Support on Knot Spans]
    D --> D3[No End Point Interpolation]
    D --> D4[Strong Convex Hull Property]
    D --> D5[Industry Standard NURBS]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cubic Bezier Evaluator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input Parameter u] --> EVAL[Evaluate Bernstein Polynomials]
    P0[Control Point P0] --> MIX[Linear Combiner]
    P1[Control Point P1] --> MIX
    P2[Control Point P2] --> MIX
    P3[Control Point P3] --> MIX
    EVAL --> B0F[B0 equals 1 minus u cubed]
    EVAL --> B1F[B1 equals 3u times 1 minus u squared]
    EVAL --> B2F[B2 equals 3u squared times 1 minus u]
    EVAL --> B3F[B3 equals u cubed]
    B0F --> MIX
    B1F --> MIX
    B2F --> MIX
    B3F --> MIX
    MIX --> OUTPUT[Output Curve Point C of u]
    OUTPUT --> NEXT[Next Iteration with u plus du]
    NEXT --> EVAL
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Degree Elevation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cubic Bezier n equals 3] --> B[Apply Degree Elevation Formula]
    B --> C[Quartic Bezier n equals 4 with 5 Control Points]
    C --> D[Apply Again]
    D --> E[Quintic Bezier n equals 5 with 6 Control Points]
    E --> F[Limit: Approaches B-Spline Basis]
    F --> G[Shape Preserved Exactly Throughout]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description of Cubic Bernstein Blending Functions (Graphical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Schematic (described for sketch):** Plot the four cubic Bernstein blending functions on the interval $u \in [0, 1]$:
> - $B_{0,3}(u) = (1-u)^3$: **monotonically decreasing** from $(0, 1)$ to $(1, 0)$, with inflection.
> - $B_{1,3}(u) = 3u(1-u)^2$: **rises from 0, peaks at $u = 1/3$ (value $4/9 \approx 0.444$), falls to 0 at $u = 1$**.
> - $B_{2,3}(u) = 3u^2(1-u)$: **rises from 0, peaks at $u = 2/3$ (value $4/9$), falls to 0 at $u = 1$**.
> - $B_{3,3}(u) = u^3$: **monotonically increasing** from $(0, 0)$ to $(1, 1)$, with inflection.
> - The **sum of all four curves is identically 1** at every $u$ — partition of unity visualized as a horizontal line at height 1.

# MODULE 3 — BLENDING FUNCTIONS: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define blending functions. State any three properties that blending functions must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **blending function** $B_i(u)$ is a scalar weight function that linearly combines control points $P_i$ to define a parametric curve $C(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i(u) P_i$. It determines *how much* each control point influences the curve at any parameter $u$.

**Three essential properties:**
1. **Partition of Unity:** $\sum_{i=0}^{n} B_i(u) = 1$ for all valid $u$.
2. **Non-negativity:** $B_i(u) \geq 0$ over the parameter domain.
3. **End-point interpolation (for Bezier):** $B_{0,n}(0) = 1$ and $B_{n,n}(1) = 1$, ensuring the curve starts at $P_0$ and ends at $P_n$.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Write the cubic Bernstein blending functions. Verify that they satisfy the partition of unity property.**

**Model Answer:**

The four cubic Bernstein blending functions for $n = 3$ are:

$$B_{0,3}(u) = (1-u)^3, \quad B_{1,3}(u) = 3u(1-u)^2$$
$$B_{2,3}(u) = 3u^2(1-u), \quad B_{3,3}(u) = u^3$$

**Verification of partition of unity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=0}^{3} B_{i,3}(u) &= (1-u)^3 + 3u(1-u)^2 + 3u^2(1-u) + u^3 \\
&= (1-u)^2[(1-u) + 3u] + u^2[3(1-u) + u] \\
&= (1-u)^2(1 + 2u) + u^2(3 - 2u) \\
&= (1 - 2u + u^2)(1+2u) + 3u^2 - 2u^3 \\
&= 1 + 2u - 2u - 4u^2 + u^2 + 2u^3 + 3u^2 - 2u^3 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}$$

Hence partition of unity is satisfied. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### **Module 3 Internal Choice — Full-Length ESE Questions**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the four cubic Bernstein blending functions starting from the binomial theorem identity $(u + (1-u))^n = 1$. Verify that they satisfy the partition of unity property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given control points $P_0 = (0, 0)$, $P_1 = (2, 4)$, $P_2 = (6, 4)$, $P_3 = (8, 0)$, compute the curve point $C(0.25)$ using cubic Bezier blending. Show the Bernstein polynomial values explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Binomial identity:** For $u + (1-u) = 1$, raising to the $n^{th}$ power and using the binomial theorem:

$$(u + (1-u))^n = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \binom{n}{i} u^{i} (1-u)^{n-i} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Define Bernstein polynomials** as the terms of this sum:

$$B_{i,n}(u) = \binom{n}{i} u^{i}(1-u)^{n-i}$$

**Step 3 — Specialize to $n = 3$:**

$$B_{0,3}(u) = \binom{3}{0} u^0 (1-u)^3 = (1-u)^3$$
$$B_{1,3}(u) = \binom{3}{1} u^1 (1-u)^2 = 3u(1-u)^2$$
$$B_{2,3}(u) = \binom{3}{2} u^2 (1-u)^1 = 3u^2(1-u)$$
$$B_{3,3}(u) = \binom{3}{3} u^3 (1-u)^0 = u^3$$

**Step 4 — Verify partition of unity** (algebraic sum shown in Question 2 above) = 1. ✓ **(7 marks complete)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate Bernstein polynomials at $u = 0.25$:**

$$B_{0,3}(0.25) = (1 - 0.25)^3 = (0.75)^3 = 0.421875$$
$$B_{1,3}(0.25) = 3(0.25)(0.75)^2 = 0.75 \times 0.5625 = 0.421875$$
$$B_{2,3}(0.25) = 3(0.25)^2(0.75) = 3 \times 0.0625 \times 0.75 = 0.140625$$
$$B_{3,3}(0.25) = (0.25)^3 = 0.015625$$

**Step 2 — Check sum:** $0.421875 + 0.421875 + 0.140625 + 0.015625 = 1.000000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute x-coordinate:**

$$C_x(0.25) = 0.421875(0) + 0.421875(2) + 0.140625(6) + 0.015625(8)$$
$$= 0 + 0.84375 + 0.84375 + 0.125 = 1.8125$$

**Step 4 — Compute y-coordinate:**

$$C_y(0.25) = 0.421875(0) + 0.421875(4) + 0.140625(4) + 0.015625(0)$$
$$= 0 + 1.6875 + 0.5625 + 0 = 2.25$$

**Final Answer:** $C(0.25) = (1.8125, \, 2.25)$ **(7 marks complete)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternate Module Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the four Hermite cubic blending functions $h_0(u), h_1(u), h_2(u), h_3(u)$ from the eight boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cubic Hermite curve is to pass through $P_0 = (1, 1)$ and $P_1 = (5, 3)$ with tangent vectors $P_0' = (2, 4)$ and $P_1' = (4, 2)$. Compute $C(0.5)$ and state whether Hermite blending functions form a convex combination (i.e., satisfy partition of unity). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The four Hermite cubic blending functions must satisfy the eight boundary conditions:

| Function | $h(0)$ | $h(1)$ | $h'(0)$ | $h'(1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h_0$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $h_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $h_2$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $h_3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

For a general cubic $h(u) = a_0 + a_1 u + a_2 u^2 + a_3 u^3$ with $h'(u) = a_1 + 2a_2 u + 3a_3 u^2$:

**Deriving $h_0$:** $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 + a_3 = -1$, $2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -3, a_3 = 2$. $\Rightarrow h_0 = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1$.

**Deriving $h_1$:** $a_0 = 0, a_1 = 0, a_2 + a_3 = 1, 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -3, a_3 = 2$. $\Rightarrow h_1 = -2u^3 + 3u^2$.

**Deriving $h_2$:** $a_0 = 0, a_1 = 1, a_2 + a_3 = -1, 2a_2 + 3a_3 = -2 \Rightarrow a_2 = -1, a_3 = 0$. $\Rightarrow h_2 = u^3 - 2u^2 + u$.

**Deriving $h_3$:** $a_0 = 0, a_1 = 0, a_2 + a_3 = 0, 2a_2 + 3a_3 = 1 \Rightarrow a_2 = -1, a_3 = 1$. $\Rightarrow h_3 = u^3 - u^2$. **(7 marks complete)**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate Hermite blending functions at $u = 0.5$:**

$$h_0(0.5) = 2(0.125) - 3(0.25) + 1 = 0.25 - 0.75 + 1 = 0.5$$
$$h_1(0.5) = -2(0.125) + 3(0.25) = -0.25 + 0.75 = 0.5$$
$$h_2(0.5) = 0.125 - 2(0.25) + 0.5 = 0.125 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 0.125$$
$$h_3(0.5) = 0.125 - 0.25 = -0.125$$

**Step 2 — Check partition of unity:**
$$0.5 + 0.5 + 0.125 - 0.125 = 1.0 \text{ at } u = 0.5$$
But at $u = 0.25$ for example: $h_2 + h_3 = 0.25^3 - 2(0.25)^2 + 0.25 + 0.25^3 - 0.25^2 = 0.109 \neq 0$. So in general, **Hermite functions do NOT satisfy strict partition of unity** — they are interpolation functions, not convex-combination blending functions. The curve can lie outside the convex hull.

**Step 3 — Compute x-coordinate of $C(0.5)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_x(0.5) &= 0.5(1) + 0.5(5) + 0.125(2) + (-0.125)(4) \\
&= 0.5 + 2.5 + 0.25 - 0.5 \\
&= 2.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute y-coordinate of $C(0.5)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_y(0.5) &= 0.5(1) + 0.5(3) + 0.125(4) + (-0.125)(2) \\
&= 0.5 + 1.5 + 0.5 - 0.25 \\
&= 2.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $C(0.5) = (2.75, 2.25)$. The Hermite blending functions are **not convex combinations**; they can take negative values, so the curve may overshoot the convex hull of the boundary data. **(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in Blending Function Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{i}$** — students often write $B_{i,n}(u) = u^i(1-u)^{n-i}$ instead of $\binom{n}{i} u^i(1-u)^{n-i}$. This loses **2 marks** immediately.
>
> 2. **Confusing Hermite with Bezier boundary conditions** — Hermite fixes *tangents* at endpoints; Bezier fixes *control points* at endpoints. Mixing them up loses **3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Assuming all blending functions satisfy partition of unity** — Hermite functions do **NOT** form a convex combination. This is a high-yield conceptual trap.
>
> 4. **Not checking that sum equals 1** in the verification step — examiners specifically look for this 1–2 line verification. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to normalize the parametric domain** — for B-splines, students must explicitly state the knot vector; for Bezier/Hermite, $u \in [0,1]$ must be stated.
>
> 6. **Sign error in Hermite $h_2$ and $h_3$** — these are easily confused ($h_2 = u^3 - 2u^2 + u$ vs. $h_3 = u^3 - u^2$). Verify by checking $h_2'(0) = 1$ and $h_3'(1) = 1$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — BLENDING FUNCTIONS**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** $B_i(u)$ is the weight function multiplying control point $P_i$ in the curve sum $C(u) = \sum B_i(u) P_i$.
>
> ✅ **Three Mandatory Properties:** (1) Partition of Unity, (2) Non-negativity (for convex-hull blending), (3) Smoothness (typically $C^1$ or $C^2$).
>
> ✅ **Bernstein Polynomial Formula:** $B_{i,n}(u) = \binom{n}{i} u^{i}(1-u)^{n-i}$ — ALWAYS include the binomial coefficient.
>
> ✅ **Endpoint Behavior (Bezier):** $C(0) = P_0$ and $C(1) = P_n$ always; intermediate control points are NOT interpolated.
>
> ✅ **Hermite Cubic Set:** $h_0 = 2u^3 - 3u^2 + 1$, $h_1 = -2u^3 + 3u^2$, $h_2 = u^3 - 2u^2 + u$, $h_3 = u^3 - u^2$.
>
> ✅ **Hermite vs. Bezier:** Hermite interpolates endpoints + tangents; Bezier interpolates endpoints only. Hermite functions CAN be negative — NOT a convex combination.
>
> ✅ **B-Spline Cox–de Boor Recursion:** $N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u-u_i}{u_{i+p}-u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1}-u}{u_{i+p+1}-u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u)$.
>
> ✅ **Local Support Property:** A B-spline basis $N_{i,p}(u)$ is non-zero only over the interval $[u_i, u_{i+p+1}]$ — this is the key engineering advantage.
>
> ✅ **Binomial Theorem Connection:** Bernstein basis is the binomial expansion of $(u + (1-u))^n = 1$ — this is why they sum to 1.
>
> ✅ **Real-world use:** NURBS (the industry standard for CAD/CAM) is built on B-spline blending functions. Mastery here = foundation for surface modeling.
>
> ✅ **Recurrence shortcut:** Bernstein polynomials obey $B_{i,n}(u) = (1-u)B_{i,n-1}(u) + uB_{i-1,n-1}(u)$ — useful for higher-degree computation.
>
> ✅ **Degree Elevation:** You can convert a cubic Bezier to a quartic (and beyond) without changing the shape — used in CAD to match curve degrees.
>
> ✅ **Standard Symmetry:** $B_{i,n}(u) = B_{n-i,n}(1-u)$ — reversing the control polygon reverses the curve.
>
> ✅ **Peak Locations:** $B_{i,n}(u)$ achieves maximum value $\binom{n}{i}(i/n)^i((n-i)/n)^{n-i}$ at $u = i/n$.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **straight line** is the shortest distance between two points in Euclidean space, possessing constant direction and zero curvature. In the context of **Computer Aided Design and Analysis (CAD)**, a straight line is the most fundamental geometric primitive — a one-dimensional entity that serves as the building block for complex wireframe, surface, and solid models.

Mathematically, a straight line in a two-dimensional Cartesian plane is defined as a locus of points $(x, y)$ satisfying a first-degree linear equation:

$$ax + by + c = 0$$

where $a$, $b$, and $c$ are real constants, with the condition that $a$ and $b$ are not simultaneously zero ($a^2 + b^2 \neq 0$). The geometric significance of this general form lies in the fact that every term is of **first degree** (no $x^2$, $xy$, or $y^2$ terms), which is what guarantees the resulting curve is a straight line and not a conic section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In CAD, a straight line is the foundational entity used in:
> - **DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) Algorithm** for rasterization
> - **Bresenham's Line Algorithm** for efficient pixel plotting
> - **Parametric Line Representation** for vector graphics
> - **Clipping Operations** (Cohen-Sutherland against rectangular windows)
> - **Geometric Transformations** (translation, rotation, scaling of line segments)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

Imagine a perfectly taut, infinite string stretched between two pins on a flat floor. That string is the **physical embodiment** of a straight line. Now, think of the floor as your $xy$-plane (the Cartesian coordinate system), and the two pins as two known points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$.

Every single point you can touch on that taut string satisfies a **single linear rule** — a relationship of the form $y = mx + c$ where $m$ is the "steepness" (slope) and $c$ is where the string crosses the vertical axis. No matter which tiny segment of the string you examine, it always points in exactly the same direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Translation:** A straight line is the simplest curve — it never bends, never curves, and never doubles back on itself. It is uniquely defined by just **two pieces of information** (e.g., two points, or one point and a slope, or a slope and a y-intercept). This minimal information requirement is precisely why it is the **first primitive** taught in any CAD/CAGD curriculum.

## 1.3 The Slope — The Line's Personality

The **slope** (often denoted $m$) is the single most important property of a line in 2D space. It quantifies how steep the line is and in which direction it ascends or descends.

$$m = \tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle the line makes with the **positive $x$-axis**, measured counter-clockwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Metric:** A line's slope in CAD is directly tied to:
> - **Direction vectors** for parametric forms
> - **Normal vectors** for surface definitions
> - **Angle calculations** for joins, miters, and fillets
> - Standard reference: **horizontal lines have $m = 0$**; **vertical lines have undefined $m$** (infinite slope).

## 1.4 Why Straight Lines Matter in Computer-Aided Design

In CAD, virtually every complex shape — from the fuselage of an aircraft to the silhouette of a smartphone — begins as a network of straight-line segments (a **polygon** or **polyline**) before being refined into smooth curves via spline interpolation. Understanding the mathematics of straight lines is therefore the **gateway** to understanding:

| CAD Application | Role of Straight Line Mathematics |
|----------------|-----------------------------------|
| **2D Drafting (AutoCAD, DraftSight)** | Core entity for drawings |
| **3D Modeling (SolidWorks, CATIA)** | Edge definition, skeleton curves |
| **CNC Machining** | Tool path linear interpolation (G01) |
| **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** | Mesh edges, linear element formulations |
| **Computer Graphics** | Rasterization, anti-aliasing, clipping |
| **Robotics** | Path planning between waypoints |
