# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks

Before exploring the various forms of line equations, we must establish three foundational concepts:

### 2.1.1 Slope of a Line
For any two distinct points $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ on a line:

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}, \quad \text{provided } x_1 \neq x_2$$

### 2.1.2 Angle of Inclination
The angle $\theta$ that a line makes with the positive $x$-axis (measured counter-clockwise, $0 \le \theta < \pi$):

$$\theta = \arctan(m) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\right)$$

### 2.1.3 Intercepts
- **$x$-intercept $(a, 0)$:** Where the line crosses the $x$-axis ($y = 0$)
- **$y$-intercept $(0, b)$:** Where the line crosses the $y$-axis ($x = 0$)

## 2.2 The Five Standard Forms of a Line

A straight line can be expressed in **five** mathematically equivalent forms, each suited for a different problem type. Mastering all five is non-negotiable for KTU examinations.

### Form 1: General Form
$$ax + by + c = 0$$
- **Most general** representation
- Slope: $m = -\frac{a}{b}$, $y$-intercept: $-\frac{c}{b}$

### Form 2: Slope-Intercept Form
$$y = mx + c$$
- Easiest for **graphing** and identifying slope at a glance
- $m$ = slope, $c$ = $y$-intercept

### Form 3: Point-Slope Form
$$y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$$
- Best when **one point and slope are known**

### Form 4: Two-Point Form
$$\frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}, \quad x_1 \neq x_2, \ y_1 \neq y_2$$
- Best when **two points are known**

### Form 5: Intercept Form
$$\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1$$
- $a$ = $x$-intercept, $b$ = $y$-intercept (both must be non-zero)

### Form 6: Normal Form
$$x \cos\alpha + y \sin\alpha = p$$
- $p$ = perpendicular distance from origin to the line ($p \geq 0$)
- $\alpha$ = angle made by the normal (perpendicular from origin) with positive $x$-axis
- Most useful for **distance calculations**

## 2.3 Special Cases and Their CAD Relevance

| Line Type | Equation | Slope $m$ | CAD Use Case |
|-----------|----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Horizontal line** | $y = c$ | $0$ | Datum lines, baselines for dimensions |
| **Vertical line** | $x = a$ | Undefined ($\infty$) | Reference axes, side walls |
| **Line through origin** | $y = mx$ | $m$ | Centerline of symmetric parts |
| **Line at $45°$** | $y = \pm x$ | $\pm 1$ | Isometric drafting axes |
| **Parallel to $x$-axis** | $y = c$ | $0$ | Grid lines, ruled surfaces |
| **Parallel to $y$-axis** | $x = a$ | Undefined | Section view indicators |

## 2.4 ⭐ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day arsenal**. Every formula here has appeared (or will appear) in KTU question papers. Memorize the boxed conditions — they are the most common sources of mark loss.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Slope between two points** | $m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | $x_1 \neq x_2$ |
| 2 | **Slope-Intercept form** | $y = mx + c$ | Standard graphing form |
| 3 | **Point-Slope form** | $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ | One known point |
| 4 | **Two-Point form** | $\dfrac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | Two known points |
| 5 | **Intercept form** | $\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1$ | $a \neq 0$, $b \neq 0$ |
| 6 | **Normal form** | $x \cos\alpha + y \sin\alpha = p$ | $p \geq 0$, $0 \leq \alpha < 2\pi$ |
| 7 | **General form** | $ax + by + c = 0$ | $a^2 + b^2 \neq 0$ |
| 8 | **Angle of inclination** | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(m)$ | $0 \leq \theta < \pi$ |
| 9 | **Parallel lines** | $m_1 = m_2$ | Same slope, different intercept |
| 10 | **Perpendicular lines** | $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ | Product of slopes = $-1$ |
| 11 | **Angle between two lines** | $\tan\phi = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$ | $1 + m_1 m_2 \neq 0$ |
| 12 | **Distance: point to line** | $d = \dfrac{\vert ax_1 + by_1 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | General form prerequisite |
| 13 | **Distance: point to line (normal form)** | $d = \vert x_1 \cos\alpha + y_1 \sin\alpha - p \vert$ | Normal form prerequisite |
| 14 | **Distance between parallel lines** | $d = \dfrac{\vert c_1 - c_2 \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Lines: $ax + by + c_1 = 0$, $ax + by + c_2 = 0$ |
| 15 | **Parametric form (2D)** | $x = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1)$, $y = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1)$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 16 | **Parametric form (3D)** | $x = x_1 + at$, $y = y_1 + bt$, $z = z_1 + ct$ | Direction ratios $(a, b, c)$ |
| 17 | **Vector form** | $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda \vec{b}$ | $\vec{a}$ = position, $\vec{b}$ = direction |
| 18 | **Foot of perpendicular from point to line** | Solved via projection formula | See derivation in Section 3 |
| 19 | **Image of point in a line** | Mirror reflection formula | See derivation in Section 3 |
| 20 | **Angle bisectors of two lines** | $\dfrac{a_1 x + b_1 y + c_1}{\sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2}} = \pm \dfrac{a_2 x + b_2 y + c_2}{\sqrt{a_2^2 + b_2^2}}$ | $+$ for acute, $-$ for obtuse |

## 2.5 Geometric Significance of Key Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in real engineering:**
> - The **distance from a point to a line** (#12) is used in FEA to compute the shortest distance from a load application point to a structural member's neutral axis.
> - The **angle between two lines** (#11) is critical in robotics for computing joint angles and in sheet-metal design for fold-line calculations.
> - **Parametric form** (#15) is the **lingua franca** of CAD — every modern CAD kernel (ACIS, Parasolid, OpenCASCADE) internally represents lines parametrically because it allows seamless extension to 3D space and curves.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slope of a Line from Two Points

**Given:** Two distinct points $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ on a line $L$.

**To derive:** The slope $m$ of the line.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:** Consider any third point $P(x, y)$ lying on the same line $L$ between (or beyond) $P_1$ and $P_2$. Since all three points are collinear, the ratio of vertical change to horizontal change must be the same for any pair.

**Step 2 — Apply the Ratio Equivalence:**

$$\frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

**Step 3 — Define the slope as this constant ratio:**

$$m \triangleq \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the slope is:**

$$\boxed{m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The slope is **direction-invariant** — it doesn't matter which two points you pick on the line; the ratio is always the same. This is the mathematical expression of the line's "straightness."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: All Five Standard Forms from First Principles

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Slope-Intercept Form from Two Points

**Given:** Two points $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$.

**Step 1 — Start with the two-point relationship:**

$$\frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = m$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $(x - x_1)$:**

$$y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$y - y_1 = mx - m x_1$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $y$:**

$$y = mx - mx_1 + y_1$$

**Step 5 — Define the constant $c = y_1 - mx_1$:**

$$y = mx + c \quad \text{where } c = y_1 - m x_1$$

$$\boxed{y = mx + c}$$

**Verification:** Setting $x = 0$ gives $y = c$, confirming $c$ is the $y$-intercept. ✓

---

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Intercept Form

**Given:** A line cuts the $x$-axis at $(a, 0)$ and the $y$-axis at $(0, b)$, where $a \neq 0$ and $b \neq 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply the two-point form with $P_1 = (a, 0)$ and $P_2 = (0, b)$:**

$$\frac{y - 0}{x - a} = \frac{b - 0}{0 - a}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{y}{x - a} = \frac{b}{-a} = -\frac{b}{a}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$ay = -b(x - a)$$

**Step 4 — Expand:**

$$ay = -bx + ab$$

**Step 5 — Divide throughout by $ab$:**

$$\frac{ay}{ab} = \frac{-bx}{ab} + \frac{ab}{ab}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1}$$

---

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Normal Form

**Given:** A line $L$ at perpendicular distance $p$ from the origin $O$, with the perpendicular from $O$ to $L$ making an angle $\alpha$ with the positive $x$-axis.

**Step 1 — The foot of the perpendicular from $O$ onto $L$ is the point $N$:**

$$N = (p\cos\alpha, \ p\sin\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Any point $P(x, y)$ on the line $L$ satisfies the condition that $ON \perp LP$.**

**Step 3 — Vector $ON$ = $(p\cos\alpha, p\sin\alpha)$. Vector $NP$ = $(x - p\cos\alpha, y - p\sin\alpha)$.**

**Step 4 — Perpendicularity condition via dot product = 0:**

$$ON \cdot NP = 0$$

$$(p\cos\alpha)(x - p\cos\alpha) + (p\sin\alpha)(y - p\sin\alpha) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand:**

$$px\cos\alpha - p^2\cos^2\alpha + py\sin\alpha - p^2\sin^2\alpha = 0$$

**Step 6 — Factor and use $\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha = 1$:**

$$p(x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha) - p^2(\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha) = 0$$

$$p(x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha) - p^2 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Divide by $p$ (since $p > 0$):**

$$\boxed{x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha = p}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The normal form is invaluable because the constant $p$ **directly gives the perpendicular distance** from the origin to the line — no computation needed. This is the form used in ray-casting, shadow projection, and offset curve calculations in CAD.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Angle Between Two Lines

**Given:** Two lines $L_1$ and $L_2$ with slopes $m_1$ and $m_2$ (with $m_1 \neq m_2$).

**Step 1 — Let $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ be the angles of inclination of $L_1$ and $L_2$ with the positive $x$-axis:**

$$\tan\theta_1 = m_1, \quad \tan\theta_2 = m_2$$

**Step 2 — The acute angle $\phi$ between the lines is:**

$$\phi = \theta_1 - \theta_2 \quad \text{(or its supplement)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the tangent subtraction formula:**

$$\tan(\theta_1 - \theta_2) = \frac{\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2}{1 + \tan\theta_1\tan\theta_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $m_1$ and $m_2$:**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}$$

**Step 5 — Take absolute value to ensure $\phi$ is the acute angle (since angle between lines is conventionally $\leq 90°$):**

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert, \quad 1 + m_1 m_2 \neq 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case:** If $1 + m_1 m_2 = 0$, the lines are **perpendicular**, and $\phi = 90°$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Distance from a Point to a Line

**Given:** A line $L: ax + by + c = 0$ and a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ not on $L$.

**Step 1 — Find any two points on $L$, say $A(x_2, y_2)$ and $B(x_3, y_3)$, satisfying $ax + by + c = 0$.**

**Step 2 — The distance from $P$ to $L$ equals the area of triangle $PAB$ divided by the base $\vert AB \vert$:**

$$d = \frac{\text{Area}(\triangle PAB)}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \vert AB \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Area}}{\vert AB \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Area of triangle using coordinates:**

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right\vert$$

**Step 4 — Length of $AB$:**

$$\vert AB \vert = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_3)^2 + (y_2 - y_3)^2}$$

**Step 5 — After extensive algebraic manipulation (shown compactly), we arrive at the elegant closed form:**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{\vert ax_1 + by_1 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}}$$

**Verification by example:** For line $3x + 4y - 10 = 0$ and point $(1, 1)$:

$$d = \frac{\vert 3(1) + 4(1) - 10 \vert}{\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}} = \frac{\vert -3 \vert}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \text{ units} \ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Foot of Perpendicular from Point to Line

**Given:** Line $L: ax + by + c = 0$ and external point $P(x_1, y_1)$.

**Step 1 — The foot of perpendicular $Q(h, k)$ lies on $L$, so:**

$$ah + bk + c = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — The line $PQ$ is perpendicular to $L$, so its slope is $\frac{b}{a}$ (the negative reciprocal of $-\frac{a}{b}$):**

$$\frac{k - y_1}{h - x_1} = \frac{b}{a}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$a(k - y_1) = b(h - x_1)$$

$$ak - ay_1 = bh - bx_1$$

$$bh - ak = bx_1 - ay_1 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve equations (i) and (ii) simultaneously for $h$ and $k$.**

**Step 5 — Multiplying (i) by $b$ and (ii) by $a$, and adding:**

$$ab(h+k) + b^2 k + ... $$

*(Detailed algebra follows; the final result is presented below.)*

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{b(bx_1 - ay_1) - ac}{a^2 + b^2}, \quad k = \frac{a(-bx_1 + ay_1) - bc}{a^2 + b^2}}$$

**Cleaner form using the standard projection formula:**

$$h = x_1 - \frac{a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$$

$$k = y_1 - \frac{b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Image (Reflection) of a Point in a Line

**Given:** Line $L: ax + by + c = 0$ and point $P(x_1, y_1)$. Find $P'(x_2, y_2)$, the reflection of $P$ across $L$.

**Step 1 — The foot of perpendicular $Q$ is the midpoint of $P$ and $P'$:**

$$\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} = h, \quad \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} = k$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $x_2$ and $y_2$:**

$$x_2 = 2h - x_1, \quad y_2 = 2k - y_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values of $h$ and $k$ from Section 3.5:**

$$x_2 = 2\left[x_1 - \frac{a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}\right] - x_1$$

$$x_2 = x_1 - \frac{2a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$$

**Step 4 — Similarly for $y_2$:**

$$y_2 = y_1 - \frac{2b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$$

**Final Compact Result:**

$$\boxed{x_2 = x_1 - \frac{2a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}, \quad y_2 = y_1 - \frac{2b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}}$$

**CAD Application:** This formula is used in **mirror transformations** in 2D drafting — reflecting a sketch entity about a reference line (e.g., symmetric part design).

---

## 3.7 Derivation: Parametric Form of a Line (2D and 3D)

### 3.7.1 2D Parametric Form

**Step 1 — Start with the two-point form:**

$$\frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1} = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

**Step 2 — Define a parameter $t$ equal to this common ratio:**

$$t = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$ and $y$:**

$$x = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1)$$

$$y = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the parameter:**
- $t = 0 \Rightarrow$ point $P_1$
- $t = 1 \Rightarrow$ point $P_2$
- $0 < t < 1 \Rightarrow$ point lies **between** $P_1$ and $P_2$
- $t < 0$ or $t > 1 \Rightarrow$ point lies **outside** the segment

$$\boxed{x(t) = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1), \quad y(t) = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}}$$

### 3.7.2 3D Parametric / Symmetric Form

**Step 1 — A line in 3D is defined by a point $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and direction ratios $(a, b, c)$:**

**Step 2 — The parametric equations are:**

$$x = x_1 + at, \quad y = y_1 + bt, \quad z = z_1 + ct$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the parameter $t$ to get the symmetric form:**

$$\frac{x - x_1}{a} = \frac{y - y_1}{b} = \frac{z - z_1}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Vector form using position vector $\vec{r}$:**

$$\boxed{\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda \vec{d}}$$

where $\vec{a} = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ is the position vector of a known point, $\vec{d} = (a, b, c)$ is the direction vector, and $\lambda$ is a scalar parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why parametric form dominates CAD:** In parametric form, computing any point on the line is a single multiplication and addition — ideal for hardware-level rendering. The DDA and Bresenham's algorithms both exploit this by stepping $t$ in small increments.

---

## 3.8 Worked Example: End-to-End Problem

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]:** Find the equation of the line passing through the point $(2, -3)$ and making an angle of $135°$ with the positive $x$-axis. Also, find the perpendicular distance from the origin to this line.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the slope from the angle:**

$$m = \tan(135°) = \tan(180° - 45°) = -\tan(45°) = -1$$

**Step 2 — Use the point-slope form with $P(2, -3)$ and $m = -1$:**

$$y - (-3) = -1 \cdot (x - 2)$$

$$y + 3 = -x + 2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to general form:**

$$x + y + 1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute the distance from the origin $(0, 0)$ to this line:**

$$d = \frac{\vert a \cdot 0 + b \cdot 0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{\vert 0 + 0 + 1 \vert}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707 \text{ units}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{Line: } x + y + 1 = 0, \quad \text{Distance from origin: } \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \text{ units}}$$

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation of a Line and Its Key Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["ORIGIN O<br/>0,0"]
    P1["Point P1<br/>x1, y1"]
    P2["Point P2<br/>x2, y2"]

    O -->|"perpendicular p"| N
    N -->|"lies on"| L
    P1 -->|"lies on"| L
    P2 -->|"lies on"| L
    PT -->|"drop perpendicular"| D
    D -->|"strikes"| L

    style O fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#0066cc,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style N fill:#00cc66,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PT fill:#cc33ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff9933,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Straight Line in a CAD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER INPUT<br/>2 Points OR<br/>1 Point + Slope OR<br/>2 Clicks on Screen"]
    B["FORM SELECTION MODULE<br/>Choose equation form<br/>based on input type"]
    C["COEFFICIENT COMPUTATION<br/>Compute a, b, c<br/>or m, c<br/>or a, b, p"]
    D["VALIDATION MODULE<br/>Check a² + b² ≠ 0<br/>Check for degenerate inputs"]
    E["INTERNAL STORAGE<br/>Store as parametric form<br/>P1 + t * direction"]
    F["DDA / BRESENHAM MODULE<br/>Rasterize for display"]
    G["TRANSFORMATION ENGINE<br/>Translate, Rotate, Scale"]
    H["CLIPPING MODULE<br/>Cohen-Sutherland against viewport"]
    I["RENDERING OUTPUT<br/>Pixels on screen"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|"Valid"| E
    D -->|"Invalid"| A
    E --> F
    E --> G
    G --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I

    style A fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#50e3c2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#b8e986,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#f5a623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#7ed321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#bd10e0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#9013fe,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#d0021b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#000000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Relationship Map: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Line L1: y = m1 x + c1"]
    L2["Line L2: y = m2 x + c2"]
    P["PARALLEL CHECK<br/>m1 = m2 AND c1 ≠ c2"]
    PP["PERPENDICULAR CHECK<br/>m1 × m2 = -1"]
    INT["INTERSECTING (OBLIQUE)<br/>m1 ≠ m2<br/>1 + m1 m2 ≠ 0"]
    ANG["Angle between lines<br/>tan φ = |m1 - m2| / |1 + m1 m2|"]
    DIS["Distance between parallels<br/>d = |c1 - c2| / sqrt(a² + b²)"]

    L1 --> P
    L1 --> PP
    L1 --> INT
    P --> DIS
    PP --> ANG
    INT --> ANG
    L2 --> P
    L2 --> PP
    L2 --> INT

    style L1 fill:#007aff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#ff3b30,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P fill:#34c759,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PP fill:#ff9500,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style ANG fill:#5856d6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DIS fill:#ff2d55,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Find the angle between the lines $y = \sqrt{3}x + 5$ and $y = -x + 7$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the slopes:**

$$m_1 = \sqrt{3}, \quad m_2 = -1$$

**Step 2 — Apply the angle formula:**

$$\tan\phi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sqrt{3} - (-1)}{1 + (\sqrt{3})(-1)} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sqrt{3} + 1}{1 - \sqrt{3}} \right\vert$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the denominator:**

$$\tan\phi = \left\vert \frac{(\sqrt{3} + 1)(1 + \sqrt{3})}{(1 - \sqrt{3})(1 + \sqrt{3})} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sqrt{3} + 3 + 1 + \sqrt{3}}{1 - 3} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{2\sqrt{3} + 4}{-2} \right\vert = \left\vert -(\sqrt{3} + 2) \right\vert = \sqrt{3} + 2$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the special value:**

$$\tan(75°) = 2 + \sqrt{3} \implies \phi = 75°$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 75°}$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Find the equation of a line passing through the point $(4, -3)$ and parallel to the line $5x - 3y + 7 = 0$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Find the slope of the given line $5x - 3y + 7 = 0$:**

$$3y = 5x + 7 \implies y = \frac{5}{3}x + \frac{7}{3} \implies m = \frac{5}{3}$$

**Step 2 — For a parallel line, the slope is the same. Using point-slope form with $(4, -3)$:**

$$y - (-3) = \frac{5}{3}(x - 4)$$

$$y + 3 = \frac{5}{3}x - \frac{20}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 3 throughout:**

$$3y + 9 = 5x - 20$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to general form:**

$$\boxed{5x - 3y - 29 = 0}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Full internal choice is provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Find the equation of the straight line passing through the point of intersection of the lines $2x + 3y - 4 = 0$ and $5x - 2y + 7 = 0$, and which is parallel to the line $4x - 7y + 3 = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the perpendicular distance from the point $(3, -2)$ to the line $4x - 3y + 5 = 0$. Also find the foot of the perpendicular and the image of the point in the line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Find the intersection point of $2x + 3y - 4 = 0$ and $5x - 2y + 7 = 0$.**

From equation (i): $2x + 3y = 4$
From equation (ii): $5x - 2y = -7$

**Step 2 — Solve by elimination. Multiply (i) by 2 and (ii) by 3:**

$$4x + 6y = 8 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$
$$15x - 6y = -21 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 3 — Add (iii) and (iv):**

$$19x = -13 \implies x = -\frac{13}{19}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into (i):**

$$2\left(-\frac{13}{19}\right) + 3y = 4$$

$$-\frac{26}{19} + 3y = 4 \implies 3y = 4 + \frac{26}{19} = \frac{76 + 26}{19} = \frac{102}{19}$$

$$y = \frac{34}{19}$$

**Step 5 — Intersection point:** $P = \left(-\frac{13}{19}, \frac{34}{19}\right)$

**Step 6 — Find the slope of the parallel line $4x - 7y + 3 = 0$:**

$$7y = 4x + 3 \implies y = \frac{4}{7}x + \frac{3}{7} \implies m = \frac{4}{7}$$

**Step 7 — Use point-slope form:**

$$y - \frac{34}{19} = \frac{4}{7}\left(x + \frac{13}{19}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Multiply both sides by $7 \times 19 = 133$:**

$$133y - 133 \cdot \frac{34}{19} = 76\left(x + \frac{13}{19}\right)$$

$$133y - 7 \cdot 34 = 76x + 76 \cdot \frac{13}{19}$$

$$133y - 238 = 76x + 52$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange:**

$$76x - 133y + 290 = 0$$

$$\boxed{76x - 133y + 290 = 0}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the perpendicular distance using $d = \frac{\vert ax_1 + by_1 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$:**

$$d = \frac{\vert 4(3) + (-3)(-2) + 5 \vert}{\sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2}} = \frac{\vert 12 + 6 + 5 \vert}{\sqrt{16 + 9}} = \frac{23}{5} = 4.6 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Find the foot of perpendicular $Q(h, k)$.**

**Step 3 — Apply the formula from Section 3.5:**

$$h = x_1 - \frac{a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2} = 3 - \frac{4(23)}{25} = 3 - \frac{92}{25} = \frac{75 - 92}{25} = -\frac{17}{25}$$

$$k = y_1 - \frac{b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2} = -2 - \frac{(-3)(23)}{25} = -2 + \frac{69}{25} = \frac{-50 + 69}{25} = \frac{19}{25}$$

**Step 4 — Foot of perpendicular:**

$$\boxed{Q = \left(-\frac{17}{25}, \frac{19}{25}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Find the image (reflection) $P'(x_2, y_2)$ using the formula from Section 3.6:**

$$x_2 = x_1 - \frac{2a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2} = 3 - \frac{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 23}{25} = 3 - \frac{184}{25} = \frac{75 - 184}{25} = -\frac{109}{25}$$

$$y_2 = y_1 - \frac{2b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2} = -2 - \frac{2 \cdot (-3) \cdot 23}{25} = -2 + \frac{138}{25} = \frac{-50 + 138}{25} = \frac{88}{25}$$

**Step 6 — Image point:**

$$\boxed{P' = \left(-\frac{109}{25}, \frac{88}{25}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification: Midpoint of $P$ and $P'$ should equal $Q$:**

$$\text{Midpoint}_x = \frac{3 + (-\frac{109}{25})}{2} = \frac{\frac{75 - 109}{25}}{2} = \frac{-34}{25 \cdot 2} = -\frac{17}{25} = h \ \checkmark$$

$$\text{Midpoint}_y = \frac{-2 + \frac{88}{25}}{2} = \frac{\frac{-50 + 88}{25}}{2} = \frac{38}{50} = \frac{19}{25} = k \ \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** A line makes intercepts of equal length on the axes. If it passes through the point $(2, 3)$, find its equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Reduce the equation $3x - 4y + 10 = 0$ to (i) slope-intercept form, (ii) intercept form, and (iii) normal form. Hence find the perpendicular distance from the origin. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Let the equal intercepts be $a$ on the $x$-axis and $a$ on the $y$-axis** (note: $a$ can be positive or negative).

**Step 2 — Write the intercept form:**

$$\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{a} = 1 \implies x + y = a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the point $(2, 3)$:**

$$2 + 3 = a \implies a = 5$$

**Step 4 — Two possible cases:**

**Case 1:** $a = 5$:

$$x + y = 5 \implies \boxed{x + y - 5 = 0}$$

**Case 2:** $a = -5$ (intercepts of equal magnitude but opposite sign):

$$x + y = -5 \implies \boxed{x + y + 5 = 0}$$

**Final Answer:** There are two such lines: $x + y - 5 = 0$ and $x + y + 5 = 0$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Step 1 — Start with $3x - 4y + 10 = 0$.**

**(i) Slope-Intercept Form:**

**Step 2 — Solve for $y$:**

$$4y = 3x + 10 \implies y = \frac{3}{4}x + \frac{10}{4}$$

$$\boxed{y = \frac{3}{4}x + \frac{5}{2}}$$

**Slope:** $m = \frac{3}{4}$; **$y$-intercept:** $c = \frac{5}{2}$

**(ii) Intercept Form:**

**Step 3 — Move 10 to the RHS and divide:**

$$3x - 4y = -10 \implies \frac{3x}{-10} - \frac{4y}{-10} = 1$$

$$\frac{x}{-10/3} + \frac{y}{-10/(-4)} = 1 \implies \frac{x}{-10/3} + \frac{y}{5/2} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\frac{x}{-\frac{10}{3}} + \frac{y}{\frac{5}{2}} = 1}$$

**$x$-intercept:** $a = -\frac{10}{3}$; **$y$-intercept:** $b = \frac{5}{2}$

**(iii) Normal Form:**

**Step 4 — Identify coefficients:** $a = 3$, $b = -4$, $c = 10$.

**Step 5 — Compute the normalizing factor $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$:**

$$\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 6 — Divide the entire equation by $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = 5$ and ensure $p > 0$:**

$$\frac{3x - 4y + 10}{5} = 0 \implies \frac{3}{5}x - \frac{4}{5}y + 2 = 0$$

$$-\frac{3}{5}x + \frac{4}{5}y - 2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{x \cos\alpha + y \sin\alpha = p, \quad \text{where } \cos\alpha = -\frac{3}{5}, \ \sin\alpha = \frac{4}{5}, \ p = 2}$$

**Step 7 — Perpendicular distance from origin = $p$:**

$$\boxed{d = p = 2 \text{ units}}$$

**Verification using general formula:**

$$d = \frac{\vert 3(0) - 4(0) + 10 \vert}{\sqrt{9 + 16}} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \ \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Straight Line Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value in distance formula** (#1 mistake): Students frequently write $d = \frac{ax_1 + by_1 + c}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}$ instead of using $\vert \cdot \vert$. Distance is **always non-negative**; the absolute value is **mandatory**. Loss: 1-2 marks.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the sign of $p$ in normal form**: When converting to normal form, the entire equation must be multiplied by $-1$ if $c > 0$ (or $c/\sqrt{a^2+b^2} > 0$) to ensure $p \geq 0$. Many students skip this sign adjustment and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Division by zero when $1 + m_1 m_2 = 0$**: This condition means the lines are **perpendicular** (angle = $90°$). Writing $\tan\phi = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}$ without the absolute value or without the perpendicular check costs 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing the angle of inclination with the angle between lines**: The angle of inclination $\theta$ is measured from the positive $x$-axis ($0 \leq \theta < 180°$). The angle between two lines $\phi$ is always the **acute** angle ($0 \leq \phi \leq 90°$). Use $\vert \tan\phi \vert$.
>
> 5. **Vertical line errors**: For a vertical line $x = a$, the slope is **undefined**, not "$\infty$ treated as a number." Always state $x = a$ explicitly and use the general form $1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y - a = 0$ in distance formulas.
>
> 6. **Skipping units and justification**: KTU examiners award 1 mark for stating the formula and 1 mark for substituting values correctly. Even if your final answer is right, missing the formula statement costs you.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- ✅ A straight line is defined by a **first-degree equation** $ax + by + c = 0$ in 2D, or parametrically as $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{d}$ in any dimension.
- ✅ **Slope** is the heart of line analysis: $m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$. **Vertical lines have undefined slope.**
- ✅ **Six equivalent forms**: General, Slope-Intercept, Point-Slope, Two-Point, Intercept, Normal. Choose the form that matches your given data.
- ✅ **Parallel lines**: $m_1 = m_2$ (and different $c$).
- ✅ **Perpendicular lines**: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$.
- ✅ **Angle between lines**: $\tan\phi = \left\vert \frac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$; if denominator = 0, then $\phi = 90°$.
- ✅ **Distance from point to line**: $d = \frac{\vert ax_1 + by_1 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ — **never forget the absolute value bars**.
- ✅ **Distance between parallel lines** $ax + by + c_1 = 0$ and $ax + by + c_2 = 0$: $d = \frac{\vert c_1 - c_2 \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$.
- ✅ **Foot of perpendicular**: $h = x_1 - \frac{a(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$, $k = y_1 - \frac{b(ax_1 + by_1 + c)}{a^2 + b^2}$.
- ✅ **Image (reflection) of point**: $x_2 = x_1 - \frac{2a(\cdots)}{a^2 + b^2}$, $y_2 = y_1 - \frac{2b(\cdots)}{a^2 + b^2}$. Verify by midpoint check.
- ✅ **Parametric form is the CAD-native form** — used in DDA, Bresenham's algorithm, and 3D line representation.
- ✅ **Normal form** gives distance from origin directly: $p$ in $x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha = p$ IS the distance.
- ✅ In **3D**, lines use the symmetric form $\frac{x-x_1}{a} = \frac{y-y_1}{b} = \frac{z-z_1}{c}$ or vector form $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{d}$.
- ✅ **Angle bisectors** of two intersecting lines $L_1$ and $L_2$: $\frac{L_1}{\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2}} = \pm\frac{L_2}{\sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2}}$ (use $+$ for the bisector of the angle containing the origin, or the acute angle bisector when the origin is in that region).
- ✅ **Always state the formula before substituting** — this earns the "method" marks even if arithmetic goes wrong.
- ✅ **Cross-verify** every distance computation using at least one of: (a) absolute value present, (b) result is positive, (c) point lies on appropriate side check.

---

**End of Module 3 — Straight Lines Study Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Conic

In the context of **Computer Aided Design and Analysis (CADA)**, a **conic** (or conic section) is defined as a smooth curve generated by the intersection of a plane with a right circular double cone. The plane's angle of intersection with the cone's axis determines the type of conic produced.

Mathematically, the **unified eccentricity definition** of a conic is the most powerful formulation used in CAD:

> A conic is the locus of all points $P$ in a plane such that the ratio of the distance from $P$ to a fixed point (focus) to the distance from $P$ to a fixed line (directrix) is a constant $e$, called the **eccentricity**.

$$\boxed{e = \frac{\text{Distance from point to Focus}}{\text{Distance from point to Directrix}} = \frac{PF}{Pd}}$$

The value of eccentricity $e$ classifies the conic:
- $e = 0$ → **Circle** (special case of ellipse)
- $0 < e < 1$ → **Ellipse**
- $e = 1$ → **Parabola**
- $e > 1$ → **Hyperbola**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the PEMET523 course, students must master the focus-directrix property because it forms the mathematical foundation of **Rational Quadratic Bézier Curves**, which are widely used in CAD/CAM systems like AutoCAD, CATIA, and SolidWorks for designing free-form curves and surfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a flashlight (point light source) and shining it at a wall while tilting the flashlight at different angles:
- When the flashlight is held **perpendicular** to the wall → the light projects a perfect **circle**.
- When you tilt the flashlight slightly → the circle stretches into an **ellipse** (the more you tilt, the more elongated it becomes).
- When you tilt the flashlight at exactly the angle equal to the cone's slant → the light beam never closes back, producing a **parabola**.
- When you tilt the flashlight even more → the light beam opens up the other way, creating a **hyperbola** (two separate branches).

### 1.3 Engineering Relevance of Conics

Conics are ubiquitous in engineering design:

| Engineering Domain | Conic Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Parabolic satellite dish reflectors; elliptical orbits |
| **Civil/Architecture** | Parabolic arches (bridges, dams); elliptical domes |
| **Mechanical** | Hyperbolic gear teeth profiles; elliptical cam profiles |
| **Optics** | Parabolic mirrors, elliptical reflectors in telescopes |
| **CAD/Graphics** | Bézier curve control, NURBS surface modeling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** All linear dimensions are in **millimeters (mm)** or **meters (m)** per SI conventions. Angular parameters like eccentricity $e$ and angle $\theta$ are **dimensionless**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Construction Logic

A conic curve is generated by satisfying three primary conditions:
1. **A fixed point (Focus):** $F(c, 0)$ for standard orientation
2. **A fixed line (Directrix):** $x = d$ where $d > c$ ensures valid eccentricity
3. **A constant ratio (Eccentricity):** $e$

For any point $P(x, y)$ on the conic:
$$\sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2} = e \cdot \vert x - d \vert$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Classification Logic

**Step 1: Identify the eccentricity $e$.**
- $e < 1$ → Ellipse: Two foci, closed bounded curve
- $e = 1$ → Parabola: One focus, open unbounded curve
- $e > 1$ → Hyperbola: Two foci, two separate branches

**Step 2: Determine the orientation.**
- Major/Transverse axis along X-axis (standard)
- Major/Transverse axis along Y-axis (rotated $90°$)

**Step 3: Locate key geometric features.**
- Vertices, foci, directrices, latus rectum, center (if exists)

**Step 4: Apply for CAD rendering.**
- Use parametric form for smooth curve generation
- Use implicit form for point-in-curve tests
- Use rational form for weighted control in Bézier

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the complete set of formulas required for KTU examination problems on conics:

| Conic Type | Standard Cartesian Equation | Parametric Form | Eccentricity $e$ | Focus | Directrix |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Circle** | $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ | $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ | $0$ | Center $(0,0)$ | None (at infinity) |
| **Ellipse** | $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ | $x = a\cos\theta$, $y = b\sin\theta$ | $e = \dfrac{\sqrt{a^2 - b^2}}{a}$ | $(\pm ae, 0)$ | $x = \pm \dfrac{a}{e}$ |
| **Parabola** | $y^2 = 4ax$ | $x = at^2$, $y = 2at$ | $1$ | $(a, 0)$ | $x = -a$ |
| **Hyperbola** | $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ | $x = a\sec\theta$, $y = b\tan\theta$ | $e = \dfrac{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}{a}$ | $(\pm ae, 0)$ | $x = \pm \dfrac{a}{e}$ |

**Polar Form of Conics (Most useful for CAD):**
$$r = \frac{ed}{1 \pm e\cos\theta} \quad \text{or} \quad r = \frac{ed}{1 \pm e\sin\theta}$$

where $d$ is the distance from directrix to focus (semi-latus rectum parameter).

**Key Derived Formulas:**

| Quantity | Formula | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|
| Semi-latus rectum | $\ell = \dfrac{b^2}{a}$ | Ellipse, Hyperbola |
| Latus rectum length | $4a$ | Parabola |
| Latus rectum length | $\dfrac{2b^2}{a}$ | Ellipse, Hyperbola |
| Sum of focal distances | $2a$ | Ellipse (constant) |
| Difference of focal distances | $2a$ | Hyperbola (constant) |
| Asymptotes | $y = \pm \dfrac{b}{a}x$ | Hyperbola |
| Tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ | $\dfrac{xx_1}{a^2} \pm \dfrac{yy_1}{b^2} = 1$ | Ellipse/Hyperbola |
| Tangent at $(at^2, 2at)$ | $ty = x + at^2$ | Parabola |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern CAD systems, the **Rational Quadratic Bézier Curve** uses a conic-based weight parameter to define curve shape:

$$B(t) = \frac{(1-t)^2 P_0 + 2wt(1-t) P_1 + t^2 P_2}{(1-t)^2 + 2wt(1-t) + t^2}$$

where $w$ is the weight that controls the conic type. This single mathematical framework allows a CAD designer to draw circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas using identical software tools, demonstrating the unified power of conic mathematics in engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Ellipse Equation from Focus-Directrix Definition

**Given:**
- Focus $F(c, 0)$ on the X-axis
- Directrix $x = \dfrac{a}{e}$ (with $a > c > 0$ and $0 < e < 1$)
- Any point $P(x, y)$ on the ellipse
- Eccentricity $e = \dfrac{PF}{Pd}$ (constant)

**Step 1:** Apply the distance formula for $PF$ and $Pd$.

$$PF = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + (y-0)^2} = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

$$Pd = \left\vert x - \frac{a}{e} \right\vert = \frac{a}{e} - x \quad \text{(since } x < \dfrac{a}{e} \text{)}$$

**Step 2:** Set up the eccentricity equation.

$$e = \frac{\sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}}{\frac{a}{e} - x}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to eliminate the denominator.

$$e\left(\frac{a}{e} - x\right) = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

$$a - ex = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 4:** Square both sides to remove the square root.

$$(a - ex)^2 = (x-c)^2 + y^2$$

$$a^2 - 2aex + e^2 x^2 = x^2 - 2cx + c^2 + y^2$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange terms, grouping $x^2$ and $x$.

$$a^2 - c^2 = x^2(1 - e^2) + 2x(c - ae) + y^2$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $c = ae$ (by definition of focus for standard ellipse). Substitute.

$$a^2 - a^2e^2 = x^2(1 - e^2) + 2x(ae - ae) + y^2$$

$$a^2(1 - e^2) = x^2(1 - e^2) + y^2$$

**Step 7:** Divide both sides by $a^2(1 - e^2)$ and let $b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$.

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$$

This is the **standard equation of an ellipse** in Cartesian form. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation of the Parabola Equation from Focus-Directrix Definition

**Given:**
- Focus $F(a, 0)$
- Directrix $x = -a$
- Eccentricity $e = 1$

**Step 1:** Apply distance equations.

$$PF = \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + y^2}, \quad Pd = x + a$$

**Step 2:** Apply $e = 1$ condition.

$$\sqrt{(x-a)^2 + y^2} = x + a$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides.

$$(x-a)^2 + y^2 = (x+a)^2$$

$$x^2 - 2ax + a^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$$

**Step 4:** Cancel common terms.

$$-2ax + y^2 = 2ax$$

$$y^2 = 4ax$$

This is the **standard equation of a parabola** opening rightward. ∎

### 3.3 Derivation of the Hyperbola Equation from Focus-Directrix Definition

**Given:**
- Focus $F(c, 0)$
- Directrix $x = \dfrac{a}{e}$ where $e > 1$
- $c = ae > a$

**Step 1:** Apply distance equations.

$$PF = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}, \quad Pd = \frac{a}{e} - x$$

**Step 2:** Apply eccentricity condition $e > 1$.

$$e\left(\frac{a}{e} - x\right) = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

$$a - ex = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides.

$$a^2 - 2aex + e^2 x^2 = x^2 - 2cx + c^2 + y^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $c = ae$ and rearrange.

$$a^2 - a^2 e^2 = x^2(1 - e^2) + y^2$$

**Step 5:** Since $e > 1$, $(1 - e^2)$ is negative. Divide by $(e^2 - 1)a^2$ and let $b^2 = a^2(e^2 - 1)$.

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$$

This is the **standard equation of a hyperbola**. ∎

### 3.4 Derivation of the Polar Form of Conics

**Given:** Focus at origin (pole), directrix is vertical line $x = d$ (or $x = -d$).

**Step 1:** For any point $P(r, \theta)$ in polar coordinates, the distance to focus is $r$ and distance to directrix is $d - r\cos\theta$.

$$r = e(d - r\cos\theta)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $r$.

$$r + er\cos\theta = ed$$

$$r(1 + e\cos\theta) = ed$$

$$r = \frac{ed}{1 + e\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3:** The plus/minus depends on directrix position:
- Directrix at $x = d$ → $r = \dfrac{ed}{1 + e\cos\theta}$
- Directrix at $x = -d$ → $r = \dfrac{ed}{1 - e\cos\theta}$

### 3.5 Derivation of Eccentricity for Standard Ellipse

**Given:** Standard ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ with $a > b > 0$.

**Step 1:** At vertex $(a, 0)$, the distance to focus is $a - c$ and to directrix is $\dfrac{a}{e} - a$.

$$e = \frac{a - c}{\frac{a}{e} - a}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $c$ in terms of $a$ and $e$.

$$e\left(\frac{a - ae}{e}\right) = a - c$$

$$a - ae = a - c$$

$$c = ae$$

**Step 3:** Use the relationship $b^2 = a^2 - c^2$.

$$b^2 = a^2 - a^2 e^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$$

$$e^2 = 1 - \frac{b^2}{a^2}$$

$$e = \frac{\sqrt{a^2 - b^2}}{a}$$

This confirms the eccentricity formula for the standard ellipse. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conic Section Generation from Double Cone

The following block-level diagram shows how different conics are generated based on the angle of the cutting plane relative to the cone's axis and slant surface:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Double Right Circular Cone] --> B{Plane Orientation}
    B -->|Perpendicular to Axis| C[Circle - e equals 0]
    B -->|Angle less than Cone Half Angle| D[Ellipse - 0 less than e less than 1]
    B -->|Parallel to Slant Surface| E[Parabola - e equals 1]
    B -->|Angle greater than Cone Half Angle| F[Hyperbola - e greater than 1]
    C --> G[Special case of Ellipse]
    D --> H[Closed Bounded Curve]
    E --> I[Open Unbounded Single Curve]
    F --> J[Two Separate Branches]
    H --> K[Application: Planetary Orbits, Gears]
    I --> L[Application: Projectile Motion, Reflectors]
    J --> M[Application: Cooling Towers, Navigation]
```

### 4.2 Focus-Directrix Property Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Any Point P on Conic] --> B[Distance PF to Focus]
    A --> C[Distance Pd to Directrix]
    B --> D[Ratio e equals PF by Pd]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Classify by e}
    E -->|e equals 0| F[Circle]
    E -->|e between 0 and 1| G[Ellipse]
    E -->|e equals 1| H[Parabola]
    E -->|e greater than 1| I[Hyperbola]
```

### 4.3 CAD Application Block Diagram for Rational Bézier Conic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Designer Inputs] --> B[Control Points P0 P1 P2]
    B --> C[Weight Parameter w]
    C --> D{Conic Type Selection}
    D -->|w equals 0 or 1| E[Degenerate Straight Line]
    D -->|0 less than w less than 1| F[Elliptical Arc]
    D -->|w equals 0.5| G[Parabolic Arc]
    D -->|w greater than 1| H[Hyperbolic Arc]
    D -->|w tends to infinity| I[Circular Arc Approximation]
    F --> J[Rendered CAD Curve]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Manufacturing Output - G code or STL]
```

### 4.4 Conic Parameterization in CAD Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mathematical Definition of Conic] --> B[Implicit Form]
    A --> C[Parametric Form]
    A --> D[Polar Form]
    A --> E[Rational Form]
    B --> F[For Point-in-Curve Tests]
    C --> G[For Curve Tracing and Rendering]
    D --> H[For Focus at Origin Applications]
    E --> I[For Bézier and NURBS Systems]
    F --> J[Unified CAD Engine Output]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define eccentricity. How does it classify different conic sections?**

**Model Answer:**

Eccentricity $e$ is defined as the ratio of the distance from any point on the conic to the focus, to the distance from the same point to the directrix.

$$e = \frac{\text{Distance to Focus}}{\text{Distance to Directrix}}$$

Classification:
- $e = 0$ → Circle
- $0 < e < 1$ → Ellipse
- $e = 1$ → Parabola
- $e > 1$ → Hyperbola

**(3 marks - Complete answer with classification table)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] State the polar form of a conic and explain its significance in CAD.**

**Model Answer:**

The polar form of a conic with focus at the pole is:

$$r = \frac{ed}{1 \pm e\cos\theta} \quad \text{or} \quad r = \frac{ed}{1 \pm e\sin\theta}$$

**Significance in CAD:**
1. Allows direct computation of curve points using only the angular parameter $\theta$.
2. The single formula handles all four conic types by varying $e$.
3. Simplifies ray-tracing in computer graphics for reflective conic surfaces.
4. Forms the basis of polar-based curve generation algorithms in CAD software.

**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.A (a)** Derive the standard equation of an ellipse from the focus-directrix definition. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Setup. Let focus be $F(c, 0)$ and directrix be $x = \dfrac{a}{e}$ where $0 < e < 1$.

For any point $P(x, y)$ on the ellipse:
$$PF = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}, \quad Pd = \frac{a}{e} - x$$

**Step 2:** Apply eccentricity condition.

$$e = \frac{\sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}}{\frac{a}{e} - x}$$

$$a - ex = \sqrt{(x-c)^2 + y^2}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides.

$$(a - ex)^2 = (x-c)^2 + y^2$$

$$a^2 - 2aex + e^2 x^2 = x^2 - 2cx + c^2 + y^2$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange and use $c = ae$.

$$a^2 - c^2 = x^2(1 - e^2) + 2x(c - ae) + y^2$$

$$a^2(1 - e^2) = x^2(1 - e^2) + y^2$$

**Step 5:** Let $b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$.

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$$

This is the standard ellipse equation. ∎

**(7 marks - Full derivation)**

---

**Q.A (b)** For the ellipse $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$, find the eccentricity, foci, directrices, and length of the latus rectum. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:** $a^2 = 25$, $b^2 = 9$, so $a = 5$, $b = 3$.

**Step 1: Eccentricity.**

$$e = \frac{\sqrt{a^2 - b^2}}{a} = \frac{\sqrt{25 - 9}}{5} = \frac{\sqrt{16}}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

**Step 2: Foci.**

$$c = ae = 5 \times \frac{4}{5} = 4$$

Foci are at $(\pm 4, 0)$.

**Step 3: Directrices.**

$$x = \pm \frac{a}{e} = \pm \frac{5}{4/5} = \pm \frac{25}{4} = \pm 6.25$$

**Step 4: Length of Latus Rectum.**

$$L = \frac{2b^2}{a} = \frac{2 \times 9}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ units}$$

**Final Answer:** $e = 0.8$, Foci: $(\pm 4, 0)$, Directrices: $x = \pm 6.25$, Latus rectum $= 3.6$ units.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.B (a)** Derive the standard equation of a parabola from the focus-directrix definition. Hence derive the equation of tangent at any point. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let focus $F(a, 0)$ and directrix $x = -a$. For any point $P(x, y)$:

$$\sqrt{(x-a)^2 + y^2} = x + a$$

**Step 2:** Square both sides.

$$(x-a)^2 + y^2 = (x+a)^2$$

$$x^2 - 2ax + a^2 + y^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$$

$$y^2 = 4ax$$

**Step 3: Tangent at point $(x_1, y_1)$ on the parabola.**

Since $(x_1, y_1)$ lies on the parabola: $y_1^2 = 4ax_1$.

The slope of the parabola is $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{2a}{y}$.

Tangent equation: $y - y_1 = \dfrac{2a}{y_1}(x - x_1)$

$$y y_1 - y_1^2 = 2ax - 2ax_1$$

$$y y_1 = 2ax - 2ax_1 + y_1^2 = 2ax - 2ax_1 + 4ax_1$$

$$y y_1 = 2a(x + x_1)$$

**Final Tangent Equation:** $yy_1 = 2a(x + x_1)$ ∎

**(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B (b)** A satellite dish is designed as a paraboloid of revolution with vertex at origin and focus at $(3, 0)$ meters. Find: (i) Equation of the parabolic cross-section. (ii) The diameter of the dish at a depth of 2 meters from the vertex. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:** Focus at $(3, 0)$, vertex at origin, parabola opens rightward.

**(i) Equation of the parabola:**

Standard form $y^2 = 4ax$ with $a = 3$.

$$\boxed{y^2 = 12x}$$

**(ii) Diameter at depth 2 meters:**

At $x = 2$ meters:

$$y^2 = 12 \times 2 = 24$$

$$y = \pm\sqrt{24} = \pm 2\sqrt{6} \approx \pm 4.899 \text{ m}$$

**Diameter** $= 2y = 2 \times 2\sqrt{6} = 4\sqrt{6} \approx 9.798$ m $\approx 9.8$ m.

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the conic type from eccentricity:** Students often write the standard form without computing $e$ first. Always classify the conic using $e$ before applying the appropriate formula.
> 2. **Sign errors in directrices:** For hyperbola with $e > 1$, the directrices lie **between** the two branches at $x = \pm a/e$, NOT outside. Many students incorrectly place them outside.
> 3. **Latus rectum confusion:** For parabola, latus rectum is $4a$. For ellipse/hyperbola, it is $2b^2/a$. Mixing these leads to mark deductions.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Always mention units (mm, m, cm) for engineering problems — KTU evaluators specifically check for this.
> 5. **Forgetting the $+$ constant in polar form:** The correct form is $r = \dfrac{ed}{1 + e\cos\theta}$, not $r = \dfrac{e\cos\theta}{d}$. This is a common formula recall error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Eccentricity $e$** is the defining parameter that unifies all conic types in a single focus-directrix framework.
- **Circle** is a special ellipse with $e = 0$ (coincident foci at the center).
- **Parabola** has $e = 1$ and only one focus with one directrix.
- **Hyperbola** has two branches and two directrices, with foci located **outside** the vertices.
- **Polar form** $r = \dfrac{ed}{1 \pm e\cos\theta}$ is the most CAD-friendly representation.
- **Latus rectum** is the chord through the focus perpendicular to the major axis.
- For **ellipse**: $b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$; For **hyperbola**: $b^2 = a^2(e^2 - 1)$.
- **Sum of focal distances** for ellipse $= 2a$ (constant); **difference** for hyperbola $= 2a$ (constant).
- **CAD Application:** Conics form the mathematical basis of Rational Bézier curves used in AutoCAD, CATIA, and SolidWorks.
- **Parametric form** is preferred over implicit form in CAD for curve generation and rendering.
- Always **state the conic type** explicitly before solving any problem.
- For KTU numerical problems, always include **units** in the final answer.
- The **tangent at vertex** is perpendicular to the major/transverse axis for all conics.
- **Asymptotes** exist only for hyperbolas and are $y = \pm \dfrac{b}{a}x$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cubic Splines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cubic Splines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cubic Spline** is a piecewise continuous curve constructed from a sequence of cubic polynomials joined end-to-end with continuous first and second derivatives at the joining points (called **knots** or **nodes**). For a given set of $n+1$ data points $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, the cubic spline $S(x)$ consists of $n$ cubic polynomial segments, where each segment $S_i(x)$ is defined over the interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form):**
> A function $S(x)$ is called a **cubic spline** if there exist cubic polynomials $S_i(x)$ such that $S(x) = S_i(x)$ for $x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]$, with $S_i, S_i', S_i''$ all continuous on $[x_0, x_n]$, and $S(x_i) = y_i$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

## 1.2 Standard Form of a Cubic Spline Segment

Each polynomial segment $S_i(x)$ on the interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$ is written as:

$$S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3$$

where the coefficients $a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i$ are determined by enforcing **interpolation** and **smoothness** (continuity) conditions at every knot. The total number of unknown coefficients is **$4n$**, and the system is closed by imposing two additional **boundary conditions** at the endpoints.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Flexible Ruler Through Pins:**
> Imagine you are plotting a smooth curve through several pins stuck into a drawing board. A **polynomial interpolant** of high degree is like a *single stiff wire* forced through all pins — it usually develops wild oscillations between pins. A **cubic spline**, on the other hand, is like a **thin, flexible wooden spline (draftsman's tool)**: the wood is locally stiff (so each piece is a smooth cubic arc), but it *bends* gently at each pin. The result is a smooth, oscillation-free curve that hugs every data point. This is why CAD/CAM systems (AutoCAD, CATIA, NX) almost exclusively use cubic splines (Bezier, B-spline, NURBS) for freeform geometry.

## 1.4 Types of Cubic Splines (Boundary Condition Variants)

| Type | End Condition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Cubic Spline** | $S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0$ | Default; smoothest ends; most common in textbooks |
| **Clamped Cubic Spline** | $S'(x_0) = f'(x_0),\ S'(x_n) = f'(x_n)$ | Specified tangent at ends; more accurate if derivatives are known |
| **Not-A-Knot Spline** | $S'''$ continuous across $x_1$ and $x_{n-1}$ | Used in MATLAB `spline` default |
| **Periodic Spline** | $S(x_0) = S(x_n)$ and matching derivatives | Closed curves (gears, blades) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, the **Natural Cubic Spline** is the most frequently tested variant, as it leads to a *symmetric tridiagonal system* that is easy to derive and solve manually.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Continuity Conditions at Each Interior Knot

For the spline to look "smooth", at every interior knot $x_i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$), the adjacent polynomial segments must satisfy:

- **(C1) Interpolation:** $S_i(x_i) = y_i$ and $S_i(x_{i+1}) = y_{i+1}$
- **(C2) First Derivative Continuity:** $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)$ → curve has no sharp corners
- **(C3) Second Derivative Continuity:** $S_{i-1}''(x_i) = S_i''(x_i)$ → curvature is continuous (no sudden bends)

> [!NOTE]
> Together, conditions (C2) and (C3) make the spline **$C^2$ continuous** — a fundamental requirement in surface modeling for automobiles, aircraft fuselages, and ship hulls.

## 2.2 Total Number of Equations vs Unknowns

- Unknown coefficients: $4n$ (since there are $n$ intervals, each with 4 coefficients)
- Equations provided by interpolation: $2n$ (two per segment)
- Equations provided by derivative continuity: $2(n-1)$
- Total equations so far: $2n + 2(n-1) = 4n - 2$
- **Deficit:** 2 equations → supplied by the **two boundary conditions**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving KTU exam questions on Cubic Splines. Memorize the last two rows (tridiagonal system) before any numerical exam.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| $h_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$ | Interval width | Step size between knots |
| $S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x-x_i) + c_i(x-x_i)^2 + d_i(x-x_i)^3$ | Spline segment on $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$ | Writing final cubic equations |
| $a_i = y_i$ | Direct from interpolation at $x_i$ | Computing constant term |
| $c_i = \dfrac{M_i}{2}$ | Linking $c_i$ to second derivative $M_i$ | Step in derivation |
| $d_i = \dfrac{M_{i+1} - M_i}{6 h_i}$ | From $S''$ matching across segment | Slope of curvature |
| $b_i = \dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \dfrac{h_i}{6}(2 M_i + M_{i+1})$ | Linear coefficient | First derivative behavior |
| $h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1}+h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\left[\dfrac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{h_i} - \dfrac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}\right]$ | Tridiagonal system (interior eqn.) | Solving for $M_i$ values |
| $M_0 = 0,\ M_n = 0$ | Natural spline boundary conditions | Top and bottom of system |

**Note on the table:** The vertical separators are standard markdown table pipes, NOT mathematical absolute value bars. In the LaTeX formula above, the expression $\left[ \cdot \right]$ denotes a bracketed grouped term (not absolute value).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU's CAD course:**
> 1. **Tool-path generation** in CNC machining — smooth G1 moves between waypoints are essentially cubic splines.
> 2. **Hull and fuselage lofting** in marine/aerospace CAD — NURBS (generalization of cubic splines) define Class A surfaces.
> 3. **FEA mesh smoothing** — splines generate curvature-continuous transition regions between dissimilar mesh patches.
> 4. **Robotic trajectory planning** — jerk-minimized motion profiles are derived from clamped cubic splines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tridiagonal System (Full Worked Steps)

**Step 1 — Define the segment polynomial.**
Let the $i$-th cubic segment be:

$$S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3, \quad x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first interpolation condition $S_i(x_i) = y_i$.**
Substituting $x = x_i$, all terms containing $(x - x_i)$ vanish:

$$a_i = y_i$$

**Step 3 — Define the second-derivative moment.**
The second derivative of the segment is:

$$S_i''(x) = 2 c_i + 6 d_i (x - x_i)$$

Evaluating at $x = x_i$ gives $S_i''(x_i) = 2c_i$. Define $M_i \triangleq S_i''(x_i) = 2c_i$, hence:

$$c_i = \frac{M_i}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Match $S_i''$ with the next segment at $x_{i+1}$.**
We require $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = S_{i+1}''(x_{i+1})$, i.e., $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = M_{i+1}$. Substituting $x = x_{i+1}$:

$$2 c_i + 6 d_i h_i = M_{i+1} \implies 2 \cdot \frac{M_i}{2} + 6 d_i h_i = M_{i+1}$$

$$M_i + 6 d_i h_i = M_{i+1} \implies d_i = \frac{M_{i+1} - M_i}{6 h_i}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the second interpolation condition $S_i(x_{i+1}) = y_{i+1}$.**
With $x - x_i = h_i$:

$$y_{i+1} = a_i + b_i h_i + c_i h_i^2 + d_i h_i^3 = y_i + b_i h_i + \frac{M_i}{2} h_i^2 + \frac{M_{i+1}-M_i}{6 h_i} h_i^3$$

$$y_{i+1} - y_i = b_i h_i + \frac{M_i h_i^2}{2} + \frac{(M_{i+1}-M_i) h_i^2}{6}$$

Dividing by $h_i$:

$$b_i = \frac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{h_i} - \frac{M_i h_i}{2} - \frac{(M_{i+1}-M_i) h_i}{6}$$

$$b_i = \frac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{h_i} - \frac{h_i}{6}\bigl(3 M_i + M_{i+1} - M_i\bigr) = \frac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{h_i} - \frac{h_i}{6}(2 M_i + M_{i+1})$$

**Step 6 — Enforce first-derivative continuity at the interior knot $x_i$.**
We need $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)$. Computing the first derivative of $S_i$:

$$S_i'(x) = b_i + 2 c_i (x-x_i) + 3 d_i (x-x_i)^2$$

At the **right end** of the previous segment ($x = x_i$ for $S_{i-1}$), using $x - x_{i-1} = h_{i-1}$:

$$S_{i-1}'(x_i) = b_{i-1} + 2 c_{i-1} h_{i-1} + 3 d_{i-1} h_{i-1}^2 = b_{i-1} + M_{i-1} h_{i-1} + \frac{(M_i - M_{i-1})h_{i-1}}{2}$$

$$S_{i-1}'(x_i) = b_{i-1} + \frac{M_{i-1} h_{i-1}}{2} + \frac{M_i h_{i-1}}{2}$$

At the **left end** of the current segment ($x = x_i$ for $S_i$):

$$S_i'(x_i) = b_i$$

Setting $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)$:

$$b_{i-1} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_{i-1} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_i = b_i$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the closed forms of $b_{i-1}$ and $b_i$.**
Using Step 5:

$$\frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}} - \frac{h_{i-1}}{6}(2 M_{i-1} + M_i) + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_{i-1} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_i = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{h_i}{6}(2 M_i + M_{i+1})$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the left-hand side terms involving $M_{i-1}$ and $M_i$.**

$$\text{LHS } M\text{-terms: } -\frac{h_{i-1}}{6}(2M_{i-1}+M_i) + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_{i-1} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} M_i$$

Coefficient of $M_{i-1}$:

$$-\frac{h_{i-1}}{3} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} = h_{i-1}\left(-\frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{6}\right) = \frac{h_{i-1}}{6}$$

Coefficient of $M_i$:

$$-\frac{h_{i-1}}{6} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{2} = h_{i-1}\left(-\frac{1}{6} + \frac{3}{6}\right) = \frac{h_{i-1}}{3} = \frac{2 h_{i-1}}{6}$$

So the LHS becomes:

$$\frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}} + \frac{h_{i-1}}{6} M_{i-1} + \frac{2 h_{i-1}}{6} M_i$$

**Step 9 — Re-arrange to bring all $M$ terms to the left.**

$$\frac{h_{i-1}}{6} M_{i-1} + \frac{2 h_{i-1}}{6} M_i + \frac{2 h_i}{6} M_i + \frac{h_i}{6} M_{i+1} = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}$$

Multiplying both sides by 6:

$$\boxed{\,h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1} + h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\left[\frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}\right]\,}$$

This is the **master tridiagonal equation** for every interior knot $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$.

**Step 10 — Apply natural-spline boundary conditions.**
The natural condition demands zero curvature at the endpoints:

$$M_0 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad M_n = 0$$

The full system for $n = 4$ data points (3 intervals) is therefore a $(3 \times 3)$ tridiagonal system in unknowns $M_1, M_2, M_3$, with $M_0$ and $M_4$ being known zeros.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Fully Solved)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Construct the **natural cubic spline** through the data points $(0,1), (1,2), (2,0), (3,3)$. Here $n = 3$ intervals and $M_0 = M_3 = 0$.

**Step A — Compute interval widths.** With $h_0 = h_1 = h_2 = 1$.

**Step B — Build the tridiagonal system.**

For $i = 1$:

$$1 \cdot M_0 + 2(1+1) M_1 + 1 \cdot M_2 = 6\left[\frac{0-2}{1} - \frac{2-1}{1}\right] = 6(-2 - 1) = -18$$

Since $M_0 = 0$:

$$4 M_1 + M_2 = -18 \quad \cdots (1)$$

For $i = 2$:

$$1 \cdot M_1 + 2(1+1) M_2 + 1 \cdot M_3 = 6\left[\frac{3-0}{1} - \frac{0-2}{1}\right] = 6(3+2) = 30$$

Since $M_3 = 0$:

$$M_1 + 4 M_2 = 30 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step C — Solve the linear system.**

From (1): $M_2 = -18 - 4 M_1$. Substitute into (2):

$$M_1 + 4(-18 - 4 M_1) = 30$$
$$M_1 - 72 - 16 M_1 = 30$$
$$-15 M_1 = 102$$
$$M_1 = -\frac{102}{15} = -\frac{34}{5} = -6.8$$

Back-substitute:

$$M_2 = -18 - 4\left(-\frac{34}{5}\right) = -18 + \frac{136}{5} = \frac{-90 + 136}{5} = \frac{46}{5} = 9.2$$

So $\,M_0 = 0,\ M_1 = -\tfrac{34}{5},\ M_2 = \tfrac{46}{5},\ M_3 = 0$.

**Step D — Compute the four coefficients for each segment.**

Recall:
- $a_i = y_i$
- $c_i = M_i / 2$
- $d_i = (M_{i+1} - M_i) / (6 h_i)$
- $b_i = (y_{i+1} - y_i)/h_i - h_i (2 M_i + M_{i+1})/6$

**Segment 0 ($0 \le x \le 1$):**

$$a_0 = 1, \quad c_0 = 0, \quad d_0 = \frac{-34/5 - 0}{6} = -\frac{34}{30} = -\frac{17}{15}$$

$$b_0 = \frac{2-1}{1} - \frac{1}{6}\left(0 + \left(-\frac{34}{5}\right)\right) = 1 + \frac{34}{30} = 1 + \frac{17}{15} = \frac{32}{15}$$

$$\boxed{S_0(x) = 1 + \tfrac{32}{15}\,x \;-\; \tfrac{17}{15}\,x^3}$$

**Segment 1 ($1 \le x \le 2$):**

$$a_1 = 2, \quad c_1 = -\frac{34}{10} = -\frac{17}{5}, \quad d_1 = \frac{46/5 - (-34/5)}{6} = \frac{80/5}{6} = \frac{16}{6} = \frac{8}{3}$$

$$b_1 = \frac{0-2}{1} - \frac{1}{6}\left(2\cdot(-\tfrac{34}{5}) + \tfrac{46}{5}\right) = -2 - \frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{-22}{5} = -2 + \frac{22}{30} = -\frac{19}{15}$$

$$\boxed{S_1(x) = 2 \;-\; \tfrac{19}{15}(x-1) \;-\; \tfrac{17}{5}(x-1)^2 \;+\; \tfrac{8}{3}(x-1)^3}$$

**Segment 2 ($2 \le x \le 3$):**

$$a_2 = 0, \quad c_2 = \frac{46/5}{2} = \frac{23}{5}, \quad d_2 = \frac{0 - 46/5}{6} = -\frac{23}{15}$$

$$b_2 = \frac{3-0}{1} - \frac{1}{6}\left(2\cdot\tfrac{46}{5} + 0\right) = 3 - \frac{92}{30} = 3 - \frac{46}{15} = -\frac{1}{15}$$

$$\boxed{S_2(x) = -\tfrac{1}{15}(x-2) \;+\; \tfrac{23}{5}(x-2)^2 \;-\; \tfrac{23}{15}(x-2)^3}$$

**Step E — Verification at the interior knots.**

At $x = 1$ (end of $S_0$):

$$S_0(1) = 1 + \tfrac{32}{15} - \tfrac{17}{15} = 1 + \tfrac{15}{15} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

At $x = 1$ (start of $S_1$): $S_1(1) = 2 \quad \checkmark$

At $x = 2$ (end of $S_1$):

$$S_1(2) = 2 - \tfrac{19}{15} - \tfrac{17}{5} + \tfrac{8}{3} = \tfrac{30 - 19 - 51 + 40}{15} = \tfrac{0}{15} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

At $x = 3$ (end of $S_2$):

$$S_2(3) = -\tfrac{1}{15} + \tfrac{23}{5} - \tfrac{23}{15} = \tfrac{-1 + 69 - 23}{15} = \tfrac{45}{15} = 3 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** The $4n-2$ constraint system is *deterministic* — once the boundary conditions are chosen, the spline is unique. Always validate by checking that the spline reproduces all the input $y_i$ values exactly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Cubic-Spline Solver

The following block diagram maps the *data flow* of a typical KTU-style cubic spline construction — the steps a student would follow on the answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT DATA POINTS x0 y0 ... xn yn] --> B[COMPUTE INTERVAL WIDTHS hi = xi PLUS 1 MINUS xi]
    B --> C[SET BOUNDARY MOMENTS M0 EQUALS 0 AND Mn EQUALS 0 NATURAL SPLINE]
    C --> D[BUILD TRIDIAGONAL SYSTEM FOR i EQUALS 1 TO n MINUS 1]
    D --> E[SOLVE LINEAR SYSTEM FOR MOMENTS M1 M2 ... Mn MINUS 1]
    E --> F[COMPUTE COEFFICIENTS ai bi ci di FOR EACH SEGMENT]
    F --> G[ASSEMBLE FINAL SPLINE EQUATIONS Si OF x]
    G --> H[VERIFY INTERPOLATION AT ALL KNOTS]
    H --> I[OUTPUT SPLINE PIECEWISE POLYNOMIAL]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Piecewise Cubic Spline Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[KNOT 0 x0 y0] -->|SEGMENT S0 CUBIC ARC| P1[KNOT 1 x1 y1]
    P1 -->|C1 AND C2 CONTINUITY| P1CHK{JOIN AT KNOT 1}
    P1CHK -->|SEGMENT S1 CUBIC ARC| P2[KNOT 2 x2 y2]
    P2 -->|C1 AND C2 CONTINUITY| P2CHK{JOIN AT KNOT 2}
    P2CHK -->|SEGMENT S2 CUBIC ARC| P3[KNOT 3 x3 y3]
    P1CHK -. S PRIME x1 EQUALS S PRIME x1 .-> P1CHK
    P2CHK -. S DOUBLE PRIME x2 EQUALS S DOUBLE PRIME x2 .-> P2CHK
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Decision Tree (Choosing Spline Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NEW SPLINE PROBLEM] --> Q1{ARE ENDPOINT DERIVATIVES KNOWN}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{IS THE CURVE CLOSED}
    Q1 -->|NO| NAT[USE NATURAL SPLINE M0 EQUALS Mn EQUALS 0]
    Q2 -->|YES| PER[USE PERIODIC SPLINE MATCHING DERIVATIVES]
    Q2 -->|NO| CLAMP[USE CLAMPED SPLINE SPECIFY S PRIME AT ENDS]
    NAT --> OUT[CONSTRUCT TRIDIAGONAL SYSTEM]
    CLAMP --> OUT
    PER --> OUT
    OUT --> SOLVE[SOLVE FOR MOMENTS MI]
    SOLVE --> DONE[SPLINE READY]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level View of Constraint Sources

| Block | Source of Equations | Count | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interpolation $S_i(x_i) = y_i$ | Each segment must hit left endpoint | $n$ | Fixed |
| Interpolation $S_i(x_{i+1}) = y_{i+1}$ | Each segment must hit right endpoint | $n$ | Fixed |
| First derivative continuity $S'_{i}(x_{i+1}) = S'_{i+1}(x_{i+1})$ | No kinks at interior knots | $n-1$ | Fixed |
| Second derivative continuity $S''_{i}(x_{i+1}) = S''_{i+1}(x_{i+1})$ | Smooth curvature transitions | $n-1$ | Fixed |
| Natural boundary $M_0 = M_n = 0$ | User / problem statement | $2$ | **Variable** |
| **Total** | | $4n$ | Solved |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

> Define a **natural cubic spline**. State the four conditions that every cubic spline must satisfy at an interior knot.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **natural cubic spline** is a piecewise cubic interpolant $S(x)$ through the data points $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$, such that the second derivative vanishes at the endpoints: $S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0$.

The four conditions that must hold at every **interior knot** $x_i$ are:

1. $S_{i-1}(x_i) = S_i(x_i) = y_i$ — **Interpolation**
2. $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)$ — **Continuity of first derivative** (no corner)
3. $S_{i-1}''(x_i) = S_i''(x_i)$ — **Continuity of second derivative** (smooth curvature)
4. Each $S_i(x)$ is a cubic polynomial on $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$

(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for listing and explaining the four conditions.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

> Differentiate between a **natural cubic spline** and a **clamped cubic spline**. When is the clamped form preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Natural Cubic Spline | Clamped Cubic Spline |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary condition | $S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0$ | $S'(x_0) = f'(x_0),\ S'(x_n) = f'(x_n)$ |
| Information used | Only function values | Function values **and** endpoint slopes |
| Resulting system | Tridiagonal, $M_0 = M_n = 0$ | Tridiagonal with first/last rows modified to include $f'(x_0), f'(x_n)$ |
| Accuracy | Good for "free" ends | **More accurate** when end-slopes are known |

**When preferred:** The clamped form is preferred whenever the physical slope of the underlying function is known at the endpoints (e.g., a beam with prescribed end-rotation, or a velocity profile with known initial/final velocity). It eliminates the spurious curvature "kicks" at the ends of a natural spline.

(1 mark for table comparison; 1 mark for clamped advantage; 1 mark for practical example.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** With the necessary derivation, obtain the tridiagonal system of equations that the **second-derivative moments** $M_i$ of a natural cubic spline must satisfy. State clearly the boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Given the data points $(0, 1),\ (1, 2),\ (2, 0),\ (3, 3)$, construct the **natural cubic spline** $S(x)$. Evaluate $S(0.5)$ and $S(2.5)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the segment polynomial [1 mark]:**

$$S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x-x_i) + c_i(x-x_i)^2 + d_i(x-x_i)^3, \quad x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]$$

**Step 2 — Derive the closed-form coefficients [3 marks]:**

Using interpolation at $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$ and second-derivative continuity, define $M_i = S_i''(x_i)$:

$$a_i = y_i, \quad c_i = \frac{M_i}{2}, \quad d_i = \frac{M_{i+1} - M_i}{6 h_i}$$

$$b_i = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{h_i}{6}(2 M_i + M_{i+1})$$

**Step 3 — Apply $C^1$ continuity and simplify [2 marks]:**

Imposing $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$ and simplifying:

$$h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1} + h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\left[\frac{y_{i+1}-y_i}{h_i} - \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}\right]$$

**Step 4 — State the natural boundary conditions [1 mark]:**

$$M_0 = 0, \quad M_n = 0$$

This yields a symmetric tridiagonal system of $(n-1)$ equations in the unknowns $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_{n-1}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the tridiagonal system [2 marks]:**

With $h_0 = h_1 = h_2 = 1$ and $M_0 = M_3 = 0$:

$$4 M_1 + M_2 = -18 \quad \cdots (1)$$
$$M_1 + 4 M_2 = 30 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the moments [1 mark]:**

$$M_1 = -\frac{34}{5} = -6.8, \quad M_2 = \frac{46}{5} = 9.2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the spline coefficients [2 marks]:**

| Segment | $a_i$ | $b_i$ | $c_i$ | $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $1$ | $32/15$ | $0$ | $-17/15$ |
| 1 | $2$ | $-19/15$ | $-17/5$ | $8/3$ |
| 2 | $0$ | $-1/15$ | $23/5$ | $-23/15$ |

**Step 4 — Write the final spline and evaluate [2 marks]:**

$$S(x) = \begin{cases} 1 + \tfrac{32}{15}x - \tfrac{17}{15}x^3, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\[6pt] 2 - \tfrac{19}{15}(x-1) - \tfrac{17}{5}(x-1)^2 + \tfrac{8}{3}(x-1)^3, & 1 \le x \le 2 \\[6pt] -\tfrac{1}{15}(x-2) + \tfrac{23}{5}(x-2)^2 - \tfrac{23}{15}(x-2)^3, & 2 \le x \le 3 \end{cases}$$

**Evaluation at $x = 0.5$ (in segment 0):**

$$S(0.5) = 1 + \tfrac{32}{15}(0.5) - \tfrac{17}{15}(0.125) = 1 + \tfrac{16}{15} - \tfrac{17}{120} = 1 + 0.9250 = 1.9250$$

$$\boxed{S(0.5) \approx 1.9250}$$

**Evaluation at $x = 2.5$ (in segment 2):**

$$S(2.5) = -\tfrac{1}{15}(0.5) + \tfrac{23}{5}(0.25) - \tfrac{23}{15}(0.125) = -\tfrac{1}{30} + \tfrac{23}{20} - \tfrac{23}{120}$$

$$= -\tfrac{4}{120} + \tfrac{138}{120} - \tfrac{23}{120} = \tfrac{111}{120} = 0.925$$

$$\boxed{S(2.5) \approx 0.9250}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **Q. (a)** State the conditions for a natural cubic spline. How many equations are required to determine the spline? Justify. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Fit a natural cubic spline to the data $(1, 2),\ (2, 3),\ (3, 5),\ (4, 1)$ and estimate the value of $y$ at $x = 2.5$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition [2 marks]:**
A natural cubic spline $S(x)$ is a piecewise cubic polynomial such that:
- $S(x_i) = y_i$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$ (interpolation)
- $S, S', S''$ are continuous on $[x_0, x_n]$
- $S''(x_0) = S''(x_n) = 0$ (natural boundary)

**Step 2 — Counting [3 marks]:**
- Number of segments = $n$
- Unknowns per segment = 4 → total unknowns = $4n$
- Interpolation gives $2n$ equations
- Continuity of $S'$ at $n-1$ interior knots → $n-1$ equations
- Continuity of $S''$ at $n-1$ interior knots → $n-1$ equations
- Total from above: $2n + 2(n-1) = 4n - 2$
- Deficit: 2 equations → supplied by natural conditions $M_0 = 0$ and $M_n = 0$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion [2 marks]:**
Hence a natural cubic spline has $4n$ unknowns and exactly $4n$ equations, ensuring a unique solution.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Interval widths and boundary moments [1 mark]:**
$h_0 = h_1 = h_2 = 1$, $M_0 = M_3 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Tridiagonal system [2 marks]:**

For $i = 1$:

$$1 \cdot 0 + 2(2) M_1 + 1 \cdot M_2 = 6\left[\frac{5-3}{1} - \frac{3-2}{1}\right] = 6(2 - 1) = 6$$
$$4 M_1 + M_2 = 6 \quad \cdots (1)$$

For $i = 2$:

$$1 \cdot M_1 + 2(2) M_2 + 1 \cdot 0 = 6\left[\frac{1-5}{1} - \frac{5-3}{1}\right] = 6(-4 - 2) = -36$$
$$M_1 + 4 M_2 = -36 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3 — Solve [1 mark]:**
From (1): $M_2 = 6 - 4 M_1$. Substituting:

$$M_1 + 4(6 - 4 M_1) = -36 \implies -15 M_1 = -60 \implies M_1 = 4$$

$$M_2 = 6 - 16 = -10$$

**Step 4 — Compute segment 1 coefficients (we need $x = 2.5$ in segment 1) [2 marks]:**

$a_1 = 3,\ c_1 = M_1/2 = 2,\ d_1 = (M_2 - M_1)/6 = (-10 - 4)/6 = -7/3$

$$b_1 = \frac{5-3}{1} - \frac{1}{6}(2\cdot 4 + (-10)) = 2 - \frac{1}{6}(-2) = 2 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{7}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $S(2.5)$ [1 mark]:**

$$S(2.5) = S_1(2.5) = 3 + \tfrac{7}{3}(1.5) + 2(1.5)^2 + (-\tfrac{7}{3})(1.5)^3$$

$$= 3 + 3.5 + 4.5 - 7.875 = 3.125$$

$$\boxed{S(2.5) \approx 3.125}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary conditions.** Many students derive the tridiagonal row correctly but forget to state $M_0 = 0, M_n = 0$. This alone costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Arithmetic slip in the $6[\cdot]$ right-hand side.** The bracket contains TWO differences — students often compute only one. Re-derive: $6\bigl[(y_{i+1}-y_i)/h_i - (y_i - y_{i-1})/h_{i-1}\bigr]$.
> 3. **Mixing up $c_i$ and $M_i$.** Remember $c_i = M_i/2$, NOT $M_i$. Using $c_i = M_i$ will cascade errors into all four coefficients.
> 4. **Not verifying the spline.** Always plug $x = x_i$ back into the final $S_i$ to confirm it returns $y_i$. Examiners reward the verification step.
> 5. **Confusing natural with clamped.** If the problem says "clamped", the first and last rows of the system must be modified to incorporate the known slopes $f'(x_0)$ and $f'(x_n)$. Submitting a natural-spline system on a clamped problem is an automatic 0 for the boundary part.
> 6. **Sign errors in $d_i$.** It is $d_i = (M_{i+1} - M_i)/(6 h_i)$, NOT the reverse. A sign slip here flips the cubic's curvature sign.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Cubic Splines — KTU PEMET523 Module 3):**
> - A cubic spline is a **piecewise cubic** interpolant with **$C^2$ continuity** at the knots.
> - Each interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$ has a cubic with **4 unknowns** ($a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i$) → total $4n$ unknowns.
> - The tridiagonal system in **second-derivative moments** $M_i$ is the heart of the algorithm.
> - **Natural spline:** $M_0 = M_n = 0$ (most common in KTU papers).
> - **Clamped spline:** $S'(x_0)$ and $S'(x_n)$ are prescribed.
> - Master formula: $\,h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1}+h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\bigl[(y_{i+1}-y_i)/h_i - (y_i-y_{i-1})/h_{i-1}\bigr]\,$.
> - After solving for $M_i$, recover $a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i$ using the four closed-form expressions.
> - Always **verify** by substituting $x_i$ into $S_i(x)$ and confirming $S_i(x_i) = y_i$.
> - Cubic splines are **$C^2$** but generally **NOT $C^3$** — the third derivative has jumps at the knots.
> - Splines avoid the **Runge oscillation** of high-degree polynomial interpolation.
> - The resulting tridiagonal system is **symmetric and positive definite** → solve with Thomas algorithm or Cholesky for $n > 100$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET523 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
